topics.title: Difference Between Microprocessor and Microcontroller Architecture, 8051 Pin Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Difference Between Microprocessor and Microcontroller Architecture, 8051 Pin Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Microprocessor — The Formal Definition

A **Microprocessor (µP)** is a single-chip, general-purpose, programmable VLSI device that contains only the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — comprising the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, **Register Array**, and **Control Unit** — on a single silicon die. It is fundamentally a *CPU-only* integrated circuit and therefore depends on **external peripherals** such as program memory (ROM/EPROM), data memory (RAM), parallel I/O ports, timers, serial communication units (UART), and interrupt controllers, all connected through **external address, data, and control buses**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A microprocessor is a register-based, single-chip CPU that interprets and executes instructions fetched from external memory. Examples: Intel 8085, Intel 8086, Motorola 6800.

### 1.2 Microcontroller — The Formal Definition

A **Microcontroller (µC)** is a single-chip VLSI device that integrates a **CPU**, **program memory (ROM/Flash)**, **data memory (RAM)**, **I/O ports**, **timers/counters**, a **serial port (UART)**, an **interrupt controller**, and often an **ADC** and **PWM** unit, all on the *same die*. Because all essential computing resources are embedded within a single chip, it is often described as a **"System-on-Chip (SoC)"** or **"Computer-on-a-Chip"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A microcontroller is a microprocessor that has been *integrated* with on-chip memory and peripheral devices, optimised for embedded control applications. Examples: Intel 8051, Microchip PIC, Atmel ATmega328 (Arduino Uno).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the difference between a **generic power tool factory** and a **specialised electric toothbrush**:

| Component | Microprocessor Analogy | Microcontroller Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU** | The factory's central robotic arm | The smart motor in the toothbrush |
| **Memory** | A separate warehouse across the road | The motor's *built-in* battery compartment |
| **Peripherals (I/O, Timer, UART)** | External add-on conveyor belts & sensors | Already *soldered onto the PCB* of the toothbrush |
| **Bus system** | Long external highways connecting them | Short internal wiring on one board |
| **Cost & Power** | Heavy, expensive, power-hungry | Tiny, cheap, ultra-low-power |
| **Use-case** | Versatile (PC, server, laptop) | Dedicated (washing machine, ECU, IoT) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Summary:** A **microprocessor** is a *brain without a body*, while a **microcontroller** is a *brain with a body, senses, and reflexes* — all on one chip. The microcontroller is *purpose-built* for one job; the microprocessor is *general-purpose* for many jobs.

### 1.4 8051 Microcontroller — Introduction

The **Intel 8051** is an **8-bit**, **Harvard-architecture**, **CISC** microcontroller introduced by Intel in 1980. It is the *de-facto* reference MCU in KTU curricula because it is the canonical example of a SoC that integrates:

- An **8-bit ALU**.
- **4 KB of on-chip ROM** (program memory).
- **128 bytes of on-chip RAM** (data memory).
- **Four 8-bit I/O ports** (32 I/O lines).
- **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (T0, T1).
- A **full-duplex UART** (serial port).
- **Five interrupt sources** with two priority levels.
- A **64 KB external code & 64 KB external data** memory expansion capability.

The 8051 is packaged in a **40-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)** and operates on a single **+5 V** supply with a typical crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz** (chosen to generate standard baud rates like 9600 exactly).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Philosophy — Von Neumann vs Harvard

The most important architectural distinction is the **memory organisation**:

- **Von Neumann (Princeton) Architecture — used by Microprocessors:** A *single shared bus* carries both **instructions** and **data**. The CPU fetches an instruction **OR** data in any given bus cycle (mutually exclusive). This is a **bottleneck** called the *Von Neumann bottleneck*.

- **Harvard Architecture — used by 8051 / Microcontrollers:** *Physically separate* program-memory bus and data-memory bus exist, allowing **simultaneous** instruction fetch and data access in a single machine cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The 8051 has a **modified Harvard** architecture — it has separate internal buses for program and data memory, but the external bus is multiplexed, allowing code memory to be expanded externally while data RAM remains internal.

### 2.2 Architectural Breakdown — Microprocessor

A microcomputer system built around a microprocessor (e.g., 8085) requires these **discrete external chips**:

1. **CPU chip (8085):** Contains ALU, registers, instruction decoder.
2. **Program Memory (ROM/EPROM, e.g., 2732):** Stores the firmware.
3. **Data Memory (RAM, e.g., 6116):** Stores run-time variables.
4. **I/O Port chips (e.g., 8255 PPI):** Parallel I/O.
5. **Timer chip (e.g., 8253):** For delays/event counting.
6. **UART (e.g., 8251):** Serial communication.
7. **Interrupt Controller (e.g., 8259):** Manages multiple interrupts.
8. **Address Latch (e.g., 74LS373):** Demultiplexes A0–A7 from AD0–AD7.
9. **Clock Generator (e.g., 8284):** Provides CLK & RESET.
10. **Bus Drivers/Buffers (74LS244/245):** For current driving capability.

The CPU communicates with these via the **System Bus**:
- **Address Bus (16-bit, unidirectional):** Carries memory/I/O addresses.
- **Data Bus (8-bit, bidirectional):** Carries the actual data.
- **Control Bus:** Signals like RD, WR, IO/M, ALE, etc.

### 2.3 Architectural Breakdown — Microcontroller (8051)

In the 8051, the *same* peripheral functions exist, but they are **on-chip**:

- **CPU** → 8-bit ALU, A register, B register, PSW, SP, PC, DPTR.
- **Program Memory** → 4 KB ROM (internal); expandable to 64 KB external.
- **Data Memory** → 128 bytes RAM (internal); expandable to 64 KB external.
- **I/O** → P0, P1, P2, P3 (32 lines).
- **Timers** → T0, T1 (16-bit each).
- **UART** → SBUF, SCON, PCON (baud rate generator).
- **Interrupts** → 5 sources: INT0, INT1, T0, T1, Serial Port.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table

| Parameter | Microprocessor (e.g., 8085) | Microcontroller (e.g., 8051) |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture Type** | Von Neumann | Harvard / Modified Harvard |
| **Bus** | External Address, Data, Control bus | Internal bus; multiplexed external bus |
| **Memory** | External ROM + External RAM | Internal ROM + Internal RAM (+ optional external) |
| **Peripherals** | Must be connected externally | Integrated on-chip |
| **I/O Ports** | Need 8255 or similar | 4 built-in ports (P0–P3) |
| **Timers** | Need external 8253/8254 | 2 built-in 16-bit timers |
| **Serial Port** | Need 8251 UART | Built-in UART |
| **Interrupt Controller** | External 8259 required | Integrated, 5 sources |
| **Instruction Set** | Rich & complex (108 in 8085) | Rich for control apps (111 in 8051) |
| **Data Width** | 8/16/32/64-bit (varies) | Typically 8-bit |
| **Cost** | Higher (system-level) | Lower (single chip) |
| **Power Consumption** | High (watts) | Low (mW) |
| **Clock Speed** | Up to GHz range | Typically < 50 MHz |
| **Applications** | PC, Laptop, Server, Workstation | Washing machine, ECU, IoT, Toys |
| **Design Goal** | High computational throughput | Dedicated real-time control |
| **PCB Complexity** | Large, multi-chip board | Compact, single-chip board |
| **Bit Manipulation** | Limited | Powerful (bit-addressable RAM) |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{osc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{osc}}$ | Oscillator period | 8051 timing base |
| 2 | $T_{machine} = 12 \times T_{osc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | 1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods | Instruction timing |
| 3 | $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ | Standard crystal | Generates exact 9600 baud |
| 4 | $T_{machine} = \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085\,\mu s$ | Machine cycle time | Time per instruction fetch |
| 5 | $Baud_{Timer1\,Overflow} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | Timer-1 mode 2 baud | Serial comm. with Timer-1 |
| 6 | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}$ | TH1 reload value | For 9600 baud: TH1 = 0xFD (-3) |
| 7 | $T_{delay} = (65536 - X) \times T_{machine}$ | Timer delay formula | Mode 1 (16-bit) delay |
| 8 | $N_{cycles} = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{machine}}$ | Instruction cycles count | Loop-based delays |
| 9 | $Memory_{code} = 2^{PC_{width}} = 2^{16} = 64\,KB$ | Code memory size | Maximum external ROM |
| 10 | $Memory_{xdata} = 2^{16} = 64\,KB$ | External data memory | Maximum external RAM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "12 clocks per machine cycle":** Almost every 8051 timing problem reduces to this. Memorise: *T_machine = 12 / f_osc*. For 12 MHz crystal → 1 µs. For 11.0592 MHz → 1.085 µs.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The microprocessor-vs-microcontroller choice appears everywhere in modern industry:

- **Automotive ECU (Engine Control Unit):** Uses an **Infineon AURIX** (multi-core 32-bit microcontroller) for real-time sensor fusion and actuator control — chosen for *determinism* and *safety* (ISO 26262).
- **Smartphone SoC:** Uses an **ARM Cortex-A** microprocessor for the application processor, *plus* multiple **Cortex-M microcontrollers** as sensor-hub and power-management units.
- **IoT edge nodes (ESP32, nRF52):** Use microcontrollers for ultra-low-power sleep currents in the **µA** range, running for years on coin cells.
- **Industrial PLCs:** A single PLC may integrate a high-end microprocessor for HMI/communication, with multiple microcontrollers for I/O scanning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 8051 Machine Cycle from Crystal Frequency

**Given:** A standard 8051 system uses a quartz crystal of frequency $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$.

**Step 1 — Oscillator Period.** The crystal produces a square wave whose time period is the reciprocal of its frequency.

$$
T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^6}\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to microseconds.**

$$
T_{osc} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 0.0904\,\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 12-clock-per-machine-cycle rule.** Internally, the 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12, so one machine cycle takes 12 oscillator periods.

$$
T_{machine} = 12 \times T_{osc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$
T_{machine} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{machine} = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.** Every *single-cycle* 8051 instruction (e.g., `NOP`, `INC A`, `MOV A,Rn`) takes **exactly 1.085 µs**. A two-cycle instruction takes 2.17 µs, and so on.

$$
\text{Instruction Time} = N_{cycles} \times 1.085\,\mu s
$$

where $N_{cycles}$ is the number of machine cycles the instruction requires (1, 2, or 4 typically).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Timer-1 Reload Value for Standard Baud Rate

**Problem:** Configure the 8051 serial port at **9600 baud**, **8-bit data, no parity**, using Timer-1 in **Mode 2 (auto-reload)** with the serial port in **Mode 1**.

**Step 1 — Identify the baud-rate formula for Timer-1 in Mode 2:**

$$
Baud = \frac{f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

The factor **32 × 12 = 384** appears because the serial port divides the oscillator by 32, and the timer divides by 12 internally.

**Step 2 — Solve for (256 − TH1):**

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ and $Baud = 9600$:**

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
384 \times 9600 = 3{,}686{,}400
$$

**Step 5 — Perform the division:**

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} = 3
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for TH1:**

$$
TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD
$$

**Step 7 — Verification by back-substitution:**

$$
Baud = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 3} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{1152} = 9600 \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz and not 12 MHz?** With a 12 MHz crystal, the formula gives $(256 - TH1) = 11059200 / (384 \times 9600) = 3.0$? Actually it gives an exact integer *only* for **11.0592 MHz** when baud rates like 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200 are used. With 12 MHz, you get 2.604, a non-integer → timing error → garbled serial data. **This is THE most-asked KTU numerical.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Delay Routine Timing

**Problem:** Generate a delay of **50 ms** using Timer-0, **Mode 1 (16-bit)**, $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$.

**Step 1 — Compute machine cycle:**

$$
T_{machine} = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Required timer counts for 50 ms:**

$$
N = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{machine}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46{,}083
$$

**Step 3 — Timer initial value (16-bit timer overflows at 65,536):**

$$
X = 65536 - N = 65536 - 46083 = 19{,}453
$$

**Step 4 — Split into high and low bytes for TH0 and TL0:**

$$
X_{decimal} = 19{,}453 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_{hex} = 0x4BFD
$$

$$
TH0 = 0x4B, \quad TL0 = 0xFD
$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$
T_{actual} = (65536 - 19453) \times 1.085\,\mu s = 46083 \times 1.085 \approx 50.0\,ms \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: 8051 Pin-to-Function Mapping (Pin 1–40)

| Pin # | Name | Type | Alternate Function | Reset Value | Pull-up |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1.0 | I/O | T2 (Timer-2 in 8052) | 1 | Internal weak |
| 2 | P1.1 | I/O | T2EX (Timer-2 trigger) | 1 | Internal weak |
| 3 | P1.2 | I/O | — | 1 | Internal weak |
| 4 | P1.3 | I/O | — | 1 | Internal weak |
| 5 | P1.4 | I/O | — | 1 | Internal weak |
| 6 | P1.5 | I/O | — | 1 | Internal weak |
| 7 | P1.6 | I/O | — | 1 | Internal weak |
| 8 | P1.7 | I/O | — | 1 | Internal weak |
| 9 | **RST** | Input | Power-on reset (≥ 2 machine cycles high) | — | None |
| 10 | P3.0 | I/O | **RxD** (Serial receive) | 1 | Internal weak |
| 11 | P3.1 | I/O | **TxD** (Serial transmit) | 1 | Internal weak |
| 12 | P3.2 | I/O | **INT0** (External interrupt 0) | 1 | Internal weak |
| 13 | P3.3 | I/O | **INT1** (External interrupt 1) | 1 | Internal weak |
| 14 | P3.4 | I/O | **T0** (Timer-0 external input) | 1 | Internal weak |
| 15 | P3.5 | I/O | **T1** (Timer-1 external input) | 1 | Internal weak |
| 16 | P3.6 | I/O | **WR** (External data memory write strobe) | 1 | Internal weak |
| 17 | P3.7 | I/O | **RD** (External data memory read strobe) | 1 | Internal weak |
| 18 | **XTAL2** | Output | Crystal pin 2 | — | — |
| 19 | **XTAL1** | Input | Crystal pin 1 / external clock in | — | — |
| 20 | **GND** | Power | Ground (0 V) | — | — |
| 21 | P2.0 | I/O | A8 (External memory high address byte) | 1 | Internal weak |
| 22 | P2.1 | I/O | A9 | 1 | Internal weak |
| 23 | P2.2 | I/O | A10 | 1 | Internal weak |
| 24 | P2.3 | I/O | A11 | 1 | Internal weak |
| 25 | P2.4 | I/O | A12 | 1 | Internal weak |
| 26 | P2.5 | I/O | A13 | 1 | Internal weak |
| 27 | P2.6 | I/O | A14 | 1 | Internal weak |
| 28 | P2.7 | I/O | A15 | 1 | Internal weak |
| 29 | **PSEN** | Output | Program Store Enable (read from external ROM) | — | — |
| 30 | **ALE / PROG** | Output | Address Latch Enable / Flash programming pulse | — | — |
| 31 | **EA / VPP** | Input | External Access (low ⇒ use external ROM) | — | — |
| 32 | P0.7 | I/O | AD7 (multiplexed address/data) | 1 (Hi-Z) | **NO** internal pull-up |
| 33 | P0.6 | I/O | AD6 | 1 (Hi-Z) | **NO** internal pull-up |
| 34 | P0.5 | I/O | AD5 | 1 (Hi-Z) | **NO** internal pull-up |
| 35 | P0.4 | I/O | AD4 | 1 (Hi-Z) | **NO** internal pull-up |
| 36 | P0.3 | I/O | AD3 | 1 (Hi-Z) | **NO** internal pull-up |
| 37 | P0.2 | I/O | AD2 | 1 (Hi-Z) | **NO** internal pull-up |
| 38 | P0.1 | I/O | AD1 | 1 (Hi-Z) | **NO** internal pull-up |
| 39 | P0.0 | I/O | AD0 | 1 (Hi-Z) | **NO** internal pull-up |
| 40 | **VCC** | Power | +5 V supply | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often forget that **Port 0 has NO internal pull-up resistor** (open-drain). To use P0 as a regular output port, *external* 10 kΩ pull-ups must be wired. Ports 1, 2, 3 *do* have internal pull-ups.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: 8051 Power-On Reset Circuit

**Step 1 — Condition for valid reset:** RST pin (pin 9) must be held **HIGH for at least 2 machine cycles** (≥ 24 oscillator periods) during power-up.

**Step 2 — Standard RC reset network design:**

$$
V_{RST}(t) = V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t / RC}
$$

The reset is *released* when $V_{RST}$ falls below the logic-HIGH threshold (typically 0.7 × VCC ≈ 3.5 V for the 8051).

**Step 3 — Choose R = 10 kΩ, C = 10 µF:**

$$
\tau = RC = 10^4 \times 10^{-5} = 0.1\,s = 100\,ms
$$

**Step 4 — Verify reset pulse width is long enough:**

$$
t_{RST\,high} \approx \tau \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V_{CC}}{0.7 V_{CC}}\right) = 0.1 \times 0.357 \approx 35.7\,ms
$$

Since 35.7 ms ≫ 2 machine cycles (≈ 2.17 µs at 11.0592 MHz), the reset is **reliable** ✓.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Microprocessor-Based System (e.g., 8085)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU1["CPU 8085\nALU + Registers + CU"]
    ROM1["ROM\n2732 EPROM\nProgram Memory"]
    RAM1["RAM\n6116 Static RAM\nData Memory"]
    PPI1["PPI 8255\nParallel I/O"]
    TIM1["Timer 8253\n3 x 16-bit"]
    UART1["UART 8251\nSerial Port"]
    INTC1["PIC 8259\nInterrupt Controller"]
    LATCH1["Latch 74LS373\nAddress Demux"]
    CLK1["Clock 8284\nCLK + RESET"]
    BUS_A["Address Bus\n16-bit"]
    BUS_D["Data Bus\n8-bit"]
    BUS_C["Control Bus\nRD WR IO/M ALE"]

    CPU1 -- BUS_A --> BUS_A
    CPU1 -- BUS_D --> BUS_D
    CPU1 -- BUS_C --> BUS_C
    BUS_A --> LATCH1
    BUS_A --> ROM1
    BUS_A --> RAM1
    BUS_A --> PPI1
    BUS_A --> TIM1
    BUS_A --> UART1
    BUS_A --> INTC1
    CLK1 --> CPU1
    BUS_D --> ROM1
    BUS_D --> RAM1
    BUS_D --> PPI1
    BUS_D --> TIM1
    BUS_D --> UART1
    PPI1 -- I/O --> DEV1["External\nDevices"]
    UART1 -- Serial --> TERM1["Terminal /\nModem"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Microcontroller 8051 (Single-Chip SoC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MC["8051 MICROCONTROLLER - SINGLE CHIP"]
        CPU2["8-bit CPU\nALU + A + B + PSW\n+ SP + PC + DPTR"]
        ROM2["4 KB\nOn-chip ROM\nProgram Memory"]
        RAM2["128 B\nOn-chip RAM\nData Memory"]
        SFR["SFR Block\nPCON SCON TCON\nTMOD TCON IE IP"]
        P0["Port 0\nP0.0 - P0.7\n8-bit"]
        P1["Port 1\nP1.0 - P1.7\n8-bit"]
        P2["Port 2\nP2.0 - P2.7\n8-bit"]
        P3["Port 3\nP3.0 - P3.7\n8-bit"]
        TMR["Timers\nT0 + T1\n16-bit each"]
        UART2["UART\nSBUF + SCON\nFull Duplex"]
        INTC2["Interrupt\nController\n5 sources"]
        OSC["On-chip\nOscillator\nDriver"]
    end

    CPU2 --- ROM2
    CPU2 --- RAM2
    CPU2 --- SFR
    CPU2 --- TMR
    CPU2 --- UART2
    CPU2 --- INTC2
    CPU2 --- P0
    CPU2 --- P1
    CPU2 --- P2
    CPU2 --- P3
    CPU2 --- OSC
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — 8051 Memory Architecture (Harvard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P["PROGRAM MEMORY"]
        P_INT["Internal ROM\n4 KB\n0000H - 0FFFH"]
        P_EXT["External ROM\nup to 64 KB\n1000H - FFFFH"]
    end
    subgraph D["DATA MEMORY"]
        D_SFR["SFR\n80H - FFH\n21 Registers"]
        D_BIT["Bit Addressable\n20H - 2FH\n16 Bytes = 128 bits"]
        D_GEN["General Purpose\nRAM\n30H - 7FH\n80 Bytes"]
        D_LB["Lower 128\n00H - 7FH"]
        D_HB["Upper 128\n80H - FFH"]
    end
    PC["Program Counter\n16-bit"] --> P
    DPTR["DPTR\n16-bit"] --> D
```

### 4.5 Mermaid — Power-On Reset & Oscillator Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC5["+5V VCC"]
    R_RST["R = 10 kΩ"]
    C_RST["C = 10 µF"]
    RST_PIN["Pin 9 RST"]
    GND_R["GND"]
    XTAL["Crystal\n11.0592 MHz"]
    C1["C1 = 33 pF"]
    C2["C2 = 33 pF"]
    XTAL1["Pin 19 XTAL1"]
    XTAL2["Pin 18 XTAL2"]

    VCC5 --> R_RST --> RST_PIN
    RST_PIN --> C_RST --> GND_R
    XTAL -- between --> XTAL1
    XTAL -- between --> XTAL2
    XTAL1 --> C1 --> GND_R
    XTAL2 --> C2 --> GND_R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU:** Note the inversion of pins 18 & 19 in some textbooks — the crystal is *between* XTAL1 and XTAL2, and each pin has a 30–33 pF capacitor to ground. This forms a **Pierce oscillator**. The RST pin has a *pull-up* resistor to VCC and a *capacitor* to ground, forming an RC time constant that holds RST high for ~100 ms after power-on.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Distinguish between a microprocessor and a microcontroller. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **microprocessor** is a single-chip CPU that contains only the ALU, registers, and control unit; it requires *external* memory and peripherals to function. Examples: **Intel 8085, Intel 8086**.

A **microcontroller** is a single-chip computer that integrates the CPU *along with* on-chip program memory (ROM), data memory (RAM), I/O ports, timers, and a serial port. Examples: **Intel 8051, Microchip PIC16F877**.

Key differences: (1) Peripherals — external vs integrated, (2) Cost & Power — high vs low, (3) Applications — general-purpose computing vs dedicated embedded control, (4) Architecture — Von Neumann vs Harvard, (5) External bus width — wider vs narrower, (6) Instruction set — complex vs control-oriented.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for each correct distinction + 1 mark for examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*List any six features of the Intel 8051 microcontroller.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **8-bit CPU** with an 8-bit ALU.
2. **4 KB of on-chip ROM** (program memory), expandable to 64 KB externally.
3. **128 bytes of on-chip RAM** (data memory), expandable to 64 KB externally.
4. **Four 8-bit I/O ports** — P0, P1, P2, P3 (32 I/O lines).
5. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** — T0 and T1.
6. **Full-duplex UART** for serial communication.
7. **Five interrupt sources** with two priority levels.
8. **Harvard (modified) architecture** with separate program and data buses.
9. **Single +5 V power supply**.
10. **111 instructions** with multiple addressing modes.

*(3 marks: 0.5 mark each for any 6 features)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Draw the pin diagram of the Intel 8051 microcontroller. Explain the function of signals **PSEN, ALE, EA, RST, XTAL1, and XTAL2** in detail. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Harvard architecture** of the 8051 with a block diagram. How is it different from the Von Neumann architecture used in microprocessors? **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) 8051 Pin Diagram & Signal Functions [7 marks]

**Pin Diagram:** *(Refer to Mermaid block in Section 4.3 for the complete 40-pin layout. For an exam answer, the student must draw a rectangle with 20 pins on each side, label each pin number, name, and indicate the notch/dot at pin-1 end.)*

**Signal Functions:**

1. **PSEN — Program Store Enable (Pin 29, Output):** This is an *active-low* output signal that *enables the external program memory* (ROM/EPROM) to be read. When the 8051 fetches instruction bytes from external ROM, it activates PSEN. It is *not* activated for internal ROM access. **(1 mark)**

2. **ALE — Address Latch Enable (Pin 30, Output):** The 8051's external bus is *multiplexed* — the lower byte of address (A0–A7) and data (D0–D7) share the same physical pins (Port 0). ALE is used to *latch* the address byte into an external 74LS373 latch, so the address remains stable while data is being transferred. ALE is high during the address phase and low during the data phase of every machine cycle. **(1 mark)**

3. **EA — External Access (Pin 31, Input):** This pin selects between internal and external program memory.
   - **EA = 1 (tied to VCC):** The 8051 executes from *internal* ROM for addresses 0000H–0FFFH, and from *external* ROM for 1000H–FFFFH.
   - **EA = 0 (tied to GND):** The 8051 always fetches from *external* ROM (e.g., in 8031, which has no internal ROM). **(1 mark)**

4. **RST — Reset (Pin 9, Input):** A *high* on this pin (≥ 2 machine cycles) resets the 8051: PC ← 0000H, all SFRs ← 00H (except SP ← 07H and P0–P3 ← 0FFH). The pin has an internal pull-down. **(1 mark)**

5. **XTAL1 (Pin 19, Input) & XTAL2 (Pin 18, Output):** These two pins connect to a *quartz crystal* (typically 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz) and two 30 pF capacitors to form an **on-chip Pierce oscillator**. XTAL1 is the input to the inverting amplifier; XTAL2 is its output. The resulting clock drives the internal timing. An *external* clock source can also be driven into XTAL1. **(2 marks)**

#### (b) Harvard vs Von Neumann Architecture [7 marks]

**Harvard Architecture of 8051 (Modified Harvard):**

The 8051 uses a *modified Harvard* architecture, meaning it has **physically separate** program and data memory spaces, each with its **own address bus and data bus**. This allows the CPU to *simultaneously* access an instruction byte (from ROM) and a data byte (from RAM) in a single machine cycle, doubling throughput for many operations.

```
[Program Memory ROM] --[PC bus]--> [CPU]
[Data Memory RAM]    --[DPTR bus]--> [CPU]
```

- Program memory: 64 KB (4 KB internal + 60 KB external).
- Data memory: 64 KB external + 256 bytes internal (128 user RAM + 128 SFR).

**Von Neumann Architecture (e.g., 8085):**

A *single shared bus* carries both instructions and data. The CPU must fetch an instruction *or* data in any given bus cycle (mutually exclusive). This is called the **Von Neumann bottleneck** — it limits speed because the bus is a single shared resource.

```
[Memory (ROM + RAM)] --[Single Bus]--> [CPU]
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Von Neumann (8085) | Harvard (8051) |
|---|---|---|
| Buses | Single shared | Two separate (code, data) |
| Memory | Unified | Separate ROM & RAM |
| Simultaneous fetch + data | No | Yes |
| Speed | Slower (bottleneck) | Faster (parallelism) |
| Hardware | Simpler | More complex |
| 8051 exception | — | External bus is *multiplexed* (modified Harvard) |

**(3 marks for Harvard diagram + 2 marks for Von Neumann diagram + 2 marks for the comparison table and discussion.)**

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **microprocessor-based system** (8085). List the major external chips required and justify the need for each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare microprocessor and microcontroller architectures in a **tabular form** with at least **eight points**. State **two application areas** where a microcontroller is preferred over a microprocessor. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) Microprocessor-Based System Architecture [7 marks]

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid block in Section 4.1 for the complete architecture.)*

**Major External Chips & Justification:**

1. **Program Memory (ROM/EPROM — e.g., 2732):** 8085 has *no on-chip ROM*. Firmware must be stored in an external ROM that occupies the code memory space. **(1 mark)**

2. **Data Memory (RAM — e.g., 6116):** 8085 has *no on-chip RAM*. Run-time variables, stack, and buffers need an external RAM. **(1 mark)**

3. **I/O Port (PPI 8255):** 8085 has *no dedicated I/O pins*. The 8255 provides 24 programmable I/O lines organised into three 8-bit ports (PA, PB, PC). **(1 mark)**

4. **Timer/Counter (8253/8254):** 8085 has *no built-in timers* for delay/event counting. The 8253 provides three independent 16-bit counters. **(1 mark)**

5. **UART (8251):** 8085 has *no hardware serial port*. The 8251 implements asynchronous serial communication (RS-232 compatible). **(0.5 mark)**

6. **Interrupt Controller (8259):** 8085 has *only 5 interrupt pins* (TRAP, RST 7.5, 6.5, 5.5, INTR). The 8259 expands this to handle up to 8 (cascadable to 64) interrupts. **(0.5 mark)**

7. **Address Latch (74LS373):** The 8085 multiplexes the lower address byte (A0–A7) with the data bus (D0–D7) on pins AD0–AD7. An external latch, triggered by ALE, demultiplexes them so the address remains stable. **(1 mark)**

8. **Clock Generator (8284):** The 8085 has *no on-chip oscillator*. The 8284 generates the CLK and RESET signals from a crystal. **(1 mark)**

#### (b) Comparison & Applications [7 marks]

**Tabular Comparison:** *(Refer to the comparison table in Section 2.4 for the full 17-point matrix. Any 8 points carry 4 marks; 1 mark per row × 4 = 4 marks here.)*

**Two Application Areas Where Microcontroller is Preferred:**

1. **Automotive Engine Control Unit (ECU):** Real-time sensor sampling (crank angle, oxygen, throttle position) and actuator control (fuel injector, spark plug) require *deterministic, low-latency response* with *minimal PCB area* and *low power*. A microcontroller (e.g., Infineon AURIX) is ideal because it integrates timers, ADC, CAN, and PWM, all deterministic and in one chip. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **IoT Sensor Node (e.g., smart agriculture, wearable health monitor):** These devices must run for *months* on a coin cell (CR2032) and perform periodic sensor reads + wireless transmission. A microcontroller (e.g., ESP32, ATmega328) offers *deep-sleep currents in the µA range*, *integrated ADC*, and *on-chip flash* — impossible to achieve with a microprocessor system that needs always-on external chips. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks (verified KTU valuation patterns):**
>
> 1. **Pin number/name mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** Students write "Pin 9 = ALE" or "Pin 30 = EA". The correct assignments are: *RST = 9, PSEN = 29, ALE = 30, EA = 31, XTAL1 = 19, XTAL2 = 18*. **Memorise these six first; they appear in nearly every 8051 pin diagram question.**
>
> 2. **Confusing PSEN and RD (–1 mark):** PSEN reads *external program memory (ROM)*; RD reads *external data memory (RAM)*. They are *different* signals on different pins (29 vs 17). PSEN is activated only for code fetch.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Port 0's open-drain nature (–1 mark):** When a question asks "Can P0 be used directly as a general output port?", many students answer "Yes, like P1". The correct answer: **No, P0 needs external 10 kΩ pull-ups** because it lacks internal pull-up resistors.
>
> 4. **Treating 8051 memory as 64 KB unified (–1 mark):** The 8051 has 64 KB code + 64 KB data, *not* 64 KB total. Confusing this with the 8085 (which has 64 KB unified) costs a full mark.
>
> 5. **Mixing up machine cycle formula (–1 mark):** Writing $T_{machine} = 1/f_{osc}$ is wrong. It is $T_{machine} = 12/f_{osc}$. The "12 clocks per machine cycle" is the most-asked KTU fact for 8051.
>
> 6. **Not drawing the notch/dot indicator (–1 mark):** For the 8051 pin diagram, always mark the *notch* (or dot) at the pin-1 end to show orientation. Drawing pins without orientation loses a mark.
>
> 7. **EA pin logic inverted (–1 mark):** EA = 1 means *use internal ROM* (active); EA = 0 means *force external ROM*. Many students reverse this.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall before the exam):**
>
> - **µP vs µC core idea:** Microprocessor = *CPU only*; Microcontroller = *CPU + Memory + Peripherals on one chip*.
> - **Architecture:** µP → Von Neumann; 8051 → Modified Harvard (separate code/data, but external bus is multiplexed).
> - **8051 key numbers to remember cold:** 8-bit ALU, 4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM, 32 I/O lines, 2 timers, 1 UART, 5 interrupts, 111 instructions, 40 pins, +5 V.
> - **The "Golden 6" pins:** RST (9), PSEN (29), ALE (30), EA (31), XTAL1 (19), XTAL2 (18). Their *exact pin numbers* are asked almost every exam.
> - **Port 0 = open-drain** (no internal pull-up). Ports 1, 2, 3 have internal pull-ups.
> - **Port 0 doubles** as multiplexed low-byte address/data bus (AD0–AD7).
> - **Port 2 supplies** the high-byte address (A8–A15) when external memory is used.
> - **Port 3 alternates** as RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD — 8 alternate functions, one per pin.
> - **Machine cycle rule:** $T_{machine} = 12 / f_{osc}$. At 11.0592 MHz → 1.085 µs.
> - **Baud rate formula:** $TH1 = 256 - (f_{osc} / (384 \times Baud))$. For 9600 baud → TH1 = 0xFD.
> - **Crystal preference:** **11.0592 MHz** for serial comms (exact integer division); **12 MHz** for general timing (1 µs machine cycle).
> - **Reset pulse width:** ≥ 2 machine cycles, typically 100 ms with 10 kΩ + 10 µF RC.
> - **EA = 1 → use internal ROM first**; **EA = 0 → use external ROM only** (e.g., 8031).
> - **PSEN ↔ external code fetch; RD/WR ↔ external data access.** Never confuse them.
> - **ALE** is the demultiplex control signal — pulse it to a 74LS373 latch.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Architecture, I/O Port Structure, Register Organization - Special Function Registers, Memory Organization 8051 Microcontrollers: Instruction Set, Addressing Modes, Simple Assembly Language Programs: Arithmetic (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication and Division), Transfer a Block of Data from One Internal Memory Location to Another

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The 8051 Microcontroller — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Manoj K. Mishra / Mazidi Standard Terminology)**
> The **Intel 8051** is an **8-bit** Harvard-architecture CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) single-chip microcontroller introduced by Intel in 1980. It contains an integrated **8-bit CPU** (using the **Accumulator (A)** as the primary working register), **4 KB of on-chip ROM/Program Memory**, **128 Bytes of on-chip RAM/Data Memory**, **four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)**, **two 16-bit Timer/Counters (T0, T1)**, a **full-duplex UART (Serial Port)**, **5 interrupt sources**, and an **on-chip oscillator** — all fabricated on a single CMOS die using **HMOS/CMOS technology**, and is pin-compatible with the **40-pin DIP package**.

The 8051 family operates with a **12-clock oscillator cycle per machine cycle** and has an instruction cycle time of **1 µs** at the standard crystal frequency of **12 MHz**. The 8051 has **64 KB each of code and XDATA address space**, providing a **16-bit program counter (PC)** and a **16-bit data pointer (DPTR)** for external memory access.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Shop" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine a **fully self-contained bicycle shop** built into a single small garage:
>
> - **The Shopkeeper (CPU + ALU)** does all the thinking and calculations at a single desk.
> - **The Notice Board (Accumulator + B Register + R0–R7 Banks)** holds only the work-in-progress notes — when the shopkeeper needs to add two numbers, he places them on this small board.
> - **The Filing Cabinet (Program Memory ROM)** stores the permanent "instruction manual" — it never gets erased.
> - **The Scratchpad (Internal RAM, 128 Bytes)** holds temporary scraps of paper with current job details.
> - **The Four Shop Doors (P0, P1, P2, P3)** are the input/output points where the shop interacts with the outside world (customers, suppliers).
> - **The Wall Clock & Stopwatches (T0, T1 — Timers/Counters)** keep time for internal events.
> - **The Telephone (UART — Serial Port)** allows the shop to call and receive calls from other shops (other microcontrollers/PCs).
> - **The Intercom / Doorbell (Interrupt System)** alerts the shopkeeper to urgent events that need immediate attention, even if he is busy.

This shopkeeper never leaves the garage — **everything he needs is on the same property**, which is why we call the 8051 a *microcontroller* (a "micro-computer-in-a-chip") and not just a microprocessor.

## 1.3 Salient Features of the 8051 (Key Numbers to Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU High-Yield Numbers — Must Be Memorized**
> - **8-bit** ALU and Data Bus
> - **8-bit** Accumulator (A) and **8-bit** B Register
> - **16-bit** Program Counter (PC) — addresses up to **64 KB** code space
> - **16-bit** Data Pointer (DPTR = DPH + DPL) for external/XDATA addressing
> - **4 KB** on-chip ROM (8051) / **8 KB** (8052)
> - **128 Bytes** on-chip RAM (8051) / **256 Bytes** (8052)
> - **4 × 8-bit** I/O Ports (32 I/O lines total)
> - **2 × 16-bit** Timers/Counters (T0, T1)
> - **1** Full-Duplex UART (Serial Port)
> - **5** Interrupt Sources (Reset, 2 External, 2 Timer, 1 Serial)
> - **64 KB** external code memory (separate via **PSEN̄**)
> - **64 KB** external data memory (separate via **RD̄, WR̄**)
> - **128** user-defined software flags in SFR space (bit-addressable 20h–2Fh + SFR bits)
> - **12 MHz** standard crystal → **1 µs** instruction cycle
> - **40-pin** DIP package
> - **2 μs** for most arithmetic instructions (MOV, ADD), **4 μs** for MUL/DIV

## 1.4 Harvard vs Von Neumann Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction**
> The 8051 uses a **Harvard Architecture** in the strictest on-chip sense — it has **physically separate program and data memory buses**, allowing **simultaneous fetch of an opcode and a data byte**. This is different from a Von Neumann architecture (used in 8085, x86) which uses a single shared bus.

$$
\boxed{\text{Harvard: Separate Code \& Data Buses} \;\; \lVert \;\; \text{Von Neumann: Single Shared Bus}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Block Diagram of the 8051 — Functional Decomposition

The 8051 is composed of **9 major functional blocks** interconnected by an **8-bit internal data bus** and a **16-bit address bus**. Below is a structured analytical breakdown:

| S.No | Block | Function | KTU Significance |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **CPU + ALU** | Performs arithmetic, logic, and decision operations | Heart of the controller |
| 2 | **Program Counter (PC)** | 16-bit, holds address of next instruction | Auto-increments every machine cycle |
| 3 | **Accumulator (A)** | 8-bit primary working register | Used in ~65% of all instructions |
| 4 | **B Register** | Used only in MUL AB and DIV AB | Otherwise general-purpose scratch |
| 5 | **Stack Pointer (SP)** | 8-bit, points to top of stack in RAM | Initializes to **07H** on reset |
| 6 | **PSW (Program Status Word)** | Holds Carry, Aux Carry, Reg-Bank Select, Overflow, Parity flags | **D3H** in SFR space |
| 7 | **Data Pointer (DPTR)** | 16-bit, formed by DPH (83H) + DPL (82H) | For external/XDATA access & code lookup tables |
| 8 | **Timers T0, T1** | 16-bit, with 4 modes each | Mode 0, 1, 2, 3 |
| 9 | **UART (Serial Port)** | Full-duplex, 4 modes | Mode 1 (8-bit UART) most common |

## 2.2 The Program Status Word (PSW) — Bit-by-Bit Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PSW Register (SFR Address D3H) — Must Know for KTU**

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| PSW.7 | **CY** | D7H | **Carry Flag** — set on arithmetic overflow / borrow |
| PSW.6 | **AC** | D6H | **Auxiliary Carry** — for BCD arithmetic (bit 3 → bit 4) |
| PSW.5 | **F0** | D5H | **User-Defined Flag 0** — software free |
| PSW.4 | **RS1** | D4H | Register Bank Select Bit 1 |
| PSW.3 | **RS0** | D3H | Register Bank Select Bit 0 |
| PSW.2 | **OV** | D2H | **Overflow Flag** — signed arithmetic |
| PSW.1 | **–** | D1H | Reserved (undefined) |
| PSW.0 | **P** | D0H | **Parity Flag** — automatically set to maintain even parity of A |

$$
\boxed{\text{Register Bank Selection: } RS1RS0 = 00 \rightarrow \text{Bank 0 (00H–07H)}}
$$

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank | RAM Address |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | 00H – 07H |
| 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | 08H – 0FH |
| 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | 10H – 17H |
| 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | 18H – 1FH |

## 2.3 I/O Port Structure (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The 8051 has **four 8-bit bidirectional ports**: P0, P1, P2, P3. Each pin has a complex internal structure involving **latches, output drivers, and input buffers**.

### 2.3.1 Port 0 (P0, Pins 32–39, Address 80H)

- **Dual function**: Acts as **lower-order address/data bus (AD0–AD7)** when external memory is accessed, otherwise pure I/O.
- **Has no internal pull-up resistors** → requires **external 10 kΩ pull-ups** when used as a general-purpose output port.
- During external memory access, the port switches to **true bidirectional** (no latch, direct bus drive).

### 2.3.2 Port 1 (P1, Pins 1–8, Address 90H)

- **Pure I/O only** — no alternate function.
- Has **internal pull-up resistors** (≈ 30–40 kΩ, weak).
- **Quasi-bidirectional**: Writing 1 floats the pin; external circuit can pull it low (standard 8051 behavior).

### 2.3.3 Port 2 (P2, Pins 21–28, Address A0H)

- Acts as **higher-order address bus (A8–A15)** during external memory fetch.
- Has internal pull-ups.

### 2.3.4 Port 3 (P3, Pins 10–17, Address B0H) — Alternate Functions

| Pin | Alt. Function | Description |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| P3.0 | **RxD** | Serial input |
| P3.1 | **TxD** | Serial output |
| P3.2 | **INT0̄** | External Interrupt 0 |
| P3.3 | **INT1̄** | External Interrupt 1 |
| P3.4 | **T0** | Timer 0 external input |
| P3.5 | **T1** | Timer 1 external input |
| P3.6 | **WR̄** | External data memory write strobe |
| P3.7 | **RD̄** | External data memory read strobe |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When using alternate functions of P3, the corresponding SFR latch must be set to **1** to allow the alternate function to control the pin.

## 2.4 Special Function Registers (SFRs) — The Heart of 8051 Configuration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SFR Address Space: 80H – FFH (128 Bytes, only 21–26 are implemented in the basic 8051)**

| SFR | Address | Function |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **P0** | 80H | Port 0 latch |
| **SP** | 81H | Stack Pointer (8-bit, default 07H) |
| **DPL** | 82H | Data Pointer Low byte |
| **DPH** | 83H | Data Pointer High byte |
| **PCON** | 87H | Power Control Register |
| **TCON** | 88H | Timer/Counter Control |
| **TMOD** | 89H | Timer/Counter Mode |
| **TL0** | 8AH | Timer 0 Low |
| **TL1** | 8BH | Timer 1 Low |
| **TH0** | 8CH | Timer 0 High |
| **TH1** | 8DH | Timer 1 High |
| **P1** | 90H | Port 1 latch |
| **SCON** | 98H | Serial Port Control |
| **SBUF** | 99H | Serial Data Buffer |
| **P2** | A0H | Port 2 latch |
| **IE** | A8H | Interrupt Enable |
| **P3** | B0H | Port 3 latch |
| **IP** | B8H | Interrupt Priority |
| **PSW** | D0H | Program Status Word |
| **ACC (A)** | E0H | Accumulator |
| **B** | F0H | B Register |

> [!WARNING]
> **SFRs with addresses ending in 0H or 8H are BIT-ADDRESSABLE** (e.g., P0, TCON, P1, SCON, P2, IE, P3, IP, PSW, A, B). Others (SP, DPL, TMOD, etc.) are byte-addressable only.

## 2.5 Memory Organization (KTU-Most-Asked Topic)

The 8051 has a **physically and logically separate program and data memory** (Harvard).

### 2.5.1 Program Memory (Code Space, 64 KB Max)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Code Memory Map:} \quad & 0000\text{H to }0FFF\text{H} \rightarrow \text{On-chip ROM (4 KB in 8051)} \\
& 1000\text{H to }FFFF\text{H} \rightarrow \text{External ROM (up to 64 KB)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reset Vector = 0000H**. The first instruction after reset is fetched from address **0000H**. To leave room for the reset, programmers typically place an **LJMP 0030H** at 0000H and start the main program at 0030H.

Interrupt vectors occupy **0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H** (8 bytes each).

### 2.5.2 Internal Data Memory (Lower 128 Bytes, 00H–7FH)

| Address Range | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| 00H – 1FH | **4 Register Banks × 8 bytes** (R0–R7) |
| 20H – 2FH | **16 Bytes bit-addressable** (128 bit addresses 00H–7FH) |
| 30H – 7FH | **80 Bytes general-purpose RAM** (scratchpad) |

### 2.5.3 Upper RAM / SFR Space (80H–FFH)

From 80H to FFH, the address range is **overlapping** with the SFR space. Accessing addresses 80H–FFH depends on the instruction:
- **Direct addressing** (e.g., `MOV A, 80H`) → accesses **SFRs**.
- **Indirect addressing** (e.g., `MOV A, @R0` with R0 ≥ 80H) → accesses **upper 128 bytes** (only on 8052/DS89C4x0).

## 2.6 The 8051 Instruction Set — High-Yield Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Students must classify the instruction set and identify **opcode size** (1, 2, or 3 bytes) and **execution time** (1 or 2 machine cycles).

### 2.6.1 Total Instruction Count

$$
\boxed{\text{8051 has 111 base instructions (255 opcodes including variants)}}
$$

### 2.6.2 Five Functional Groups

| Group | Examples | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Transfer** | MOV, MOVX, MOVC, XCH, PUSH, POP | Copy data between locations |
| **Arithmetic** | ADD, ADDC, SUBB, MUL, DIV, INC, DEC | Numeric operations on A |
| **Logical** | ANL, ORL, XRL, CPL, CLR, RL, RLC, RR, RRC | Bitwise operations |
| **Boolean (Bit)** | SETB, CLR, ANL C, ORL C, MOV C, JC, JNC, JB, JNB | Single-bit operations |
| **Branch / Control** | LJMP, SJMP, AJMP, JMP @A+DPTR, ACALL, LCALL, RET, JZ, JNZ, CJNE, DJNZ, NOP | Program flow control |

### 2.6.3 Opcode Size & Machine Cycle Reference

| Opcode Bytes | Example | Machine Cycles | Time @ 12 MHz |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 byte | `INC A` | 1 | 1 µs |
| 2 bytes | `MOV A, #55H` | 1 | 1 µs |
| 3 bytes | `MOV 30H, #0FFH` | 2 | 2 µs |
| MUL/DIV | `MUL AB` | 4 | 4 µs |

## 2.7 The Five Addressing Modes of the 8051 (KTU Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Recognize and apply all five addressing modes correctly.

### 2.7.1 Immediate Addressing (`#data`)

$$
\text{Format: } \texttt{MOV A, \#25H} \quad \rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow 25H
$$

The data is part of the instruction itself. Used to load **constants**. Only source operand can be immediate.

### 2.7.2 Register Addressing

$$
\text{Format: } \texttt{MOV A, R0} \quad \rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow R0
$$

Operates on one of the **8 working registers (R0–R7)** of the currently selected bank. **Zero bytes** for operand — most efficient.

### 2.7.3 Direct Addressing

$$
\text{Format: } \texttt{MOV A, 30H} \quad \rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow \text{memory[30H]}
$$

Used to access **lower 128 bytes of RAM (00H–7FH)** and **SFRs (80H–FFH)**.

### 2.7.4 Register Indirect Addressing (`@Ri`)

$$
\text{Format: } \texttt{MOV A, @R0} \quad \rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow \text{memory[R0]}
$$

The contents of R0 or R1 are used as the **pointer to memory**. Only R0 and R1 can be used. Allows **16-bit addressing** with DPTR (`@DPTR`).

### 2.7.5 Indexed Addressing (`@A + DPTR` or `@A + PC`)

$$
\text{Format: } \texttt{MOVC A, @A+DPTR} \quad \rightarrow \quad A \leftarrow \text{code[A+DPTR]}
$$

Used exclusively for **look-up tables (LUTs)** in program memory. The most powerful addressing mode for table-lookup and branch tables (`JMP @A+DPTR`).

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Memory & Timing

| Quantity | Formula | Value @ 12 MHz |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Oscillator Period** | $T_{osc} = 1/f_{osc}$ | $1/12\text{ MHz} = 83.33$ ns |
| **State Time** | $T_S = T_{osc}$ | 83.33 ns |
| **Machine Cycle** | $T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{osc}$ | **1 µs** |
| **Instruction Time (1 cycle)** | $1 \times T_{MC}$ | 1 µs |
| **Instruction Time (2 cycles)** | $2 \times T_{MC}$ | 2 µs |
| **Max Code Space** | $2^{16}$ | 64 KB |
| **Internal RAM** | — | 128 B (8051), 256 B (8052) |
| **SFR Space** | $80\text{H} \to FF\text{H}$ | 128 bytes (21–26 used) |
| **External Data (XDATA)** | $2^{16}$ | 64 KB |

$$
\boxed{T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz} \rightarrow T_{MC} = 1\ \mu s}
$$

## 2.9 Engineering Utility — Where is the 8051 Used Today?

- **Automotive ECUs** (older systems, e.g., body control modules).
- **Consumer appliances**: washing machines, microwave ovens, air-conditioner remotes.
- **Industrial control**: PLCs, motor drivers, sensor interfaces.
- **Educational platforms**: the 8051 is the **de-facto teaching platform** in Indian universities (including KTU) because of its simple, regular, fully-documented instruction set and freely available simulators (Keil µVision, EdSim51, Proteus VSM).
- **IoT sensor nodes** (8051 cores in modern System-on-Chips, e.g., Nuvoton N76E003, Silicon Labs EFM8).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Program 1: Multi-Byte Addition of Two 16-Bit Numbers

**Problem:** Add the 16-bit number stored at RAM locations **30H (LSB)** and **31H (MSB)** to the 16-bit number at **40H (LSB)** and **41H (MSB)**. Store the 16-bit result at **50H (LSB)** and **51H (MSB)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** Since the 8051 ADD instruction operates only on 8 bits, we must add the **low bytes first** using `ADD A, src`, then add the **high bytes with carry** using `ADDC A, src`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address 30H} &= \text{X_L} \quad (\text{1st number LSB}) \\
\text{Address 31H} &= \text{X_H} \quad (\text{1st number MSB}) \\
\text{Address 40H} &= \text{Y_L} \quad (\text{2nd number LSB}) \\
\text{Address 41H} &= \text{Y_H} \quad (\text{2nd number MSB})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Add Low Bytes
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\leftarrow \text{memory}[30\text{H}] \quad &\text{(Load X\_L into A)} \\
A &\leftarrow A + \text{memory}[40\text{H}] \quad &\text{(Add Y\_L, capture Carry in CY)} \\
\text{memory}[50\text{H}] &\leftarrow A \quad &\text{(Store sum\_L to 50H)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Add High Bytes with Carry
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\leftarrow \text{memory}[31\text{H}] \quad &\text{(Load X\_H into A)} \\
A &\leftarrow A + \text{memory}[41\text{H}] + C \quad &\text{(Add Y\_H and previous Carry)} \\
\text{memory}[51\text{H}] &\leftarrow A \quad &\text{(Store sum\_H to 51H)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Complete Assembly Code

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     A, 30H        ; A = X_L  (1 byte, 1 cycle)
        ADD     A, 40H        ; A = X_L + Y_L, carry to CY
        MOV     50H, A        ; Store sum_L
        MOV     A, 31H        ; A = X_H
        ADDC    A, 41H        ; A = X_H + Y_H + CY
        MOV     51H, A        ; Store sum_H
HERE:   SJMP    HERE          ; Infinite loop (trap)
        END
```

### Byte / Cycle Analysis

| Line | Instruction | Bytes | Cycles | Time |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | MOV A, 30H | 2 | 1 | 1 µs |
| 2 | ADD A, 40H | 2 | 1 | 1 µs |
| 3 | MOV 50H, A | 2 | 1 | 1 µs |
| 4 | MOV A, 31H | 2 | 1 | 1 µs |
| 5 | ADDC A, 41H | 2 | 1 | 1 µs |
| 6 | MOV 51H, A | 2 | 1 | 1 µs |
| **Total** | | **12** | **6** | **6 µs** |

## 3.2 Worked Program 2: 8-bit × 8-bit Multiplication

**Problem:** Multiply the unsigned byte in **RAM 30H** by the byte in **RAM 31H** and store the 16-bit product in **RAM 40H (LSB)** and **41H (MSB)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** The 8051 has a built-in `MUL AB` instruction that multiplies A by B and places the 16-bit result in **B (high byte) and A (low byte)**.

### Mathematical Foundation
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } a &= \text{memory}[30\text{H}], \quad b = \text{memory}[31\text{H}] \\
\text{Product } P &= a \times b, \quad \text{where } P \in [0, 65025] \\
\text{Result layout:} \quad & B \leftarrow P_{MSB}, \quad A \leftarrow P_{LSB} \\
P &= (B \ll 8) \;\vert\; A
\end{aligned}
$$

### Derivation Step-by-Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & A \leftarrow \text{memory}[30\text{H}] \quad &\text{(2 bytes, 1 cycle)} \\
\text{Step 2: } & B \leftarrow \text{memory}[31\text{H}] \quad &\text{(2 bytes, 1 cycle)} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \{B, A\} \leftarrow A \times B \quad &\text{(1 byte, 4 cycles)} \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{memory}[40\text{H}] \leftarrow A \quad &\text{(2 bytes, 1 cycle)} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{memory}[41\text{H}] \leftarrow B \quad &\text{(2 bytes, 1 cycle)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Complete Assembly Code

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     A, 30H        ; A = multiplicand
        MOV     B, 31H        ; B = multiplier
        MUL     AB            ; B:A = A * B  (overflow flag set if > FFH)
        MOV     40H, A        ; Store LSB
        MOV     41H, B        ; Store MSB
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

### Worked Numerical Example
$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= 0\text{F}5\text{H} = 245, \quad b = 0\text{B}7\text{H} = 183 \\
P &= 245 \times 183 = 44835 = 0\text{AF1BH} \\
\text{After MUL AB:} \quad & A = 0\text{1BH} \;(\text{LSB}), \quad B = 0\text{AFH} \;(\text{MSB}) \\
\text{After stores:} \quad & \text{memory}[40\text{H}] = 1\text{BH}, \quad \text{memory}[41\text{H}] = \text{AFH}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Program 3: 8-bit ÷ 8-bit Division

**Problem:** Divide the unsigned byte in **RAM 30H** by the byte in **RAM 31H**. Store the **quotient** in **40H** and the **remainder** in **41H**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** `DIV AB` divides A by B. The **integer quotient** goes to A, the **remainder** goes to B. The `OV` flag is set if division-by-zero occurs.

### Derivation Step-by-Step
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & A \leftarrow \text{memory}[30\text{H}] \quad &\text{(Dividend)} \\
\text{Step 2: } & B \leftarrow \text{memory}[31\text{H}] \quad &\text{(Divisor)} \\
\text{Step 3: } & A \leftarrow A \,\text{DIV}\, B \quad &\text{(Quotient)} \\
\text{Step 4: } & B \leftarrow A \,\text{MOD}\, B \quad &\text{(Remainder)} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{memory}[40\text{H}] \leftarrow A \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{memory}[41\text{H}] \leftarrow B
\end{aligned}
$$

### Complete Assembly Code

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     A, 30H        ; A = dividend
        MOV     B, 31H        ; B = divisor
        DIV     AB            ; A = quotient, B = remainder
        MOV     40H, A        ; Store quotient
        MOV     41H, B        ; Store remainder
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

## 3.4 Worked Program 4: BCD Subtraction Using `SUBB`

**Problem:** Subtract the 2-digit BCD number in RAM 30H from the 2-digit BCD number in RAM 31H using `SUBB` and store the BCD-corrected result in 40H.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** `SUBB A, src` performs `A = A - src - CY`. Since 8051 has no native BCD subtract, we use the **10's complement trick**: subtract from 99H, then from 100H, then DA A to get the BCD result. This is the standard KTU approach.

### Step-by-Step BCD Subtraction Algorithm

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & A \leftarrow 99\text{H} \\
\text{Step 2: } & A \leftarrow A - \text{memory}[30\text{H}] \quad \text{(i.e., 9's complement)} \\
\text{Step 3: } & A \leftarrow A + 01\text{H} \quad \text{(i.e., add 1 to get 10's complement)} \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Add the minuend (memory[31H]) using ADDC, then DA A} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{memory}[40\text{H}] \leftarrow A
\end{aligned}
$$

### Complete Assembly Code

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     A, #99H       ; A = 99H
        SUBB    A, 30H        ; A = 99H - subtrahend (with C=0)
        ADD     A, #01H       ; A = 10's complement of subtrahend
        MOV     R0, A         ; Save 10's complement
        MOV     A, 31H        ; A = minuend
        ADDC    A, R0         ; A = minuend + 10's comp(subtrahend)
        DA      A             ; Decimal adjust
        MOV     40H, A        ; Store BCD result
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

## 3.5 Worked Program 5: Block Data Transfer (KTU Most-Asked)

**Problem:** Transfer a block of **N bytes** starting from internal RAM address **30H** to a new block starting at RAM address **60H**. The count N is stored at RAM location **20H**. The 8051 has no dedicated block-move instruction — we must use a **loop with indirect addressing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic:** Use R0 as the source pointer and R1 as the destination pointer. Use a loop counter (R2) initialized from memory[20H]. Use `DJNZ` for the loop.

### Algorithm
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initialize:} \quad & R0 \leftarrow 30\text{H} \quad \text{(source pointer)} \\
& R1 \leftarrow 60\text{H} \quad \text{(destination pointer)} \\
& R2 \leftarrow \text{memory}[20\text{H}] \quad \text{(counter)} \\
\text{Loop body:} \quad & A \leftarrow \text{memory}[R0] \\
& \text{memory}[R1] \leftarrow A \\
& R0 \leftarrow R0 + 1 \\
& R1 \leftarrow R1 + 1 \\
& R2 \leftarrow R2 - 1, \text{ if } R2 \neq 0 \text{ then jump to loop}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Mathematical Loop Analysis
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iterations} &= N \\
\text{Bytes used for loop} &= 3 \text{ registers} + 1 \text{ counter slot} \\
\text{For } N = 10: \quad & \text{Total cycles} = (4 \text{ cycles/iter} \times 10) + 5 \text{ setup} = 45 \text{ cycles} = 45\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Complete Assembly Code

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     R0, #30H      ; R0 = source pointer
        MOV     R1, #60H      ; R1 = destination pointer
        MOV     R2, 20H       ; R2 = counter (N)
LOOP:   MOV     A, @R0        ; A = source[R0]
        MOV     @R1, A        ; destination[R1] = A
        INC     R0            ; R0++
        INC     R1            ; R1++
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP      ; R2--, if R2 ≠ 0, goto LOOP
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

### Per-Iteration Cycle Cost

| Line | Instruction | Bytes | Cycles | Time |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | MOV A, @R0 | 1 | 1 | 1 µs |
| 2 | MOV @R1, A | 1 | 1 | 1 µs |
| 3 | INC R0 | 1 | 1 | 1 µs |
| 4 | INC R1 | 1 | 1 | 1 µs |
| 5 | DJNZ R2, LOOP | 2 | 2 | 2 µs |
| **Per-iter total** | | **6** | **6** | **6 µs** |

$$
\boxed{T_{total} = 5_{setup} + 6N_{loop} \;\; \mu s}
$$

### Variant — Block Transfer with XDATA (External RAM)

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     DPTR, #1000H  ; DPTR points to external source
        MOV     R0, #60H      ; Internal destination pointer
        MOV     R2, 20H       ; Counter
LOOP:   MOVX    A, @DPTR      ; Read from external memory
        MOV     @R0, A        ; Write to internal RAM
        INC     DPTR
        INC     R0
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP
HERE:   SJMP    HERE
        END
```

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Addressing Mode vs Instruction Type

| Addressing Mode | Example | Opcode (Hex) | Bytes | Cycles | Used For |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Immediate** | `MOV A, #55H` | 74 55 | 2 | 1 | Load constants |
| **Register** | `MOV A, R0` | E8 | 1 | 1 | Fast register ops |
| **Direct** | `MOV A, 30H` | E5 30 | 2 | 1 | RAM/SFR access |
| **Register Indirect** | `MOV A, @R0` | E6 | 1 | 1 | Pointer-based loops |
| **Indexed** | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | 93 | 1 | 2 | Look-up tables |
