<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stepper Motor (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> A **stepper motor** is a *brushless, synchronous electro-mechanical actuator* that converts a train of discrete digital input pulses into precisely defined angular mechanical displacements called **steps**. Each input pulse causes the rotor to advance by a fixed angular increment known as the **step angle** ($\theta_s$), making it an *open-loop position transducer-actuator* that requires no feedback sensors for basic position control.

**Conformance to KTU 2024 Module-2 Syllabus (PBBRT404):** The stepper motor is studied as the canonical *actuator peripheral* interfaced to microcontrollers through dedicated *driver ICs* (e.g., ULN2003, L293D) and *sequencer firmware* executing in *port-pulse generation mode*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tick-Tock Clock Hand**
> Imagine you are standing in front of a **classic analog wall clock**. The minute hand does **not** sweep smoothly. Instead, it moves in **tiny discrete jumps** — *click, click, click* — once every second. Each *click* corresponds to a **fixed angular step** (e.g., 6° on that clock).
>
> A **stepper motor behaves exactly like this clock hand**, but at vastly higher precision. Every **electrical pulse** you feed into its coils is equivalent to a *command* that says: *"Move exactly X degrees, and stop — do not overshoot."* Because the rotation is locked magnetically at each step, the motor **holds its position** even after the pulse ends, like a ratchet that refuses to slide backward.

| Parameter | Clock Hand | Stepper Motor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Discrete increment** | 6° per tick | Step angle $\theta_s$ (typically **0.9° to 90°**) |
| **Driver of motion** | Quartz oscillator + gear train | Microcontroller pulse train |
| **Hold mechanism** | Friction & gear mesh | Magnetic detent / holding torque |
| **Feedback required?** | No (open-loop) | No (open-loop) |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Holding Torque ($T_h$):** The maximum static torque the motor can sustain without losing synchronism (typically **0.1 N·m to 50 N·m**).
- **Step Angle ($\theta_s$):** Fixed per-unit angular advance; common values are **1.8°, 0.9°, 7.5°, 15°, 90°**.
- **Step Resolution:** Equivalent *steps per revolution* (SPR), typically **48, 100, 200, 400, 500**.
- **Supply Voltage:** Driver DC bus, typically **5 V, 12 V, 24 V, 48 V**.
- **Phase Current:** Per-winding rated current, typically **0.5 A to 5 A per phase**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:**
> A stepper motor is fundamentally a **digital-to-analog converter of position** — it transforms a *count of digital pulses* into an *analog angular position*, governed by the master equation:
> $$\theta_{\text{rotor}} = N_{\text{pulses}} \times \theta_s$$
> where $N_{\text{pulses}}$ is the number of applied step pulses and $\theta_s$ is the fundamental step angle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Stepper Motors

A stepper motor is **strictly classified by the rotor-construction principle**, because the rotor dictates the torque mechanism, step resolution, and detent behavior.

### 2.1.1 Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor
- **Rotor:** Soft-iron, toothed, *non-magnetized* cylinder.
- **Stator:** Salient-pole, energized by phased windings.
- **Operating principle:** Rotor rotates to the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** ($\mathcal{R} = l / \mu A$) when a phase is energized.
- **Why it works:** Ferromagnetic materials naturally align with magnetic flux lines, and the rotor "jumps" to the tooth-stator alignment that minimizes the air-gap reluctance path.
- **Key characteristic:** Has **no detent torque** when un-energized (rotor is free to rotate manually).

### 2.1.2 Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor
- **Rotor:** Axially or radially magnetized *permanent magnet* (typically ferrite or NdFeB).
- **Stator:** Concentric windings.
- **Operating principle:** Rotor poles are attracted/repelled by the energized stator poles, producing coarse steps (typically **45° or 90°**).
- **Key characteristic:** Possesses a strong **detent torque** even when de-energized.

### 2.1.3 Hybrid (HB) Stepper Motor
- **Rotor:** A *combination* — a permanently magnetized shaft with toothed caps at both ends (stacked with axial offset).
- **Stator:** Multi-tooth salient poles.
- **Operating principle:** Combines the **high resolution of VR** with the **high holding torque of PM**.
- **Key characteristic:** The industry *workhorse*, standard step angles of **0.9°, 1.8°**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "VPH":**
> **V**ariable Reluctance → toothed soft iron.
> **P**ermanent Magnet → magnet rotor.
> **H**ybrid → both.
> When in doubt, remember: *Hybrid = Variable Reluctance resolution + Permanent Magnet torque.*

---

## 2.2 Stepping Modes (Excitation Sequences)

| Mode | Phases Energized Simultaneously | Effective Step Angle | Relative Torque | Smoothness |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Wave (1-phase-on) drive** | 1 | $\theta_s$ | $\approx 0.707 \cdot T_{h,\text{full}}$ | Rougher |
| **Full-step (2-phase-on) drive** | 2 | $\theta_s$ | $1.0 \cdot T_{h,\text{full}}$ | Rougher |
| **Half-step drive** | Alternating 1 & 2 | $\theta_s / 2$ | Variable | Smoother |
| **Microstepping** | PWM-weighted vector sum | $\theta_s / N_{\mu}$ | Reduced at small steps | Smoothest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** In a 4-phase stepper with step angle $\theta_s$, the **half-step mode** achieves **8 distinct stator states** instead of 4, halving the step angle and doubling the resolution.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the *minimum mandatory formula inventory* for solving any KTU university question on stepper motors. Memorize these and their boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Units | Boundary / Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Step Angle** | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_s}$ | $N_s$ = steps per revolution | degrees ($\degree$) | Always $0 < \theta_s \leq 90\degree$ |
| 2 | **Step Angle (HB type)** | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N \cdot P_h}$ | $N$ = rotor teeth, $P_h$ = stator phases | degrees | For *Hybrid* motors only |
| 3 | **Steps per Revolution** | $N_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s}$ | — | dimensionless | $N_s \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ (always integer) |
| 4 | **Shaft Angular Position** | $\theta_{\text{rotor}} = N_{\text{pulses}} \cdot \theta_s$ | $N_{\text{pulses}}$ = pulse count | degrees | Cumulative, modulo $360\degree$ |
| 5 | **Shaft Speed (RPM)** | $n = \dfrac{f \cdot 60}{N_s}$ | $f$ = pulse frequency (Hz) | rpm | Valid only when motor does not stall |
| 6 | **Shaft Speed (rad/s)** | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{N_s}$ | — | rad/s | $f \leq f_{\text{max, no-load}}$ |
| 7 | **Number of Rotor Teeth (HB)** | $N = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{P_h \cdot \theta_s}$ | $P_h$ = phases (2 or 4) | dimensionless | Must be an integer |
| 8 | **Pull-out Torque vs. Speed** | $T_{po} \propto \dfrac{1}{f}$ (approx.) | Empirical | N·m | Valid in mid-speed range |
| 9 | **Detent Torque (PM/HB)** | $T_d \approx (0.01 \text{ to } 0.1) \cdot T_h$ | $T_h$ = holding torque | N·m | Empirical, manufacturer-specific |
| 10 | **Microstep Angle** | $\theta_{\mu} = \dfrac{\theta_s}{N_{\mu}}$ | $N_{\mu}$ = microstep index (1, 2, 4, 8, …, 256) | degrees | $N_{\mu} = 2^k$, $k \in \mathbb{N}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Note on Table:** All vertical bars in equations above use $\vert$ (LaTeX), **never** a raw `|` pipe — this is enforced to keep KTU-Premier markdown tables parsable.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stepper motors are the **backbone actuator** of *open-loop* precision motion control. They are extensively used in:

- **3D Printers & CNC Machines** — X-Y-Z axis positioning (typically **NEMA 17, 1.8°, 200 steps/rev**).
- **Robotics** — Joint actuation in pick-and-place arms.
- **Disk Drives & Scanners** — Head-carriage positioning.
- **Medical Pumps & Syringe Drivers** — Precise volumetric fluid delivery.
- **Camera Lens Autofocus** — Smooth fine-grained lens movement.
- **Textile & Packaging Machinery** — Synchronized indexing of conveyor belts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The defining engineering advantage is **open-loop simplicity**: by counting pulses alone, the controller *knows* the rotor position to within $\pm \theta_s / 2$, eliminating expensive encoders.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Step Angle of a Hybrid Stepper Motor

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the master equation $\theta_s = 360^{\circ} / (N \cdot P_h)$ from first principles, starting with the geometry of the toothed rotor.

### Step 1 — Establish the Mechanical Geometry

A hybrid stepper motor has a rotor with $N$ uniformly distributed teeth around its circumference. The angular pitch (tooth-to-tooth spacing) on the rotor is:

$$
\theta_{t} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N}
$$

where:
- $\theta_{t}$ = rotor tooth pitch (in degrees),
- $N$ = number of teeth on the rotor.

### Step 2 — Identify the Phase-Advancement Per Step

When the stator excitation shifts from one phase to the next, the rotor must rotate so that **one rotor tooth aligns with the next energized stator pole pair**. The angular distance the rotor must travel depends on the number of stator phases $P_h$.

For a motor with $P_h$ phases, the rotor advances by **one tooth pitch per full electrical cycle**, but the number of distinct stable positions per cycle is:

$$
\text{Stator stable states per revolution} = N \cdot P_h
$$

**Reasoning (textual):** Each of the $N$ rotor teeth, in turn, must align with each of the $P_h$ phase-energized stator pole configurations. The total number of unique detent positions in one full revolution equals the product $N \cdot P_h$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Definition of Step Angle

By definition, the step angle is the **angle subtended between two adjacent stable detent positions** in a full $360^{\circ}$ revolution:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{\text{Number of stable positions per revolution}} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N \cdot P_h}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Master Equation

$$
\boxed{\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N \cdot P_h}}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU 2024 step-angle equation** for hybrid stepper motors.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> A 4-phase hybrid stepper motor has **50 rotor teeth**. Find: (a) the step angle, (b) the steps per revolution, (c) the rotor position after **325 input pulses**.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Step angle.

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N \cdot P_h} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{50 \cdot 4} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{200} = 1.8^{\circ}
$$

**Part (b):** Steps per revolution.

$$
N_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{1.8^{\circ}} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

**Part (c):** Rotor position after 325 pulses.

$$
\theta_{\text{rotor}} = N_{\text{pulses}} \cdot \theta_s = 325 \times 1.8^{\circ} = 585^{\circ}
$$

To express within a single revolution (mod $360^{\circ}$):

$$
\theta_{\text{rotor, mod}} = 585^{\circ} \mod 360^{\circ} = 225^{\circ}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\theta_s = 1.8^{\circ}$, $N_s = 200$ steps/rev, rotor settles at $225^{\circ}$ (i.e., $585^{\circ} \equiv 225^{\circ}$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shaft Speed as a Function of Pulse Frequency

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Establish the master speed equation $n = (f \cdot 60) / N_s$ from kinematic first principles.

### Step 1 — Define Angular Position vs. Time

Each input pulse causes a step of $\theta_s$ radians. Therefore, the rotor's angular position evolves as:

$$
\theta_{\text{rotor}}(t) = N_{\text{pulses}}(t) \cdot \theta_s
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Pulse Count to Time

If the input pulse train has a constant frequency $f$ (in Hz = pulses per second), then the number of pulses received in time $t$ is:

$$
N_{\text{pulses}}(t) = f \cdot t
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Position Equation

$$
\theta_{\text{rotor}}(t) = (f \cdot t) \cdot \theta_s
$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate to Obtain Angular Velocity

Angular velocity in rad/s is the time derivative:

$$
\omega = \frac{d\theta_{\text{rotor}}}{dt} = f \cdot \theta_s
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Revolutions per Minute (RPM)

Substitute $\theta_s = 2\pi / N_s$ (in radians per step):

$$
\omega = f \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N_s} \quad \text{[rad/s]}
$$

Convert rad/s to RPM by multiplying by $60 / (2\pi)$:

$$
n = \omega \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = f \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N_s} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \cdot f}{N_s}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Master Equation

$$
\boxed{n = \frac{60 \cdot f}{N_s} \quad \text{[rpm]}}
$$

### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> A stepper motor has **200 steps/rev** and is driven by a pulse train of **1.2 kHz**. Find: (a) the rotational speed in RPM, (b) the angular velocity in rad/s, (c) the time required for one full revolution.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Speed in RPM.

$$
n = \frac{60 \cdot f}{N_s} = \frac{60 \times 1200}{200} = \frac{72000}{200} = 360 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Part (b):** Angular velocity in rad/s.

$$
\omega = f \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N_s} = 1200 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{200} = 1200 \cdot 0.031416 = 37.70 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Part (c):** Time per revolution.

$$
T_{\text{rev}} = \frac{60}{n} = \frac{60}{360} = 0.1667 \text{ s} = 166.67 \text{ ms}
$$

**Alternative check:** $T_{\text{rev}} = N_s / f = 200 / 1200 = 0.1667$ s. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Quantities and Their Inter-Relationships

| Derived Quantity | Primary Inputs | Output | Common KTU Question Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Step angle $\theta_s$ | $N$, $P_h$ | Angular resolution | "Compute $\theta_s$ for a 4-phase motor with $N$ teeth." |
| Steps per revolution $N_s$ | $\theta_s$ | Total positions per turn | "How many pulses for one full revolution?" |
| Rotor position $\theta_{\text{rotor}}$ | $N_{\text{pulses}}$, $\theta_s$ | Cumulative angle | "Where is the shaft after $N$ pulses?" |
| Shaft speed $n$ | $f$, $N_s$ | RPM | "What is the RPM at frequency $f$?" |
| Pulse frequency $f$ | $n$, $N_s$ | Hz | "What clock frequency gives $n$ RPM?" |
| Time per revolution $T_{\text{rev}}$ | $f$, $N_s$ | Seconds | "How long for one revolution?" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Chain Mastery:**
> $f$ (Hz) $\longleftrightarrow$ $n$ (rpm) $\longleftrightarrow$ $\omega$ (rad/s) — these three are interchangeable. KTU questions will test *any* leg of this triangle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams below represent *block-level functional architecture* and *control-signal flow* for stepper motor systems, conforming to the KTU 2024 Module-2 pedagogical style. Physical cutaway drawings are not used; instead, *signal flow* and *sequencing logic* are emphasized — which is what KTU examiners test.

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Microcontroller-to-Stepper-Motor Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU["Microcontroller Unit 8051 / ARM Cortex"]
    PORTA["Port P1.0 to P1.3 GPIO Lines"]
    SEQ["Step Sequence Firmware Full Step / Half Step"]
    DRV["Driver IC ULN2003 or L293D"]
    COIL["Stepper Motor Coils Phase A B C D"]
    SHAFT["Mechanical Shaft Load / Coupling"]

    MCU --> PORTA
    PORTA --> SEQ
    SEQ --> DRV
    DRV --> COIL
    COIL --> SHAFT

    DRV -.->|"Current Amplification 4x or 8x"| COIL
    SEQ -.->|"Direction Control DIR pin"| DRV
```

**Interpretation:**
- The microcontroller runs a **step-sequence firmware** that toggles 4 GPIO lines in a precise order.
- The **driver IC** amplifies the low-current logic signals (mA) to the high-current levels (hundreds of mA to A) required by the motor coils.
- The **shaft** physically rotates by $\theta_s$ per step.

---

## 4.2 State Diagram — 4-Phase Full-Step Excitation Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State1
    State1 : Phase A ON, B OFF, C OFF, D OFF
    State2 : Phase A OFF, B ON, C OFF, D OFF
    State3 : Phase A OFF, B OFF, C ON, D OFF
    State4 : Phase A OFF, B OFF, C OFF, D ON

    State1 --> State2 : Pulse 1
    State2 --> State3 : Pulse 2
    State3 --> State4 : Pulse 3
    State4 --> State1 : Pulse 4
    State1 --> State4 : Reverse Pulse
    State4 --> State3 : Reverse Pulse
    State3 --> State2 : Reverse Pulse
    State2 --> State1 : Reverse Pulse
```

**Interpretation:** Each pulse advances the rotor by one step in the chosen direction. The reverse-direction loop is initiated when the **DIR** logic pin is toggled low in firmware.

---

## 4.3 Classification Flowchart — Types of Stepper Motors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SM["Stepper Motor"]
    SM --> VR["Variable Reluctance VR"]
    SM --> PM["Permanent Magnet PM"]
    SM --> HB["Hybrid Synchronous HB"]

    VR --> VR_PROP["Rotor Soft Iron No Detent Torque Coarse Step"]
    PM --> PM_PROP["Rotor Permanent Magnet High Detent Torque Coarse Step 45 or 90 deg"]
    HB --> HB_PROP["Rotor Magnet Plus Toothed Caps High Resolution 0.9 or 1.8 deg Industry Standard"]
```

**Interpretation:** The Hybrid type **dominates** modern industry because it offers the best trade-off between resolution and holding torque.

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Open-Loop Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OpenLoop["Open Loop Control Architecture No Encoder"]
        C1["Controller Pulse Generator"]
        D1["Driver IC"]
        M1["Stepper Motor"]
        L1["Mechanical Load"]
        C1 --> D1 --> M1 --> L1
    end

    subgraph ClosedLoop["Closed Loop Control Architecture With Encoder Feedback"]
        C2["Controller"]
        D2["Driver"]
        M2["Stepper or Servo Motor"]
        E2["Encoder / Resolver"]
        C2 --> D2 --> M2
        M2 --> L2["Load"]
        E2 -.->|"Position Feedback"| C2
    end
```

**Interpretation:** Stepper motors are *traditionally* open-loop, but high-end CNC applications add encoders to detect stall — this is the "closed-loop stepper" or "servo-stepper" hybrid. KTU 2024 typically tests the **open-loop** configuration.

---

## 4.5 Step Pulse Timing Diagram (Textual Representation)

```mermaid
gantt
    title 4-Phase Full-Step Wave Drive Sequence
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s

    section Phase A
    PhaseA ON :a1, 0, 1
    PhaseA OFF :a2, 1, 3

    section Phase B
    PhaseB OFF :b1, 0, 1
    PhaseB ON  :b2, 1, 1
    PhaseB OFF :b3, 2, 2

    section Phase C
    PhaseC OFF :c1, 0, 2
    PhaseC ON  :c2, 2, 1
    PhaseC OFF :c3, 3, 1

    section Phase D
    PhaseD OFF :d1, 0, 3
    PhaseD ON  :d2, 3, 1
```

**Interpretation:** In **wave-drive (1-phase-on)**, only one phase is energized at any instant. The phases are excited in a cyclic ABCD-ABCD pattern.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question is designed to be answered in **3–4 lines**, **one diagram allowed**, and is mapped to **CO2 / CO3** of the PBBRT404 syllabus under KTU 2024 scheme.

---

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a stepper motor. List any two advantages and one disadvantage of using a stepper motor in an embedded system.**

**Model Answer:**

> A stepper motor is a *brushless synchronous motor* that rotates in **discrete angular steps** in response to a train of electrical pulses applied to its stator windings. Each pulse advances the rotor by a fixed **step angle** $\theta_s$.
>
> **Advantages:**
> 1. **Open-loop position control** — no encoder/feedback required; position is known by pulse count.
> 2. **Excellent holding torque at standstill** — the motor locks magnetically at each step.
>
> **Disadvantage:**
> 1. **Resonance and loss of synchronism at high speeds** if pulse rate exceeds the motor's pull-out capability, leading to missed steps and positional error.

**Marks Distribution (3):**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Two advantages: 1 mark (0.5 each)
- One disadvantage: 1 mark

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Variable Reluctance and Permanent Magnet stepper motors. (Any 3 points)**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. | Parameter | Variable Reluctance (VR) | Permanent Magnet (PM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Rotor material** | Soft iron, no magnetization | Permanently magnetized (ferrite/NdFeB) |
| 2 | **Detent torque** | Negligible (rotor free to rotate when OFF) | Significant (rotor holds position when OFF) |
| 3 | **Step angle** | Typically small (5°–15°) | Larger (45° or 90°) |
| 4 | **Holding torque** | Lower | Higher |

**Marks Distribution (3):**
- 3 correct differentiating points × 1 mark each = 3 marks

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Long-Answer (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, every Part B question carries **internal choice**. The two alternatives below are designed so that either is independently answerable in 14 marks.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a microcontroller-based stepper motor control system. Describe the role of the **driver IC** and **sequencer firmware** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-phase hybrid stepper motor has **80 rotor teeth**. Calculate:
  - (i) The step angle $\theta_s$.
  - (ii) The number of steps required for the shaft to complete **3 full revolutions**.
  - (iii) The pulse frequency required to drive the motor at **240 RPM**.
  - (iv) The angular position of the shaft after **750 input pulses**, expressed as a position in the range $[0^{\circ}, 360^{\circ})$.
  **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

> A microcontroller-based stepper motor control system consists of the following functional blocks (as per the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1):
>
> 1. **Microcontroller (MCU):** Generates the timing and logic sequence. Typical chips used in KTU labs: **8051, AT89C51, ARM Cortex-M0**.
> 2. **Port Interface (GPIO):** Four output pins (e.g., P1.0–P1.3) feed the 4 phase lines of the motor.
> 3. **Step Sequencer Firmware:** Implements the excitation table as a **lookup array** indexed by a *step counter*. The array holds the 4-bit binary pattern for each of the 4 (or 8 for half-step) states.
> 4. **Driver IC (ULN2003 / L293D):** Performs **current amplification** and **back-EMF isolation**; the MCU GPIO pins cannot source the 200 mA–2 A coil currents directly.
> 5. **Stepper Motor:** Converts the energized phase pattern into mechanical rotation.
> 6. **Direction Control:** A dedicated DIR GPIO pin controls whether the sequencer counts up or down.
> 7. **Speed Control:** Achieved by varying the **delay between successive sequencer state updates** — a software delay or timer ISR (interrupt service routine) sets the effective pulse frequency $f$.
>
> **Role of Driver IC:** Provides *high-current drive* (Darlington pairs in ULN2003 or H-bridge in L293D) and *flyback diode protection* against inductive kickback from the motor coils.
>
> **Role of Sequencer Firmware:** Implements the **phase excitation table** in a cyclical manner; controls direction by reading or inverting the index increment; controls speed by adjusting the inter-step delay.

**Marks Distribution (7):**
- Block diagram with explanation: 3 marks
- Role of driver IC: 2 marks
- Role of sequencer firmware: 2 marks

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

> **Given:** $P_h = 4$ phases, $N = 80$ rotor teeth.
>
> **Part (i) — Step angle $\theta_s$:**
>
> $$\theta_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N \cdot P_h} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{80 \cdot 4} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{320} = 1.125^{\circ}$$
>
> **Part (ii) — Steps for 3 revolutions:**
>
> Steps per revolution: $N_s = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s} = \dfrac{360}{1.125} = 320$ steps/rev.
>
> Steps for 3 revolutions: $N_{\text{pulses}} = 3 \times 320 = \mathbf{960 \text{ pulses}}$.
>
> **Part (iii) — Pulse frequency for 240 RPM:**
>
> Using $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{N_s}$, solve for $f$:
>
> $$f = \frac{n \cdot N_s}{60} = \frac{240 \times 320}{60} = \frac{76800}{60} = 1280 \text{ Hz} = \mathbf{1.28 \text{ kHz}}$$
>
> **Part (iv) — Angular position after 750 pulses:**
>
> $$\theta_{\text{rotor}} = 750 \times 1.125^{\circ} = 843.75^{\circ}$$
>
> Reduce modulo $360^{\circ}$:
>
> $$843.75^{\circ} \div 360^{\circ} = 2 \text{ remainder } 123.75^{\circ}$$
>
> $$\theta_{\text{rotor, mod}} = 843.75^{\circ} - 2 \times 360^{\circ} = 843.75^{\circ} - 720^{\circ} = \mathbf{123.75^{\circ}}$$

**Marks Distribution (7):**
- (i) Step angle: 1.5 marks
- (ii) Steps for 3 revolutions: 1.5 marks
- (iii) Pulse frequency: 2 marks
- (iv) Angular position (with mod $360^{\circ}$): 2 marks

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **wave-drive**, **full-step drive**, and **half-step drive** excitation modes of a 4-phase stepper motor. Compare their **torque**, **resolution**, and **smoothness** using a suitable table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unipolar 5-wire stepper motor has a step angle of **$7.5^{\circ}$**. The motor is driven by a pulse train of **400 Hz**:
  - (i) Compute the **steps per revolution**.
  - (ii) Compute the **shaft speed in RPM** and in **rad/s**.
  - (iii) If the motor is used in **half-step mode**, what is the new step angle and the new shaft speed in RPM at the same pulse frequency?
  - (iv) The motor must drive a lead-screw of pitch **2 mm/rev**. How many pulses are needed to linearly translate the load by **30 mm**?
  **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

> **Wave-Drive (1-Phase-On):**
> Only one phase is energized at any instant. The phase-ON pattern cycles A → B → C → D → A… Each cycle produces **4 steps per electrical revolution**. Holding torque is lower because only one coil pulls the rotor at a time.
>
> **Full-Step Drive (2-Phase-On):**
> Two adjacent phases are energized simultaneously. Pattern: AB → BC → CD → DA → AB… This produces the same step angle as wave drive but with **~1.4× higher holding torque** because two coils constructively add their magnetic fields.
>
> **Half-Step Drive:**
> Alternates between wave and full-step states. Pattern: A → AB → B → BC → C → CD → D → DA → A… This yields **8 steps per electrical cycle**, halving the effective step angle and providing smoother rotation at the cost of variable intermediate torque.
>
> **Comparison Table:**
>
> | Mode | Phases ON | Step Angle | Holding Torque | Smoothness | Power Consumption |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Wave drive | 1 | $\theta_s$ | $0.707 \cdot T_{\max}$ | Poor | Lowest |
> | Full-step | 2 | $\theta_s$ | $T_{\max}$ | Moderate | Higher |
> | Half-step | 1 or 2 | $\theta_s / 2$ | Variable | Best | Highest |

**Marks Distribution (7):**
- Explanation of 3 modes: 3 marks
- Comparison table with torque/resolution/smoothness: 3 marks
- Working + neat diagram description: 1 mark

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

> **Given:** $\theta_s = 7.5^{\circ}$, $f = 400$ Hz.
>
> **Part (i) — Steps per revolution:**
>
> $$N_s = \frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta_s} = \frac{360}{7.5} = \mathbf{48 \text{ steps/rev}}$$
>
> **Part (ii) — Shaft speed:**
>
> $$n = \frac{60 \cdot f}{N_s} = \frac{60 \times 400}{48} = \frac{24000}{48} = \mathbf{500 \text{ rpm}}$$
>
> $$\omega = n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{60} = 500 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 52.36 \text{ rad/s}$$
>
> **Part (iii) — Half-step mode:**
>
> New step angle: $\theta_{s,\text{half}} = \theta_s / 2 = 7.5^{\circ} / 2 = \mathbf{3.75^{\circ}}$.
>
> New steps per revolution: $N_{s,\text{half}} = 360 / 3.75 = 96$ steps/rev.
>
> New shaft speed at same $f = 400$ Hz:
>
> $$n_{\text{half}} = \frac{60 \times 400}{96} = \frac{24000}{96} = \mathbf{250 \text{ rpm}}$$
>
> **Part (iv) — Pulses for 30 mm linear translation:**
>
> Lead-screw pitch: $L_p = 2$ mm/rev → 1 revolution = 2 mm linear travel.
>
> Revolutions needed: $N_{\text{rev}} = 30 \text{ mm} / 2 \text{ mm/rev} = 15$ revolutions.
>
> Pulses needed: $N_{\text{pulses}} = N_{\text{rev}} \times N_s = 15 \times 48 = \mathbf{720 \text{ pulses}}$.

**Marks Distribution (7):**
- (i) Steps per rev: 1 mark
- (ii) Speed in RPM and rad/s: 2 marks
- (iii) Half-step angle and speed: 2 marks
- (iv) Linear translation calculation: 2 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on stepper-motor questions (Decoded from Valuation Key patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the modulo-$360^{\circ}$ reduction** when the cumulative pulse count exceeds one revolution. Always reduce $\theta_{\text{rotor}}$ to the canonical range $[0^{\circ}, 360^{\circ})$.
> 2. **Mixing up VR vs. PM vs. HB** — remember the rotor material: *iron*, *magnet*, *magnet+teeth*.
> 3. **Using the wrong formula:** $N_s = 360/\theta_s$ for "total steps" but $\theta_s = 360/(N \cdot P_h)$ for *hybrid* — students often forget the $P_h$ factor.
> 4. **Unit errors in speed calculation:** $f$ is in **Hz** (pulses per second), but $n$ is in **RPM** — always multiply by 60 to convert seconds to minutes.
> 5. **Half-step mode in speed calculation:** when half-stepping, the effective $N_s$ *doubles*, so the speed at the same $f$ is **halved** — this is the most common conceptual trap in Part B.
> 6. **Not showing the conversion chain** (Hz → RPM → rad/s) — KTU examiners award a partial mark for the explicit chain.
> 7. **Skipping units in the final answer** — losing 0.5 to 1 mark for unit omission is shockingly common.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Stepper Motors (KTU 2024 Module 2):**
>
> - **Definition:** A *brushless synchronous motor* that rotates in **discrete fixed angular steps** $\theta_s$ in response to input pulses.
> - **Master Step Angle Equation (Hybrid):** $\theta_s = 360^{\circ} / (N \cdot P_h)$.
> - **Master Step Angle Equation (General):** $\theta_s = 360^{\circ} / N_s$ where $N_s$ is the manufacturer's stated steps/rev.
> - **Position Equation:** $\theta_{\text{rotor}} = N_{\text{pulses}} \cdot \theta_s$ (always reduce mod $360^{\circ}$ for final answer).
> - **Speed Equation:** $n = (60 \cdot f) / N_s$ [RPM]; equivalently $\omega = (2\pi f) / N_s$ [rad/s].
> - **Time per Revolution:** $T_{\text{rev}} = N_s / f$ [seconds].
> - **Three Types:** *Variable Reluctance* (iron rotor, no detent), *Permanent Magnet* (magnet rotor, high detent), *Hybrid* (magnet + toothed, industry standard).
> - **Drive Modes:** *Wave* (1-phase, low torque), *Full-step* (2-phase, high torque), *Half-step* (alternating, smoother, halved angle).
> - **Microstepping:** $\theta_{\mu} = \theta_s / N_{\mu}$, $N_{\mu} \in \{1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256\}$.
> - **Direction Control:** Achieved by *reversing the phase sequence* (ABCDA → ADCBA).
> - **Speed Control:** Achieved by *varying the inter-step delay* (or pulse frequency $f$).
> - **Holding Torque $T_h$:** Static torque motor can hold without losing step.
> - **Detent Torque $T_d$:** Residual torque when un-energized; significant in PM and HB, negligible in VR.
> - **Pull-out Torque $T_{po}$:** Maximum load torque at a given speed without losing synchronism.
> - **Driver ICs:** ULN2003 (unipolar, Darlington array), L293D (bipolar, H-bridge), DRV8825 (microstepping capable).
> - **Open-loop vs. Closed-loop:** Stepper motors are inherently *open-loop*; encoders may be added for *closed-loop servo-stepper* configurations.
> - **Resonance Phenomenon:** At certain pulse frequencies, the rotor may resonate and stall — modern drives use **microstepping** and **damping** to mitigate.
> - **Common KTU Example:** *NEMA 17, 1.8°, 200 steps/rev, 12 V, 1.5 A/phase* — the de-facto standard for academic and hobbyist 3D-printer projects.
> - **Open-loop position error bound:** $\pm \theta_s / 2$ (no cumulative error if motor does not stall).
> - **Key Rule of Thumb:** Higher step resolution → smoother motion → less resonance → lower per-step torque at high microstep counts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Keyboard Interfacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBBRT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Keyboard Interfacing # MODULE 2 — KEYBOARD INTERFACING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > **Keyboard Interfacing** in embedded systems refers to the technique of connecting a matrix-organized set of mechanical key switches (push buttons) to a microcontroller so that a large number of keys can be efficiently read through a limited number of I/O port pins. The technique relies on a *row–column scanning algorithm* combined with *debouncing* logic to convert a physical key-press event into a stable, unique digital code. ### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **chessboard**. Instead of giving each square its own dedicated wire back to the controller, we only wire the **rows** and the **columns** of the board. To find which square is "pressed," we energize one row at a time and see which column "responds." That crossing point uniquely identifies the key. The same trick is used in keyboards, memory arrays, and even digital number pads on microwave ovens. - A **linear keyboard** is one where every key has its own wire — like a row of 16 separate doorbells, each with its own bell wire. Practical only for 1–8 keys. - A **matrix keyboard** is the chessboard arrangement — $4 \times 4 = 16$ keys needing only **8 pins** instead of 16. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric for KTU Examinations:** The most commonly tested configuration is the **$4 \times 4$ matrix** yielding **16 keys** using **8 GPIO lines** (typically Port 1 of the 8051 or any 8-bit port of an ARM/LPC2148). ### 1.2 Why Matrix Keyboards? | Number of Keys | Linear Pins Needed | Matrix Pins ($R+C$) | Savings | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 4 | 4 | — | None | | 8 | 8 | — | None | | **16** | **16** | **8 (4+4)** | **50 %** | | 25 | 25 | 10 (5+5) | 60 % | | 64 | 64 | 16 (8+8) | 75 % | > [!TIP] > **Key Insight:** A matrix of $R$ rows and $C$ columns accommodates $R \times C$ keys using only $R+C$ port lines. The savings grow quadratically. ### 1.3 Physical Construction Each intersection of a row and a column contains a **SPST momentary push-to-make switch**. When no key is pressed, the column lines are held HIGH by **pull-up resistors** (typical value **10 kΩ**). When a key is pressed, the corresponding row–column junction is shorted, allowing the column to be pulled to the row's logic level. ``` Row ──────┬───────┬───────┬─────── (driven OUTPUT by MCU) │ │ │ [K1] [K2] [K3] (SPST momentary switches) │ │ │ Col ──────┴───────┴───────┴─────── (read INPUT by MCU, pulled up) ``` ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hardware Architecture A matrix keyboard has two sets of lines: - **Row lines** (outputs from the microcontroller) — driven LOW or HIGH by the controller. - **Column lines** (inputs to the microcontroller) — read by the controller, normally pulled HIGH through **pull-up resistors** ($R_{PU} \approx 10 \text{ k}\Omega$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Standard:** A $4 \times 4$ matrix is the textbook reference. Rows are connected to the lower nibble (P1.0–P1.3) and columns to the upper nibble (P1.4–P1.7) of Port 1 of the 8051. ### 2.2 The Two Fundamental Scanning Algorithms #### Algorithm A: **Row Scanning with "All-Rows-Low" (Ground-Based)** **Logic flow:** 1. Initialize: Drive **all rows LOW** and read all columns. 2. Check if **any key is pressed**: if **all columns are HIGH**, no key is pressed — wait and loop. 3. The moment **any column reads LOW**, a key press is detected (debounce delay ≈ **20 ms**). 4. Identify the row: Drive one row at a time LOW (others HIGH). The column that still reads LOW identifies the active key. 5. Wait for **key release** (all columns HIGH again) before returning to step 1. #### Algorithm B: **"Make All Bits High" (Active-High Pull-Down)** 1. Initialize: Drive **all rows HIGH** and read all columns (which rest at LOW via pull-downs). 2. Any column going HIGH indicates a key press. 3. Drive rows one-by-one HIGH; the column that reads HIGH identifies the key. ### 2.3 Debouncing — The Hidden Complexity Mechanical switches exhibit **contact bounce** for typically **5 – 20 ms** after actuation. Without debouncing, a single key press is interpreted as 2–10 rapid presses. **Two approaches:** | Method | Mechanism | Cost | Reliability | | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | | **Hardware** | RC filter (10 kΩ + 0.1 µF) + Schmitt trigger (74LS14) | Extra components | Excellent | | **Software** | Insert 20–30 ms delay after first detection | Zero hardware | Good (sufficient for KTU) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget the *second* debounce delay after key release. Always re-check after a 20 ms wait that the key is *still* pressed before accepting it as a valid input. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units / Notes | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | Total keys in matrix | $N = R \times C$ | dimensionless | | Required I/O lines | $L = R + C$ | GPIO pins | | Pin savings vs linear | $S = N - L = RC - R - C = (R-1)(C-1)$ | dimensionless | | Debounce time (software) | $t_d = 20 \text{ to } 30$ | ms | | Pull-up resistance | $R_{PU} \approx 10$ | kΩ | | Typical key contact resistance (closed) | $R_c \le 50$ | mΩ | | Typical key isolation resistance (open) | $R_o \ge 100$ | MΩ | | Bounce settle time (mechanical) | $t_b \le 5$ | ms | | Max scan cycle time (worst case) | $T_{scan} = R \cdot t_d$ | ms | | Lookup table size | $2^{R+C}$ codes decoded to $RC$ keys | — | > [!NOTE] > **No vertical pipes (`\vert x \vert`)** are used in this table. All absolute-value references are deliberately omitted to preserve markdown parsing. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Microwave ovens / washing machines** — cheap $4 \times 4$ numeric keypads. - **Industrial PLC panels** — HMI keyboards using $8 \times 8$ matrices with diode isolation to prevent *ghosting*. - **ATM keypads** — heavily secured with hardware encryption, but the scanning front-end is still matrix-based. - **PC keyboards (PS/2, USB)** — internally an $8 \times 16$ or larger matrix scanned by a dedicated controller IC (e.g., the Intel 8048 in classic keyboards). - **Mobile phones (pre-touchscreen)** — $4 \times 3$ or $4 \times 4$ matrices. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Pin Savings of a Matrix over Linear Configuration We start with a single key requiring one dedicated I/O line. For $N$ keys, a linear connection requires $N$ lines. A matrix connection uses $R$ row lines and $C$ column lines. **Step 1 — Define the variables.** Let $N$ be the total number of keys, $R$ the number of rows, and $C$ the number of columns such that $N = R \times C$. **Step 2 — Write the linear pin count.** The number of I/O pins for a linear (one-pin-per-key) connection is simply: $$ L_{linear} = N = R \times C $$ **Step 3 — Write the matrix pin count.** The number of I/O pins for the matrix connection is: $$ L_{matrix} = R + C $$ **Step 4 — Compute the absolute savings.** $$ \Delta L = L_{linear} - L_{matrix} = RC - R - C $$ **Step 5 — Factor the expression for insight.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta L &= RC - R - C + 1 - 1 \\ &= (R-1)(C-1) - 1 \end{aligned} $$ This shows the savings depend on the product $(R-1)(C-1)$. **Step 6 — Compute the relative savings (efficiency metric).** $$ \eta = \frac{\Delta L}{L_{linear}} = \frac{(R-1)(C-1)-1}{RC} $$ **Step 7 — Evaluate for the canonical $4 \times 4$ case.** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{linear} &= 4 \times 4 = 16 \\ L_{matrix} &= 4 + 4 = 8 \\ \Delta L &= 16 - 8 = 8 \\ \eta &= \frac{8}{16} = 0.5 = 50\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — Evaluate for the $4 \times 3$ numeric keypad (common in phones).** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{linear} &= 4 \times 3 = 12 \\ L_{matrix} &= 4 + 3 = 7 \\ \Delta L &= 12 - 7 = 5 \\ \eta &= \frac{5}{12} \approx 41.67\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 9 — Show the asymptotic behaviour as $R = C = n \to \infty$.** $$ \eta = \frac{(n-1)^2 - 1}{n^2} = \frac{n^2 - 2n}{n^2} = 1 - \frac{2}{n} $$ As $n \to \infty$, $\eta \to 1$, i.e., **the matrix approach approaches 100 % pin savings** for very large keypads. ### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Debounce Delay from RC Time Constant (Hardware Perspective) Even when a software debounce is used, the underlying physics of contact bounce is governed by the parasitic RC of the switch and the trace. **Step 1 — Mechanical contact model.** A bouncing switch behaves like an RC circuit where the contact capacitance discharges through the contact resistance during the brief "open" phase of a bounce. **Step 2 — Define the bounce time constant.** $$ \tau = R_c C_{contact} $$ **Step 3 — Settle time (bounce cessation) is typically 5 time constants.** $$ t_{settle} = 5 \tau = 5 R_c C_{contact} $$ **Step 4 — Use typical values.** $R_c \approx 50 \text{ m}\Omega$ and $C_{contact} \approx 1 \text{ nF}$ gives: $$ t_{settle} = 5 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-9} = 250 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 5 — Note the discrepancy.** Mechanical settling is microseconds, but **chatter of the armature** lasts **5 – 20 ms**. The 20 ms software debounce dominates to cover mechanical inertia, not electrical bounce alone. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Trace for a $4 \times 4$ Keypad Assume P1.0–P1.3 are rows, P1.4–P1.7 are columns. The lookup table maps the detected (row, column) pair to the ASCII / numeric code. | Step | Rows Driven (P1.3–P1.0) | Cols Read (P1.7–P1.4) | Detected Key | ASCII Code | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | | 1 | 0000 (all LOW) | wait until any bit = 0 | — | — | | 2 | 1110 (Row0 LOW) | P1.7 P1.6 P1.5 P1.4 | if Col0 LOW → K0 | '0' | | 2 | 1110 (Row0 LOW) | any other col LOW | K1, K2, K3 | '1','2','3' | | 3 | 1101 (Row1 LOW) | Col 0..3 LOW | K4..K7 | '4'..'7' | | 4 | 1011 (Row2 LOW) | Col 0..3 LOW | K8..K11 | '8'..'B' | | 5 | 0111 (Row3 LOW) | Col 0..3 LOW | K12..K15 | 'C'..'F' | | 6 | 0000 | 1111 (all HIGH) | release detected | exit | > [!TIP] > **Row pattern rule:** Exactly one row bit is LOW at a time; the other three are HIGH. This avoids *ghosting* (phantom key detection) when two or more keys in the same row are pressed. ### 3.4 Ghosting and Masking — An Important Caveat When **three keys forming an L-shape** are pressed simultaneously, the matrix can falsely indicate a fourth key (the corner of the L). This is the *ghosting* effect. Solutions include: - Adding a **diode in series with each key** (PCB-cost increase). - Using a *pin-diode* matrix controller IC. - Implementing *N-key rollover* (NKRO) firmware. > [!NOTE] > KTU does not typically demand diode-based anti-ghosting derivations, but stating the limitation earns bonus marks in design questions. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["MCLR Reset"] --> B["Initialize Port 1 as 8-bit I/O"] B --> C["Set all rows LOW, all columns as INPUT"] C --> D{"Any Column LOW?"} D -- "No" --> C D -- "Yes" --> E["20 ms Debounce Delay"] E --> F{"Key Still Pressed?"} F -- "No" --> C F -- "Yes" --> G["Scan Row 0 LOW, others HIGH"] G --> H{"Which Column LOW?"} H -- "Col0" --> I["Key = Row0,Col0"] H -- "Col1" --> J["Key = Row0,Col1"] H -- "Col2" --> K["Key = Row0,Col2"] H -- "Col3" --> L["Key = Row0,Col3"] I --> M["Lookup ASCII in Table"] J --> M K --> M L --> M M --> N["Wait for Key Release (All Cols HIGH)"] N --> O["20 ms Debounce Delay"] O --> P["Return to Main Loop"] P --> C G -- "No match" --> Q["Scan Row 1 LOW"] Q --> R["Scan Row 2 LOW"] R --> S["Scan Row 3 LOW"] S -- "No match" --> C ``` ### 4.2 Matrix Interconnection Schematic (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU["8051 / ARM MCU
Port P1"] --> R0["Row 0
P1.0"] MCU --> R1["Row 1
P1.1"] MCU --> R2["Row 2
P1.2"] MCU --> R3["Row 3
P1.3"] R0 --> K0["SW 0"] R0 --> K1["SW 1"] R0 --> K2["SW 2"] R0 --> K3["SW 3"] R1 --> K4["SW 4"] R1 --> K5["SW 5"] R1 --> K6["SW 6"] R1 --> K7["SW 7"] R2 --> K8["SW 8"] R2 --> K9["SW 9"] R2 --> KA["SW A"] R2 --> KB["SW B"] R3 --> KC["SW C"] R3 --> KD["SW D"] R3 --> KE["SW E"] R3 --> KF["SW F"] K0 --> C0["Col 0
P1.4"] K4 --> C0 K8 --> C0 KC --> C0 K1 --> C1["Col 1
P1.5"] K5 --> C1 K9 --> C1 KD --> C1 K2 --> C2["Col 2
P1.6"] K6 --> C2 KA --> C2 KE --> C2 K3 --> C3["Col 3
P1.7"] K7 --> C3 KB --> C3 KF --> C3 C0 --> PU0["10 kohm Pull-up to Vcc"] C1 --> PU1["10 kohm Pull-up to Vcc"] C2 --> PU2["10 kohm Pull-up to Vcc"] C3 --> PU3["10 kohm Pull-up to Vcc"] ``` ### 4.3 Debounce Filter (Hardware RC + Schmitt Trigger) ```mermaid flowchart LR SW["Mechanical Key"] --> RC["RC Low-Pass
10 kohm + 0.1 uF"] RC --> ST["Schmitt Trigger
74LS14"] ST --> MCU["MCU Input Pin"] VCC["Vcc 5V"] --> R["10 kohm Pull-up"] R --> MCU ``` > [!NOTE] > The **time constant** of the RC filter is $\tau = RC = 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \text{ ms}$. After ~5 time constants (5 ms), the input is guaranteed clean — comfortably faster than the typical 20 ms software debounce. ### 4.4 Algorithm State Diagram — Firmware Perspective ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> IDLE IDLE --> DETECT : Column goes LOW DETECT --> DEBOUNCE1 : 20 ms wait DEBOUNCE1 --> IDLE : Bounce ended (col HIGH) DEBOUNCE1 --> SCANROW0 : Key still pressed SCANROW0 --> SCANROW1 : Row0 not the source SCANROW1 --> SCANROW2 : Row1 not the source SCANROW2 --> SCANROW3 : Row2 not the source SCANROW3 --> SCANROW0 : Loop safeguard SCANROW0 --> ENCODE : Match found at Row0 SCANROW1 --> ENCODE : Match found at Row1 SCANROW2 --> ENCODE : Match found at Row2 SCANROW3 --> ENCODE : Match found at Row3 ENCODE --> WAITRELEASE : Save code WAITRELEASE --> DEBOUNCE2 : All columns HIGH DEBOUNCE2 --> IDLE : Stable release ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]** > **Q:** *Differentiate between a linear keyboard and a matrix keyboard. In which case is a matrix keyboard preferred?* > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A **linear keyboard** dedicates one I/O pin to every key; for $N$ keys it needs $N$ lines. A **matrix keyboard** arranges keys in a row–column grid; for $R \times C$ keys it needs only $R + C$ lines. A matrix is preferred when the number of keys exceeds 8, because the pin saving $(R-1)(C-1)$ grows quadratically. For example, a $4 \times 4$ keypad needs **8 pins** versus 16 in linear mode — a **50 % saving**. **(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]** > **Q:** *What is contact bounce? How is it handled in a matrix keyboard?* > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Contact bounce** is the rapid mechanical opening and closing of switch contacts for 5–20 ms after actuation. If not handled, the MCU sees multiple transitions for a single press. It is handled by **(i) hardware debounce** (RC filter $\tau \approx 1 \text{ ms}$ followed by a Schmitt trigger like 74LS14) and **(ii) software debounce** (insert a 20 ms delay after first detection and re-verify). **(3 marks: 1 + 2)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Adapted]` > **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **interface of a $4 \times 4$ matrix keyboard to the 8051 microcontroller**. Describe the **row scanning algorithm** in detail. **Model Solution:** 1. **Hardware Interface [3 marks]** - Connect the **4 row lines** of the keypad to the lower nibble of Port 1 (P1.0–P1.3) configured as **OUTPUT**. - Connect the **4 column lines** to the upper nibble of Port 1 (P1.4–P1.7) configured as **INPUT**. - Each column is pulled HIGH through a **10 kΩ pull-up resistor** to Vcc. - Pressing a key shorts one row to one column. 2. **Algorithm Steps [4 marks]** - **Step 1:** Set P1 = `0x0F` (all rows LOW, all columns HIGH read). - **Step 2:** Mask the upper nibble; if all four column bits are 1, no key is pressed — loop to Step 1. - **Step 3:** If any column is 0, call a **20 ms delay** for debounce. - **Step 4:** Re-check. If still pressed, proceed. - **Step 5:** Drive rows sequentially LOW: P1 = `0x0E`, then `0x0D`, then `0x0B`, then `0x07`. For each pattern, identify the column that is LOW. The (row, column) pair uniquely identifies the key. - **Step 6:** Encode the key using a lookup table. - **Step 7:** Wait for all columns to return HIGH (key release), debounce again, and return to Step 1. > **(b) [7 marks]** Explain **contact bounce** and describe both **hardware and software methods** of debouncing with suitable diagrams. **Model Solution:** 1. **Contact Bounce Definition [2 marks]** — Mechanical switches exhibit multiple rapid open–close cycles (bounce) lasting 5–20 ms due to spring inertia of the contact armature. Without suppression, a single key press registers as several presses. 2. **Hardware Debounce [3 marks]** — - Place an **RC low-pass filter** ($R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1 \mu\text{F}$) at the switch output, giving $\tau = 1 \text{ ms}$. - Follow it with a **Schmitt trigger** (74LS14) which snaps the slow analog edge into a clean digital level. - Settling time $5\tau = 5 \text{ ms}$ is sufficient to suppress the bounce. 3. **Software Debounce [2 marks]** — - On first detection, **wait 20 ms** then re-read the input. - If the key is *still* pressed, accept it. - On release, **wait another 20 ms** to confirm the release was real (anti-multiple-trigger). - Trade-off: 20 ms per detected press; acceptable for human-operated keypads. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]` > **(a) [7 marks]** Draw the block diagram of an **$8 \times 8$ matrix keyboard interfaced to an 8051** and explain the **scanning technique** with a flowchart. **Model Solution:** 1. **Hardware [2 marks]** — 8 rows connected to Port 1 (P1.0–P1.7) and 8 columns to Port 3 (P3.0–P3.7). All columns pulled HIGH via 10 kΩ resistors to Vcc. The 8051 requires **16 GPIO pins**, which is feasible across two ports. 2. **Scanning Technique [3 marks]** — - **Phase 1 — Detect press:** Drive all rows LOW. Read all 8 columns. If every column is HIGH, no key — wait and loop. - **Phase 2 — Debounce:** 20 ms delay; re-verify. - **Phase 3 — Identify row:** Sequentially drive each row LOW while the other 7 remain HIGH. Test columns each time. The (row, column) intersection that goes LOW is the pressed key. - **Phase 4 — Encode & release:** Look up the ASCII code; wait for the key release (all columns HIGH) with a second 20 ms debounce. 3. **Flowchart [2 marks]** — A textual flowchart: `Init → Check Any Key? → Debounce → Scan Rows 0–7 → Read Columns → Lookup Code → Wait for Release → Debounce → Loop`. > **(b) [7 marks]** Compare **hardware** and **software** debouncing. Why is **software debouncing preferred in KTU lab examinations**? **Model Solution:** | Aspect | Hardware Debounce | Software Debounce | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Components | RC + Schmitt trigger | None | | Cost | Higher | Zero | | CPU load | None | Negligible (20 ms wait) | | Reliability | Excellent | Very good | | Design flexibility | Fixed at design time | Adjustable in firmware | | **Lab suitability** | Less convenient (extra wiring) | **Highly preferred** | **Why software is preferred in KTU labs [2 marks]:** - No additional components are needed on the trainer kit, simplifying the wiring diagram. - The 8051 trainer kits at KTU-approved labs (e.g., NXP/Freescale, Vi Microsystems) expose Port 1 directly to the keypad, and students can implement debounce entirely in assembly/C. - Easier to demonstrate and explain during the **lab viva voce**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > - **Forgetting the "wait for release" step (–2 marks):** A complete answer must include a release-and-debounce loop; otherwise the system re-fires on the same key. > - **Confusing rows with columns (–1 mark):** The KTU standard is "rows are OUTPUT, columns are INPUT (with pull-up)." Reversing this is a common error. > - **Not justifying the choice of 20 ms (–1 mark):** Always quote the range "5 – 20 ms" with **20 ms** as a safe upper bound. > - **Ignoring the *ghosting* limitation (–1 mark in design questions):** Mention the L-shaped key-press ghosting phenomenon and the diode mitigation. > - **Wrong formula for pin savings (–2 marks):** Correct formula is $(R-1)(C-1)$, not $RC - 1$ or similar. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **matrix keyboard** uses $R+C$ pins for $R \times C$ keys — pin saving $= (R-1)(C-1)$. - **Standard KTU configuration** = $4 \times 4$ matrix on 8051 Port 1. - **Row scanning algorithm** drives rows LOW one at a time; columns (with 10 kΩ pull-ups) are read. - **Debounce** — **20 ms** is the canonical software wait; hardware uses RC + Schmitt trigger. - **Algorithm sequence** — *Detect → Debounce → Identify row → Identify column → Encode → Wait for release → Debounce → Loop*. - **Ghosting** occurs with 3-key L-shape press; mitigated with **per-key diodes**. - **Pull-up resistors** ($R_{PU} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$) are essential to keep idle columns at a defined logic HIGH. - **Rows are OUTPUTS; columns are INPUTS** — remember this polarity inversion. - The **lookup table** maps the detected (row, column) pair to the desired ASCII / numeric code. - KTU evaluation favours **flowcharts** in Part B — always include one. - For **ARM/LPC2148** variants, replace Port 1 with **IO0Pin / IO1Pin** of the LPC2148, but the algorithm remains identical. - In **PS/2 and USB keyboards**, the matrix is internal and a controller IC performs the scan; the host sees only the scan code. > [!NOTE] > **Final Revision Hook:** If you can draw the flowchart in Section 4.4 from memory and recite the *pin-saving formula* $(R-1)(C-1)$, you are **fully prepared** for any 3-mark or 14-mark KTU question on Keyboard Interfacing.

topics.title: 7 Segment LED (Embedded C Programming)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 7 Segment LED (Embedded C Programming)

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Seven-Segment Display (SSD)** is an electronic display device consisting of **seven Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs)** arranged in the shape of the numeral **"8"**, combined with an optional eighth segment — the **decimal point (dp)**. Each individual LED is referred to as a *segment* and is conventionally labelled **a, b, c, d, e, f, g**, plus **dp**, following a standard layout defined by the electronics industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBBRT404 Module 2):**
> A Seven-Segment LED is a display device that uses a combination of seven LEDs in a specific arrangement (segments a–g) to represent decimal digits 0–9 (and limited hexadecimal letters A–F). It is interfaced to a microcontroller either through current-limiting resistors directly (segment-by-segment driving) or via dedicated decoder/driver ICs (e.g., **BCD-to-7-segment decoder 7447/7448**). For multi-digit displays, the **time-multiplexing technique** is employed in which only one digit is activated at a time, but the human eye perceives all digits as glowing simultaneously due to **persistence of vision (≈ 1/16th of a second)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **digital clock at a railway station**. Each digit (0–9) you see glowing is not actually a single piece of shaped glass — it is a clever arrangement of **seven small straight line lights (LEDs)** that switch on/off in specific patterns to "draw" the number. The microcontroller is like a **choreographer**, deciding *which* of the seven line-lights should glow and *for how long*.

- If you want to display **"3"**, the choreographer turns ON segments **a, b, c, d, g** and switches OFF **e, f**.
- If you want to display **"7"**, only **a, b, c** glow.

For multi-digit displays (e.g., showing "1234" on four SSDs), instead of using 4 × 7 = 28 separate wires, the choreographer uses a clever trick: it shows "1" on the first display for a tiny fraction of a second, then "2" on the second, then "3" on the third, then "4" on the fourth — and repeats this cycle so fast (**typically every 1–5 ms per digit**) that your **eye cannot detect the switching** and you see all four digits glowing at once. This trick is called **multiplexing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Persistence of Vision (POV):** The human eye retains an image for approximately **1/16th of a second (≈ 62.5 ms)** after the stimulus is removed. Any visual change occurring faster than this threshold appears continuous. This is the fundamental principle behind 7-segment multiplexing.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values are used throughout KTU board problems:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Symbol/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Forward voltage drop per LED segment | **1.8 V – 2.2 V** (Red), **3.0 V – 3.6 V** (Blue/White) | $V_F$ |
| Typical segment current | **10 mA – 20 mA** | $I_F$ |
| Refresh rate (multiplexing) | **> 50 Hz** (typically 60–100 Hz) | $f_{refresh}$ |
| Time per digit in multiplexed display | **1 ms – 5 ms** | $t_{digit}$ |
| Persistence of vision threshold | **≈ 62.5 ms** | $t_{POV}$ |
| Decimal digits displayable | **0 – 9** (with hex: A–F) | — |
| Total segments | **7 + 1 (dp)** | — |

## 1.4 Common Anode vs Common Cathode — The Two Flavors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**

| Feature | Common Anode (CA) | Common Cathode (CC) |
|---|---|---|
| Common terminal | Connected to **Vcc (+5 V)** | Connected to **GND (0 V)** |
| Segment ON condition | Apply **LOW (0)** to segment pin | Apply **HIGH (1)** to segment pin |
| Segment OFF condition | Apply **HIGH (1)** to segment pin | Apply **LOW (0)** to segment pin |
| Current flow direction | Vcc → LED → Resistor → MCU pin | MCU pin → Resistor → LED → GND |
| Driver IC (active LOW output) | **7447** (BCD-to-7-seg, active LOW) | **7448** (BCD-to-7-seg, active HIGH) |
| Hex code for "0" | **0xC0** (or 0x40 with dp) | **0x3F** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Segment Arrangement and Pin Identification

A standard 7-segment display has the segments arranged as:

```
    ---- a ----
   |           |
   f           b
   |           |
    ---- g ----
   |           |
   e           c
   |           |
    ---- d ----   .  dp
```

- Segments **a, b, c, d, e, f** form the outer hexagonal boundary.
- Segment **g** is the middle horizontal bar.
- Segment **dp** is the decimal point at the bottom-right.

The segments are typically mapped to microcontroller port bits as:

| Segment | a | b | c | d | e | f | g | dp |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bit position (typical) | P2.0 | P2.1 | P2.2 | P2.3 | P2.4 | P2.5 | P2.6 | P2.7 |
| Bit weight (8-bit) | bit 0 | bit 1 | bit 2 | bit 3 | bit 4 | bit 5 | bit 6 | bit 7 |

## 2.2 Logical Steps to Derive the Hex Code

The hex code for any digit is derived by treating the segment pattern as an **8-bit binary word** where each bit corresponds to one segment:

- **Bit = 0** (Common Anode) or **Bit = 1** (Common Cathode) means the segment is **OFF**.
- **Bit = 1** (Common Anode) or **Bit = 0** (Common Cathode) means the segment is **ON**.

**Step-by-step procedure:**
1. Identify which segments must glow to form the digit.
2. Assign each segment to its corresponding bit position.
3. Set each bit according to the **active level** (active HIGH for CC, active LOW for CA).
4. Convert the resulting 8-bit binary word to **hexadecimal**.

> [!NOTE]
> The bit ordering convention in KTU textbooks and Embedded C code uses the pattern:
> **bit 7 = dp**, **bit 6 = g**, **bit 5 = f**, **bit 4 = e**, **bit 3 = d**, **bit 2 = c**, **bit 1 = b**, **bit 0 = a**
> (i.e., MSB → dp, LSB → a). This is the **MSB-first / dp-a** convention.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### 2.3.1 Hex Code Lookup Table (Most Critical — Memorize for KTU)

| Digit | Segments ON (a b c d e f g) | CC Hex Code (Active HIGH) | CA Hex Code (Active LOW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | a, b, c, d, e, f | **0x3F** | **0xC0** |
| **1** | b, c | **0x06** | **0xF9** |
| **2** | a, b, d, e, g | **0x5B** | **0xA4** |
| **3** | a, b, c, d, g | **0x4F** | **0xB0** |
| **4** | b, c, f, g | **0x66** | **0x99** |
| **5** | a, c, d, f, g | **0x6D** | **0x92** |
| **6** | a, c, d, e, f, g | **0x7D** | **0x82** |
| **7** | a, b, c | **0x07** | **0xF8** |
| **8** | a, b, c, d, e, f, g | **0x7F** | **0x80** |
| **9** | a, b, c, d, f, g | **0x6F** | **0x90** |
| **A** | a, b, c, e, f, g | **0x77** | **0x88** |
| **b** | c, d, e, f, g | **0x7C** | **0x83** |
| **C** | a, d, e, f | **0x39** | **0xC6** |
| **d** | b, c, d, e, g | **0x5E** | **0xA1** |
| **E** | a, d, e, f, g | **0x79** | **0x86** |
| **F** | a, e, f, g | **0x71** | **0x8E** |
| **Blank** | none | **0x00** | **0xFF** |

### 2.3.2 Hardware Design Formulas

| Formula | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Current-limiting resistor | $R = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}$ | Resistor in series with each segment |
| Multiplexed refresh frequency | $f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot t_{digit}}$ | $N$ = number of digits, $t_{digit}$ = time per digit |
| Total scan cycle time | $T_{scan} = N \cdot t_{digit}$ | Must be **less than 16.67 ms** (1/60 Hz) |
| Minimum time per digit | $t_{digit} \le \dfrac{T_{scan}}{N}$ | For flicker-free display |
| Power consumption (per segment) | $P_{seg} = V_F \cdot I_F$ | Typically **20 mW** per segment |
| Total display power | $P_{total} = 7 \cdot V_F \cdot I_F$ (per digit) | — |

### 2.3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Resistor Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Problem (Frequently Asked):**
> *A 7-segment common-anode display is interfaced to a 5 V microcontroller. Each LED segment has a forward voltage of 2.0 V and must be driven at 15 mA. Calculate the current-limiting resistor value.*

$$
R = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} = \dfrac{5 - 2.0}{15 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.015} = 200 \ \Omega
$$

**Standard KTU-approved value: 220 Ω (nearest E12 series).**

## 2.4 Why Multiplexing? — The Engineering Rationale

If a **4-digit display** were driven directly (non-multiplexed), it would require:
- **4 × 8 = 32 GPIO pins** for segments + digit selection, OR
- **28 segment pins + 4 common pins = 32 pins** total.

With **multiplexing**, you need only:
- **8 segment lines** (shared by all digits) + **4 digit-select lines** = **12 pins total**.

This is a **62.5% reduction in pin count**, which is critical for resource-constrained microcontrollers like the 8051. Additionally, multiplexing reduces **average current consumption** because only one digit is lit at any instant (peak current per digit can be increased to maintain brightness without exceeding average power budget — a technique called **pulse-width / peak-current compensation**).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> 7-segment displays are ubiquitous in:
> - **Digital clocks and watches**
> - **Microwave ovens, washing machines, and AC units** (appliance user interfaces)
> - **Fuel station price displays**
> - **Industrial PLC HMI panels**
> - **Elevator floor indicators**
> - **Multimeters and weighing scales**
> - **Vehicle speedometers and odometers**
> - **Vending machines and public information displays**

Despite the rise of **LCD** and **OLED** displays, 7-segment LEDs remain dominant where:
- High brightness under sunlight is needed.
- Wide viewing angles are essential.
- Long operational life (> 100,000 hours) is required.
- Low cost and simple microcontroller interfacing are priorities.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Hex Code for Digit "2" on Common-Cathode Display

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detailed Worked Derivation — Demonstrates the full logical process for KTU full-mark answers.**

### Step 1: Identify which segments must glow

To display the digit **"2"**, the following segments must be **ON**:
- **a** (top horizontal)
- **b** (upper-right vertical)
- **d** (bottom horizontal)
- **e** (lower-left vertical)
- **g** (middle horizontal)

Segments that must be **OFF**:
- **c** (lower-right vertical)
- **f** (upper-left vertical)
- **dp** (decimal point)

### Step 2: Assign bits using MSB-first / dp-a convention

Using the convention `bit 7 = dp`, `bit 6 = g`, `bit 5 = f`, `bit 4 = e`, `bit 3 = d`, `bit 2 = c`, `bit 1 = b`, `bit 0 = a`:

| Segment | a | b | c | d | e | f | g | dp |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bit position | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| Required state | ON | ON | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | ON | OFF |
| Logic level (CC) | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |

### Step 3: Construct the 8-bit binary word

$$
\text{Binary} = \underbrace{0}_{dp}\,\underbrace{1}_{g}\,\underbrace{0}_{f}\,\underbrace{1}_{e}\,\underbrace{1}_{d}\,\underbrace{0}_{c}\,\underbrace{1}_{b}\,\underbrace{1}_{a} = 01011011_2
$$

### Step 4: Convert binary 01011011 to hexadecimal

$$
\begin{aligned}
0101\,1011_2 &= 0101_2 \, \Big\vert \, 1011_2 \\[4pt]
0101_2 &= 0 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 = 4 + 1 = 5 \\[4pt]
1011_2 &= 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 = 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11 = B_{16} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad 01011011_2 &= 0x5B
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Verification against the lookup table

From the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.3.1), the CC hex code for "2" is listed as **0x5B**. ✓ **Match confirmed.**

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Hex Code for "9" on Common-Anode Display

### Step 1: Identify segments

Digit **"9"** requires ON segments: **a, b, c, d, f, g**.
OFF segments: **e, dp**.

### Step 2: Assign bits (CA — active LOW means 0 = ON, 1 = OFF)

| Segment | a | b | c | d | e | f | g | dp |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Required state | ON | ON | ON | ON | OFF | ON | ON | OFF |
| Logic level (CA) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

### Step 3: Construct binary word

$$
\text{Binary} = 1\,0\,0\,1\,0\,0\,0\,0 = 10010000_2
$$

### Step 4: Convert to hex

$$
\begin{aligned}
1001_2 &= 9_{16} \\
0000_2 &= 0_{16} \\
\therefore \quad 10010000_2 &= 0x90
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the CA hex code in the lookup table for "9" — **0x90**. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Multiplexing Time Calculation for 4-Digit Display

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Numerical Problem Type:**
> *Design a 4-digit multiplexed 7-segment display system using an 8051. The display must be flicker-free. If the refresh frequency must be at least 60 Hz, calculate the maximum time that can be allocated to each digit.*

### Step 1: Identify the parameters

- Number of digits: $N = 4$
- Minimum refresh frequency: $f_{refresh} = 60$ Hz

### Step 2: Calculate total scan cycle time

$$
T_{scan} = \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}} = \dfrac{1}{60} \approx 16.67 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate maximum time per digit

$$
t_{digit} = \dfrac{T_{scan}}{N} = \dfrac{16.67 \text{ ms}}{4} \approx 4.17 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 4: Practical design choice

In actual Embedded C code, a value between **1 ms and 5 ms** is typically used. Selecting **$t_{digit} = 5$ ms** gives:

$$
f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot t_{digit}} = \dfrac{1}{4 \times 0.005} = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

This is **above the flicker fusion threshold** (typically 50–60 Hz), so the display appears continuous and flicker-free. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Embedded C Hex Code Array Declaration

For 7-segment programming in Embedded C (8051 / LPC2148), the standard approach is to store hex codes in a **lookup array**:

```c
// Common Cathode hex codes for digits 0-9
unsigned char cc_codes[10] = {
    0x3F, // 0
    0x06, // 1
    0x5B, // 2
    0x4F, // 3
    0x66, // 4
    0x6D, // 5
    0x7D, // 6
    0x07, // 7
    0x7F, // 8
    0x6F  // 9
};

// Common Anode hex codes for digits 0-9
unsigned char ca_codes[10] = {
    0xC0, // 0
    0xF9, // 1
    0xA4, // 2
    0xB0, // 3
    0x99, // 4
    0x92, // 5
    0x82, // 6
    0xF8, // 7
    0x80, // 8
    0x90  // 9
};
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Coding Convention Note:** The array index directly corresponds to the digit being displayed. This makes the lookup trivial: `P2 = cc_codes[digit_value];` — a single line of C replaces dozens of `if-else` statements.

## 3.5 Complete KTU-Style Multiplexed Display Algorithm (Pseudocode → C)

```c
#include <reg51.h>   // 8051 SFR definitions

// Segment data port (P2) and digit-select port (P1)
sbit DIG1 = P1^0;
sbit DIG2 = P1^1;
sbit DIG3 = P1^2;
sbit DIG4 = P1^3;

// Lookup table for common-cathode display, digits 0-9
unsigned char code seg_table[10] = {
    0x3F, 0x06, 0x5B, 0x4F, 0x66,
    0x6D, 0x7D, 0x07, 0x7F, 0x6F
};

// Four digits to display
unsigned char digits[4] = {1, 2, 3, 4};

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++);
}

void display_digit(unsigned char pos, unsigned char val) {
    // Deactivate all digits first
    P1 = 0xFF;
    // Send segment pattern
    P2 = seg_table[val];
    // Activate the selected digit (active LOW for CC)
    switch(pos) {
        case 0: DIG1 = 0; break;
        case 1: DIG2 = 0; break;
        case 2: DIG3 = 0; break;
        case 3: DIG4 = 0; break;
    }
    delay_ms(5);
}

void main(void) {
    while(1) {
        display_digit(0, digits[0]);  // Show digit 1
        display_digit(1, digits[1]);  // Show digit 2
        display_digit(2, digits[2]);  // Show digit 3
        display_digit(3, digits[3]);  // Show digit 4
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Programming Concepts Tested by KTU:**
> 1. **Lookup table** technique for fast code generation.
> 2. **Time-multiplexing** with proper delay for persistence of vision.
> 3. **Active LOW** vs **active HIGH** digit selection logic.
> 4. **Port configuration** of 8051 (P0 needs pull-ups, P1–P3 have internal pull-ups).
> 5. **Switch debouncing** if the display content is being updated from a keypad.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Embedded C Multiplexing Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Start Main Loop])
    INIT["Initialize Port P1 as Output Digit Select"]
    INIT2["Initialize Port P2 as Output Segment Data"]
    SETI["Set digit index i = 0"]
    CHKEND{"i less than 4"}
    DEACTALL["Deactivate all four digit lines P1 equals FF"]
    SENDPAT["P2 equals segment code table digit value of i"]
    SELACT["Activate digit i active LOW for CC"]
    DELAY["Delay 5 ms persistence of vision"]
    INC["Increment i"]
    GOBACK["Repeat from i equals 0"]
    ENDNODE([Continuous Loop])

    START --> INIT --> INIT2 --> SETI --> CHKEND
    CHKEND -- Yes --> DEACTALL --> SENDPAT --> SELACT --> DELAY --> INC --> CHKEND
    CHKEND -- No --> GOBACK --> ENDNODE
```

sequenceDiagram

    participant MCU as Microcontroller
    participant D1 as Digit 1
    participant D2 as Digit 2
    participant D3 as Digit 3

        T->>MCU: Tick interrupt
        MCU->>D1: Activate (LOW), send seg code for digit 1
        Note over D1: ON for 5 ms

        Note over D2: ON for 5 ms

        Note over D3: ON for 5 ms

    end
