# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Common Anode and Common Cathode 7-segment displays. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Common Anode | Common Cathode |
|---|---|---|
| Common terminal tied to | Vcc (+5 V) | GND (0 V) |
| Segment ON logic | LOW (0 V) | HIGH (+5 V) |
| Segment OFF logic | HIGH (+5 V) | LOW (0 V) |
| Driver IC used | 7447 (active LOW) | 7448 (active HIGH) |
| Current direction | From Vcc to MCU pin | From MCU pin to GND |
| Typical application | Sourcing current, brighter | Sinking current, common in MCUs |

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is meant by multiplexing in 7-segment displays? Why is it used? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Multiplexing** is a technique in which multiple 7-segment displays share a **common set of segment data lines (a–g, dp)** but are activated **one at a time in rapid succession** through individual digit-select lines. The microcontroller cycles through each digit, lighting it for a few milliseconds (typically 1–5 ms), and the human eye perceives all digits as glowing simultaneously due to **persistence of vision**.

**Why it is used:**
1. **Pin count reduction** — 4 digits require only 8 segment + 4 digit = 12 pins instead of 32 pins.
2. **Power saving** — Only one digit is ON at any instant, reducing average current.
3. **Simplified wiring** — Common segment bus reduces PCB complexity.
4. **Cost-effective** — Fewer microcontroller pins and driver circuitry.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Modified for Module 2]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**A) (i)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal structure of a 7-segment LED display. List the segments required to display digits **0, 1, 7, 9** and write the corresponding hex codes for both common-cathode and common-anode configurations. **(7 Marks)**

**(ii)** Design the current-limiting resistor for a 7-segment common-anode display interfaced to a 5 V microcontroller, given forward voltage $V_F = 2.2$ V per segment and desired segment current $I_F = 12$ mA. Verify whether a standard **220 Ω** resistor is suitable. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution A(i):

**Internal Structure of 7-Segment Display:**

The display consists of **seven individual LEDs** (a, b, c, d, e, f, g) plus an **optional decimal point LED (dp)**. The LEDs are arranged in a figure-8 pattern. Either all anodes are tied together (**Common Anode**) or all cathodes are tied together (**Common Cathode**). Each segment has its own external pin; the common pin is connected to Vcc or GND.

**Segments required for given digits:**

| Digit | Segments ON | Segments OFF |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | a, b, c, d, e, f | g, dp |
| **1** | b, c | a, d, e, f, g, dp |
| **7** | a, b, c | d, e, f, g, dp |
| **9** | a, b, c, d, f, g | e, dp |

**Hex codes (using MSB-first convention, dp = bit 7, a = bit 0):**

For digit **0**:
- Segments ON: a, b, c, d, e, f → bits 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 = 1; g (bit 6) and dp (bit 7) = 0.
- **CC: 0b00111111 = 0x3F** | **CA: 0b11000000 = 0xC0**

For digit **1**:
- Segments ON: b, c → bits 1, 2 = 1; all others = 0.
- **CC: 0b00000110 = 0x06** | **CA: 0b11111001 = 0xF9**

For digit **7**:
- Segments ON: a, b, c → bits 0, 1, 2 = 1; all others = 0.
- **CC: 0b00000111 = 0x07** | **CA: 0b11111000 = 0xF8**

For digit **9**:
- Segments ON: a, b, c, d, f, g → bits 0, 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 = 1; e (bit 4) and dp (bit 7) = 0.
- **CC: 0b01101111 = 0x6F** | **CA: 0b10010000 = 0x90**

---

#### Model Solution A(ii):

**Step 1: Apply the current-limiting resistor formula**

$$
R = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values**

$$
R = \dfrac{5.0 \text{ V} - 2.2 \text{ V}}{12 \text{ mA}} = \dfrac{2.8 \text{ V}}{12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the value**

$$
R = \dfrac{2.8}{0.012} = 233.33 \ \Omega
$$

**Step 4: Compare with the standard 220 Ω value**

If a **220 Ω** resistor is used:

$$
I_F = \dfrac{5.0 - 2.2}{220} = \dfrac{2.8}{220} = 12.73 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 5: Verify safety**

- Standard LED maximum continuous current = **20–30 mA**.
- 12.73 mA is **well within safe limits** (about 63% of the 20 mA typical maximum).
- The slight increase in current (from 12 mA to 12.73 mA) results in marginally higher brightness, which is acceptable.

**Conclusion:** A **220 Ω resistor is suitable** for this design. ✓

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks)

**B) (i)** Explain the **time-multiplexing technique** used to drive a 4-digit 7-segment display. Calculate the maximum time that can be allocated per digit for a flicker-free display at a refresh frequency of **60 Hz**. **(7 Marks)**

**(ii)** Write an **Embedded C program** for the 8051 microcontroller to display the number **"2024"** on a 4-digit common-cathode multiplexed 7-segment display connected to **Port 2 (segments)** and **Port 1 (digit select)**. Use a lookup table for hex codes. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution B(i):

**Time-Multiplexing Technique:**

In a multiplexed 4-digit 7-segment display, all four digits share a **common 8-bit segment bus** (a–g, dp) connected to a single port (e.g., P2). Each digit's **common pin** is driven separately through individual GPIO lines (e.g., P1.0, P1.1, P1.2, P1.3). The microcontroller activates **one digit at a time** by pulling its common pin LOW (for CC) and simultaneously writing the appropriate segment pattern to the bus. After a short delay (1–5 ms), it moves to the next digit. This cycle repeats indefinitely.

**Calculation:**

For $N = 4$ digits and $f_{refresh} = 60$ Hz:

**Step 1:** Total scan cycle time:

$$
T_{scan} = \dfrac{1}{f_{refresh}} = \dfrac{1}{60} \approx 16.67 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2:** Maximum time per digit:

$$
t_{digit} = \dfrac{T_{scan}}{N} = \dfrac{16.67 \text{ ms}}{4} \approx 4.17 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion:** Each digit can be ON for a **maximum of 4.17 ms** to maintain flicker-free operation at 60 Hz refresh. In practice, designers use **5 ms per digit** (giving 50 Hz refresh), which is also above the flicker fusion threshold.

---

#### Model Solution B(ii):

```c
#include <reg51.h>

// Digit select lines on Port 1
sbit D1 = P1^0;
sbit D2 = P1^1;
sbit D3 = P1^2;
sbit D4 = P1^3;

// Lookup table for common-cathode display, digits 0-9
unsigned char code seg[10] = {
    0x3F, 0x06, 0x5B, 0x4F, 0x66,
    0x6D, 0x7D, 0x07, 0x7F, 0x6F
};

// Digits to display: 2, 0, 2, 4
unsigned char digit_vals[4] = {2, 0, 2, 4};

void delay(unsigned int t) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < t; i++)
        for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++);
}

void show_digit(unsigned char pos, unsigned char val) {
    // Deactivate all digits
    P1 = 0xFF;
    // Send segment code
    P2 = seg[val];
    // Activate the selected digit (active LOW)
    switch(pos) {
        case 0: D1 = 0; break;
        case 1: D2 = 0; break;
        case 2: D3 = 0; break;
        case 3: D4 = 0; break;
    }
    delay(5);   // 5 ms persistence
}

void main(void) {
    while(1) {
        show_digit(0, digit_vals[0]);  // Display '2'
        show_digit(1, digit_vals[1]);  // Display '0'
        show_digit(2, digit_vals[2]);  // Display '2'
        show_digit(3, digit_vals[3]);  // Display '4'
    }
}
```

**Explanation of the code:**

1. **Header `<reg51.h>`** provides the 8051 Special Function Register (SFR) definitions.
2. **sbit declarations** map individual port pins for digit selection.
3. **Lookup array `seg[10]`** stores the precomputed hex codes for digits 0–9 in common-cathode format.
4. **`show_digit()` function** deactivates all digits (ghost suppression), loads the segment pattern, activates the target digit, and waits 5 ms.
5. **The `main()` infinite loop** cycles through the four digits continuously — this is the multiplexing loop.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Ghosting effect** — Students often forget to deactivate all digits before activating a new one. This causes **faint glow on unintended digits** ("ghosting"). Always set `P1 = 0xFF` (all OFF) before switching.
> 2. **Wrong active logic** — Confusing common-anode (active LOW) with common-cathode (active HIGH). Carefully check the display type given in the question.
> 3. **Insufficient delay** — Using delays shorter than 1 ms causes flickering visible to the eye.
> 4. **Missing lookup table** — Writing 10 separate `if-else` statements instead of using an array wastes code memory and marks.
> 5. **Port P0 not pulled up** — On the 8051, Port 0 has **open-drain outputs** and requires external pull-up resistors for proper HIGH-level driving. Port 1, 2, 3 have internal pull-ups.
> 6. **Incorrect hex code** — Reversing bit ordering (e.g., LSB-first instead of MSB-first) yields wrong segments. Always verify the convention before writing the array.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 7 Segment LED (Embedded C Programming):**

- ⭐ **A 7-segment display has 7 segments (a–g) + 1 decimal point (dp)** arranged in figure-8 pattern.
- ⭐ **Two configurations:** **Common Anode** (common → Vcc, segment ON = LOW) and **Common Cathode** (common → GND, segment ON = HIGH).
- ⭐ **Most critical hex codes to memorize (CC):** 0 = **0x3F**, 1 = **0x06**, 2 = **0x5B**, 3 = **0x4F**, 4 = **0x66**, 5 = **0x6D**, 6 = **0x7D**, 7 = **0x07**, 8 = **0x7F**, 9 = **0x6F**.
- ⭐ **For Common Anode, simply bitwise-INVERT** the CC code (e.g., 0x3F → 0xC0, 0x06 → 0xF9).
- ⭐ **Bit convention:** MSB = dp (bit 7), LSB = a (bit 0). Verify this before writing hex codes.
- ⭐ **Current-limiting resistor formula:** $R = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}$ — standard value **220 Ω or 330 Ω**.
- ⭐ **Multiplexing reduces pin count** from N×8 to 8 + N, exploiting **persistence of vision** (≈ 16.67 ms).
- ⭐ **Minimum refresh rate = 50–60 Hz** to avoid flicker; $t_{digit} = T_{scan} / N$.
- ⭐ **Lookup table technique** is the standard Embedded C approach — index = digit, value = hex code.
- ⭐ **Ghosting prevention:** Always deactivate all digits before activating the next one.
- ⭐ **Decoder ICs:** **7447** (active LOW output, for CA displays) and **7448** (active HIGH output, for CC displays).
- ⭐ **BCD-to-7-segment decoders** accept 4-bit BCD input (0000–1001) and internally generate the 7-segment pattern, offloading the MCU.
- ⭐ **8051 port specifics:** P0 needs **external pull-ups**; P1, P2, P3 have **internal pull-ups**.
- ⭐ **Display applications:** digital clocks, calculators, weighing scales, vehicle dashboards, public info displays.
- ⭐ **Typical forward voltage:** Red LED = 1.8–2.2 V, Blue/White = 3.0–3.6 V.
- ⭐ **Typical segment current:** 10–20 mA.
- ⭐ **Code template:** `P2 = seg_table[digit_value];` then activate appropriate digit-select pin.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition, Features, Simple Example of Embedded Systems, Applications of Embedded Systems - Consumer Electronics, Robotics, Automobiles, Embedded System Architecture: HW - Processor, Controller, SoC, Memory, Peripherals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definition, Features, Simple Example of Embedded Systems, Applications of Embedded Systems - Consumer Electronics, Robotics, Automobiles, Embedded System Architecture: HW - Processor, Controller, SoC, Memory, Peripherals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔷 1.1 Formal Definition of an Embedded System

An **Embedded System** is a specialized computing subsystem that is an integral part of a larger mechanical, electrical, or electronic device. It is *purpose-built* to perform a **dedicated set of functions** — often with **real-time computing constraints** — and is *not* designed to be reprogrammed by the end user (unlike a general-purpose personal computer).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard):**
> *"An Embedded System is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based system that is designed to perform a specific task, with the software embedded permanently (typically in ROM/Flash) into the hardware, and operates under constraints of size, cost, power, and real-time response."*

Formally, the computation engine can be expressed as:

$$ \text{Embedded System} = f(\text{HW}, \text{SW}, \text{Real-time Constraints}) $$

where the function $f$ represents a **dedicated application logic** mapped onto a **constrained hardware platform**.

---

## 🔷 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Dedicated Reflex Brain"

Think of a human reflex arc — when your finger touches a hot surface, your spinal cord (the *embedded controller*) immediately commands the muscles (the *actuators*) to pull away. The brain (general-purpose CPU) isn't involved in this micro-decision.

| Human Body Analog | Embedded System Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Spinal cord (reflex action) | Microcontroller |
| Muscle contraction | Actuator / Output device |
| Pain signal from skin | Sensor input (e.g., temperature) |
| Hard-wired reflex (can't reprogram) | Firmware in ROM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Just as a reflex arc is *dedicated, fast, and limited* in scope, an embedded system is purpose-built for a **specific task**, with **no flexibility for unrelated applications**.

---

## 🔷 1.3 Essential Features of Embedded Systems

A well-engineered embedded system exhibits the following canonical features (high-yield for KTU):

1. **Single-Functioned / Task-Specific** — Performs one or a small set of pre-defined tasks (e.g., only temperature sensing in a thermostat).
2. **Tightly Constrained** — Bounded by *cost*, *power*, *size*, *weight*, and *performance* envelopes.
3. **Real-Time Responsiveness** — Must respond to external events within a *deterministic deadline* (hard or soft real-time).
4. **Reactive Operation** — Continuously interacts with its environment via sensors and actuators (closed-loop behavior).
5. **Microprocessor/Microcontroller-Based Core** — The "brain" is always a processing element (MCU, MPU, DSP, or ASIC).
6. **Memory Footprint** — Uses **on-chip memory** (SRAM, Flash) with strictly limited external memory.
7. **Dedicated User Interface** — Often has no display or only a minimal LED/button interface.
8. **Software Embedded Permanently** — Application code (firmware) is burnt into **non-volatile memory** (ROM/Flash/EPROM).
9. **High Reliability & Stability** — Designed to run for years without failure (MTBF > 100,000 hours).
10. **Low Power Consumption** — Often **battery-operated** or energy-harvested (mW range typical).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Feature Set:** In KTU 2024 University Exams, the *Top 5 most-asked features* are: **(i) Single-function, (ii) Real-time, (iii) Reactive, (iv) Constrained, (v) Software embedded in ROM**.

---

## 🔷 1.4 A Simple Canonical Example — Digital Thermometer

A handheld digital thermometer is one of the **simplest** embedded systems used in textbooks.

| Subsystem | Realization |
|---|---|
| **Processor** | 8-bit Microcontroller (e.g., 8051, ATmega328) |
| **Sensor** | Thermistor or LM35 temperature sensor (analog output) |
| **ADC** | On-chip 10-bit ADC converts voltage → digital count |
| **Memory** | Flash (program), SRAM (runtime data) |
| **Display** | 3-digit 7-segment LCD |
| **Power** | 3 V coin-cell battery |
| **Firmware** | Burnt into Flash — samples ADC, scales via lookup table, drives LCD |

**Operating Principle (Step-by-Step):**
1. Sensor produces an analog voltage $V_{out} \propto T$ (e.g., $10\ \text{mV/°C}$ for LM35).
2. ADC samples $V_{out}$ and produces a digital code: $D = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n$.
3. MCU scales $D$ back to temperature using a calibration constant.
4. Temperature is displayed on the 7-segment display via GPIO drivers.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In 3-mark questions, always illustrate your answer with a *concrete example* (washing machine, digital watch, microwave oven, ABS system). Examiners reward **application-based clarity**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 🔷 2.1 Functional Building Blocks of an Embedded System (Theory Decomposition)

A complete embedded system can be decomposed into **four interlocked layers**. Each layer has a well-defined engineering responsibility.

### Layer 1 — Sensor / Actuator Interface (The Sensory & Motor Organs)
- **Sensors** convert physical phenomena → electrical signals (temperature, pressure, light, motion).
- **Actuators** convert electrical signals → physical action (motor, relay, LED, solenoid).
- Communication uses analog voltage, current loop, or digital buses (I²C, SPI).

### Layer 2 — Processing Core (The Brain)
- Receives raw data from sensors, executes the application algorithm, and produces control commands.
- Can be a **microcontroller (MCU)**, **microprocessor (MPU)**, **DSP**, **ASIC**, or **SoC**.

### Layer 3 — Memory Hierarchy (The Knowledge & Scratchpad)
- **ROM / Flash** → Stores firmware (non-volatile, permanent).
- **RAM (SRAM/DRAM)** → Runtime variables, stack, heap (volatile).
- **Cache** → Optional high-speed buffer (in MPU-based systems).
- **EEPROM / FRAM** → Stores user settings, calibration data.

### Layer 4 — Power, Clock, & Reset Subsystem (The Life-Support)
- **Power Management Unit (PMU)** — Regulates voltage, enables sleep modes.
- **Clock Source** — Crystal oscillator, PLL, or internal RC oscillator.
- **Reset Circuitry** — Brown-out detection (BOD), watchdog timer (WDT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Mapping:** This four-layer model directly maps to the *HW Architecture* sub-topic in the syllabus. Expect a 7-mark question asking students to *"draw and explain the block diagram of an embedded system"*.

---

## 🔷 2.2 Hardware Architecture — Deep Dive into Components

### (A) Processor — The Central Decision Engine
A **processor** is a digital IC that fetches, decodes, and executes instructions from memory. The four canonical processor categories used in embedded design are:

| Processor Type | Key Trait | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Microprocessor (MPU)** | External memory & peripherals; high clock speed | Smartphones, routers, HMI panels |
| **Microcontroller (MCU)** | On-chip RAM, ROM, peripherals; low cost | Washing machines, toys, IoT nodes |
| **DSP (Digital Signal Processor)** | Optimized MAC units, Harvard architecture | Audio codecs, motor control, radar |
| **ASIC** (Application-Specific IC) | Custom-designed for one function; non-reprogrammable | Crypto-miners, smartphone PMICs |

**Why "Harvard vs. von Neumann" matters in KTU:**

$$ \text{Harvard} \Rightarrow \text{Separate program \& data buses} \Rightarrow \text{Faster (no contention)} $$
$$ \text{von Neumann} \Rightarrow \text{Single shared bus} \Rightarrow \text{Simpler hardware, possible bottleneck} $$

> [!NOTE]
> Most modern MCUs (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC) use **Modified Harvard** to combine instruction/data separation with shared main memory.

---

### (B) Controller — The Smart Microprocessor Variant
A **controller** is technically a *microcontroller* — a single IC containing:

1. **CPU core** (8/16/32-bit)
2. **Program memory** (Flash/ROM)
3. **Data memory** (SRAM)
4. **I/O ports** (GPIO)
5. **Peripheral blocks** (Timers, ADC, UART, I²C, SPI, PWM, Watchdog)

**Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):**
- *Microprocessor* = CPU alone (needs external RAM, ROM, peripherals to function).
- *Microcontroller* = CPU + RAM + ROM + Peripherals on a single chip.

---

### (C) System on Chip (SoC) — The Modern Integration
A **SoC** integrates the **entire system** (CPU + GPU + Memory controller + Peripherals + Connectivity + Power Management) onto a **single silicon die**. Examples include:

- **Qualcomm Snapdragon** (smartphone SoC)
- **ESP32** (Wi-Fi + Bluetooth + Dual-core MCU + RF)
- **NXP i.MX8** (automotive infotainment)
- **Broadcom BCM2711** (Raspberry Pi SoC)

$$ \text{SoC} = \text{MPU} + \text{MCU} + \text{Memory} + \text{RF/Connectivity} + \text{Accelerators} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Difference between *Microcontroller* and *SoC* is a high-weightage question. SoCs dominate **high-end embedded applications** (autonomous driving, smartphones) while MCUs dominate **cost-sensitive** applications (toys, appliances).

---

### (D) Memory — The Information Store

**Classification along two axes:**

| Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Volatility** | Volatile (RAM) | Non-volatile (ROM, Flash) |
| **Access Pattern** | Random Access (RAM) | Sequential Access (older tapes) |

**Memory Types in Embedded Systems:**

- **ROM (Read-Only Memory):** Mask ROM — programmed during fabrication. Cheap in mass production.
- **PROM / EPROM / EEPROM:** Programmable (UV-erase or electrically-erasable).
- **Flash Memory:** In-system programmable, blocks-erased. *De-facto* standard in modern MCUs.
- **SRAM (Static RAM):** Fast, expensive, used for cache and runtime data.
- **DRAM (Dynamic RAM):** Higher density, needs refresh, used in MPU-based systems.
- **FRAM (Ferroelectric RAM):** Combines speed of SRAM with non-volatility (used in TI MSP430).

**Memory Capacity Equivalence (Useful for numericals):**

$$ 1\ \text{KB} = 2^{10} = 1024\ \text{bytes} $$
$$ 1\ \text{MB} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576\ \text{bytes} $$
$$ 1\ \text{GB} = 2^{30}\ \text{bytes} $$

---

### (E) Peripherals — The Connection to the Outside World

Peripherals are auxiliary hardware blocks integrated into an MCU/SoC that enable interaction with the external world. They are categorized as:

| Category | Examples | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Communication** | UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB, Ethernet | Serial/parallel data transfer |
| **Timing** | Timer/Counter, RTC, PWM, Watchdog | Event counting, pulse generation |
| **Analog I/O** | ADC, DAC, Comparator, Op-Amp | Convert real-world signals |
| **Digital I/O** | GPIO, I/O Expander, Multiplexer | Bit-level control |
| **Motion Control** | PWM, QEI (Quadrature Encoder Interface), Stepper Driver | Motor control |
| **Security** | Crypto accelerator, True Random Number Generator (TRNG) | Encryption, secure boot |

> [!TIP]
> **For numerical questions on memory:** A 32-bit address bus can address $2^{32}$ unique locations = **4 GB** of memory. This is why 32-bit MCUs hit a memory ceiling without LPAE (Large Physical Address Extension).

---

## 🔷 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADC Conversion | $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n$ | $D$ in LSBs |
| 2 | Quantization Error | $Q = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Volts |
| 3 | Addressable Memory | $M = 2^{N}$ bytes ($N$ = address bits) | Bytes |
| 4 | Data Rate (Baud) | $B = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16(N+1)}$ (for UART mode 1) | bps |
| 5 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | Percent |
| 6 | Instruction Cycle Time | $T_{cyc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ (8051) | Seconds |
| 7 | Power Dissipation | $P = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ | Watts |
| 8 | I²C Clock Frequency | $f_{SCL} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 + 2 \cdot TWBR}$ (AVR) | Hz |
| 9 | Memory Capacity Conversion | $1\ \text{KB} = 1024\ \text{bytes}$ | — |
| 10 | Resolution (Sensor) | $R = \dfrac{\text{Full-Scale}}{2^n - 1}$ | Volts/LSB |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Alert — Table Syntax:** KTU answer scripts that use the `|` symbol inside cells (e.g., for absolute value $|x|$) cause **script-line misinterpretation**. Always write `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX form.

---

## 🔷 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Embedded Systems Matter |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Engine control, ADAS, infotainment — millions of MCUs per modern car. |
| **Medical** | Pacemakers, infusion pumps, MRI machines — reliability is *life-critical*. |
| **Industrial (Industry 4.0)** | PLCs, SCADA, predictive maintenance sensors. |
| **Aerospace** | Flight control computers (FBW), satellite attitude control. |
| **Consumer** | Smart TVs, IoT wearables, gaming consoles. |
| **Agriculture** | Precision farming drones, automated irrigation. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 🔷 3.1 Analytical Classification of Embedded Systems (Step-by-Step)

Embedded systems can be classified along **multiple orthogonal axes**. The KTU 2024 module requires a clear, structured classification that students should be able to **draw, label, and explain**.

### Step 1 — Classification by Performance & Complexity

$$\text{Embedded Systems} \rightarrow 
\begin{cases}
\text{Small-Scale} \\
\text{Medium-Scale} \\
\text{Large-Scale} / \text{SoC-based}
\end{cases}$$

| Class | Hardware | Typical App |
|---|---|---|
| **Small-Scale** | 8-bit MCU + minimal peripherals | Toy, calculator |
| **Medium-Scale** | 16/32-bit MCU + external peripherals | Washing machine, elevator |
| **Large-Scale** | MPU / SoC + RTOS + complex I/O | Smartphone, ADAS |

### Step 2 — Classification by Determinism (Real-Time Behavior)

$$\text{Real-Time Class} \rightarrow 
\begin{cases}
\text{Hard Real-Time} \Rightarrow \text{Missed deadline} = \text{System failure} \\
\text{Soft Real-Time} \Rightarrow \text{Missed deadline} = \text{Quality degradation} \\
\text{Firm Real-Time} \Rightarrow \text{Multiple misses} = \text{Failure}
\end{cases}$$

- **Hard RT Example:** Airbag deployment controller — *must* fire within **< 5 ms**.
- **Soft RT Example:** Video streaming — late frame is acceptable.
- **Firm RT Example:** Weather data logger — old data is useless but not catastrophic.

### Step 3 — Classification by Architecture

$$\text{Architecture} \rightarrow 
\begin{cases}
\text{von Neumann (single bus)} \\
\text{Harvard (separate instruction \& data buses)} \\
\text{Modified Harvard (cached unified memory)}
\end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Note for Real-Time Deadline Analysis:**
> Worst-case execution time (WCET) for a periodic task $\tau_i$ is computed as:
>
> $$ R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j $$
>
> where $C_i$ is the worst-case computation time, $T_j$ is the period, and $hp(i)$ is the set of higher-priority tasks.

---

## 🔷 3.2 Architecture Analysis — From Sensor to Actuator (Exhaustive Flow)

The complete **HW flow** of an embedded system, in exhaustive analytical form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 (Sense):} \quad & \text{Physical phenomenon} \xrightarrow{\text{Transducer}} V_{analog} \\
\text{Step 2 (Condition):} \quad & V_{analog} \xrightarrow{\text{Signal Conditioning (Amp/Filter)}} V_{clean} \\
\text{Step 3 (Convert):} \quad & V_{clean} \xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} D_{digital} \\
\text{Step 4 (Compute):} \quad & D_{digital} \xrightarrow{\text{MCU Algorithm}} C_{control} \\
\text{Step 5 (Convert back, if analog):} \quad & C_{control} \xrightarrow{\text{DAC or PWM}} A_{analog} \\
\text{Step 6 (Actuate):} \quad & A_{analog} \xrightarrow{\text{Driver Circuit}} \text{Physical Action}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each transformation step:**

- **Step 1 — Sensing:** Sensors (e.g., thermistor, LDR, accelerometer) convert physical quantities to voltages/currents.
- **Step 2 — Signal Conditioning:** The raw sensor output is often noisy or weak. Op-amp amplification and anti-aliasing filters prepare it for digitization.
- **Step 3 — ADC:** Samples the analog signal at a rate $\geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist theorem) and quantizes it into $n$-bit codes.
- **Step 4 — MCU Firmware:** Executes the embedded control algorithm (e.g., PID controller, lookup table, threshold comparison).
- **Step 5 — Output Conversion:** For analog outputs, DAC or PWM is used. For digital outputs, GPIO is sufficient.
- **Step 6 — Actuation:** Drivers (MOSFET/IGBT/relay) amplify the control signal to drive the physical actuator (motor, heater, buzzer).

---

## 🔷 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Address Space of a 16-bit MCU

**Problem:** Calculate the maximum directly-addressable memory of a 16-bit microcontroller.

**Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & N = 16\ \text{bits} \\
\text{Addressable memory:} \quad & M = 2^{N} \\
& M = 2^{16} \\
& M = 65536\ \text{locations} \\
& M = 64\ \text{KB} \quad (\text{since } 1\ \text{KB} = 1024\ \text{bytes}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 16-bit address bus (e.g., classic **Intel 8086**, or **8051** with 16-bit PC) can directly access **64 KB** of memory. Beyond this, **bank switching** or **memory paging** is required.

---

## 🔷 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — ADC Resolution

**Problem:** A 10-bit ADC has $V_{ref} = 5\ \text{V}$. Find the digital output for $V_{in} = 2.5\ \text{V}$ and the quantization step size.

**Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & n = 10,\ V_{ref} = 5\ \text{V},\ V_{in} = 2.5\ \text{V} \\
\text{Step 1: Quantization step size} \quad & Q = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \dfrac{5}{1024} = 4.88\ \text{mV/LSB} \\
\text{Step 2: Digital output} \quad & D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{Q} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{2.5}{4.88 \times 10^{-3}} \right\rfloor \\
& D = \left\lfloor 512 \right\rfloor = 512\ \text{decimal} = 0 \times 200\ \text{hex}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The ADC represents 2.5 V (half of $V_{ref}$) as the **mid-scale value** $512$, exactly as expected for a bipolar range.

---

## 🔷 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Processor Variants

| Attribute | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) | SoC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | CPU only | CPU + Memory + Peripherals on chip | Entire system on single die |
| **External Memory** | Required (RAM, ROM) | Not required (internal) | Often internal, but supports external |
| **Clock Speed** | High (>100 MHz) | Low–Medium (1–200 MHz) | High (>1 GHz) |
| **Cost** | High (system-level) | Low (chip-level) | Medium–High |
| **Power** | High (W range) | Low (mW range) | Variable (depends on use) |
| **OS Support** | Full OS (Linux, Android) | Bare-metal or RTOS | Full OS + RTOS coexistence |
| **Use Case** | Routers, smartphones (legacy) | Toys, sensors, appliances | Modern smartphones, drones |
| **Example** | Intel x86, ARM Cortex-A9 | ATmega328, STM32F4 | Qualcomm Snapdragon, ESP32 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** When asked *"Differentiate MPU, MCU, and SoC,"* the examiner expects a **tabular comparison** (3–5 rows minimum) with at least one **real-world example** per category.

---

## 🔷 3.6 Application Domain — Detailed Analytical Breakdown

### A) Consumer Electronics

| Sub-Domain | Embedded Role | Processor / SoC |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart TV** | Video decoding, HDMI, network stack, UI rendering | ARM Cortex-A53 / A55 SoC |
| **Smartwatch** | Health sensors, BT, RTOS, low-power always-on | Qualcomm Snapdragon Wear, Apple S-series |
| **Washing Machine** | Motor control (PWM), water level sensing, UI | 8/32-bit MCU (e.g., STM32) |
| **Microwave Oven** | Magnetron control, keypad input, display | 8-bit MCU (8051, PIC) |

### B) Robotics

| Component | Embedded Role |
|---|---|
| **Motor Drivers** | PWM generation, current sensing, closed-loop PID |
| **Sensors** | IMU, LiDAR, encoders, vision (camera + DSP) |
| **Controller** | High-performance MCU/SoC (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, STM32H7) |
| **RTOS** | FreeRTOS, ROS 2, NuttX for task scheduling |
| **Communication** | CAN bus (multi-joint), UART, SPI, EtherCAT |

### C) Automobiles

Modern cars contain **80–100+ ECUs (Electronic Control Units)**. A few major subsystems:

| Subsystem | Embedded Function |
|---|---|
| **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** | Fuel injection, ignition timing (hard RT) |
| **Anti-lock Braking (ABS)** | Wheel-speed sensing, hydraulic modulation (hard RT) |
| **Airbag System** | Crash detection, squib firing (< 5 ms) |
| **Infotainment** | Touchscreen, navigation, smartphone projection |
| **ADAS** | Radar, camera fusion, lane-keeping |
| **Body Control Module (BCM)** | Lights, wipers, central locking |
| **EV Battery Management (BMS)** | Cell voltage/temperature, SoC estimation |
| **Communication Buses** | CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet |

> [!TIP]
> **In KTU 14-mark questions on automobile applications,** draw a car-block diagram labeling ECUs, and mention **CAN protocol** for inter-ECU communication. This is a high-value addition that examiners reward.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 🔷 4.1 Generic Embedded System Block Diagram

This is the **canonical block diagram** expected in any KTU question asking to *"draw the architecture of an embedded system."*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical World] -->|Stimuli| B[Sensor]
    B -->|Analog Signal| C[Signal Conditioner]
    C -->|Clean Analog| D[Analog to Digital Converter]
    D -->|Digital Data| E[Central Processing Unit]
    E <-->|Addr Data Ctrl| F[Memory Unit]
    E -->|Control Signals| G[Output Ports and Drivers]
    G -->|Power Signal| H[Actuator]
    H -->|Physical Action| I[Physical World]
    J[Power Supply Unit] --> E
    J --> F
    J --> G
    K[Clock and Reset] --> E
    L[Communication Interfaces UART SPI I2C CAN] --> E
```

---

## 🔷 4.2 Classification Tree — Embedded System Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Embedded Systems] --> B[By Complexity]
    A --> C[By Real Time Behavior]
    A --> D[By Architecture]
    B --> B1[Small Scale 8 bit MCU]
    B --> B2[Medium Scale 16 32 bit MCU]
    B --> B3[Large Scale MPU or SoC]
    C --> C1[Hard Real Time]
    C --> C2[Soft Real Time]
    C --> C3[Firm Real Time]
    D --> D1[Von Neumann]
    D --> D2[Harvard]
    D --> D3[Modified Harvard]
```

---

## 🔷 4.3 HW Architecture — Processor Controller SoC Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Processor Family] --> P1[Microprocessor MPU]
    P --> P2[Microcontroller MCU]
    P --> P3[SoC System on Chip]
    P1 --> P1a[CPU Core Only]
    P1a --> P1b[Needs External RAM ROM]
    P2 --> P2a[CPU plus RAM plus ROM plus Peripherals]
    P3 --> P3a[CPU plus GPU plus Memory plus RF plus PMU]
    P1b --> U1[Use Routers Smartphones]
    P2a --> U2[Use Appliances Toys Sensors]
    P3a --> U3[Use Modern Smartphones Drones ADAS]
```

---

## 🔷 4.4 Memory Hierarchy in an Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Memory Subsystem] --> M1[Non Volatile Storage]
    M --> M2[Volatile Storage]
    M1 --> M1a[Flash Stores Firmware]
    M1 --> M1b[EEPROM Stores Calibration]
    M1 --> M1c[ROM Mask Programmed]
    M2 --> M2a[SRAM Runtime Variables]
    M2 --> M2b[DRAM Used in MPU Systems]
    M2 --> M2c[Cache Optional in High End]
    M1a --> CPU1[Accessed by CPU at Boot]
    M2a --> CPU2[Accessed by CPU at Run Time]
```

---

## 🔷 4.5 Automotive ECU Network (Application Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ECU1[Engine ECU] --> BUS[CAN Bus]
    ECU2[ABS ECU] --> BUS
    ECU3[Airbag ECU] --> BUS
    ECU4[Body Control Module] --> BUS
    ECU5[Infotainment Head Unit] --> BUS
    ECU6[Battery Management System] --> BUS
    BUS --> GATE[Central Gateway]
    GATE --> EXT[OBD II Diagnostic Port]
    GATE --> CLOUD[Telematics Cloud Link]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and start with letters to comply with the Mermaid v10 syntax rules. Labels are uppercase alphanumeric to prevent rendering issues.

---

## 🔷 4.6 Robotics Embedded Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Sensors IMU LiDAR Encoder] --> CTRL[Robot Controller MCU or SoC]
    HMI[Operator HMI Touchscreen] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> PWM[PWM and Motor Drivers]
    PWM --> M1[Wheel Motor]
    PWM --> M2[Joint Servo]
    CTRL --> COMM[Wireless Comms WiFi BLE]
    COMM --> OPS[Remote Operator Console]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔷 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define an embedded system. List any FOUR key features of embedded systems.**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, As per 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
An embedded system is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based computing system that is designed to perform a specific, dedicated function as part of a larger device. It operates under constraints of size, cost, power, and often real-time deadlines. The software (firmware) is permanently embedded in ROM/Flash.

**Any Four Features (1.5 marks):**

1. **Single-functioned / Task-specific** — Designed to do only one or a few related tasks (e.g., only temperature sensing in a thermostat).
2. **Tight constraints** — Limited by cost, power, memory, and size budgets.
3. **Real-time operation** — Must respond to external events within deterministic time deadlines.
4. **Reactive** — Continuously interacts with the environment through sensors and actuators.
5. **Software embedded in ROM** — Firmware is stored permanently in non-volatile memory.

*(Any four well-explained features are acceptable; examiners expect 1-mark per feature with a brief justification.)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between a Microprocessor (MPU) and a Microcontroller (MCU). Give one example of each.**
`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, As per 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Attribute | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | CPU only | CPU + RAM + ROM + Peripherals on a single chip |
| **External Memory** | Mandatory (external RAM, ROM) | Not required (internal) |
| **Cost & Power** | Higher | Lower |
| **Example** | Intel Core i3, ARM Cortex-A53 | ATmega328, STM32F103, PIC16F877A |
| **Application** | Routers, general-purpose computing | Washing machines, toys, sensor nodes |

**Examples (for full 3 marks):**
- **MPU Example:** Intel 8086 used in early IBM PCs.
- **MCU Example:** ATmega328 used in Arduino Uno boards.

---

## 🔷 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** A Module 3 14-mark question is typically divided into **two 7-mark sub-parts**, with **internal choice** (either OR between sub-parts OR alternative full questions of the same module). We model it as Question A vs. Question B.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choose this OR Question B)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: *Define an embedded system. Explain the key features with a neat block diagram. Discuss any THREE applications of embedded systems in consumer electronics, robotics, and automobiles with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Definition (1.5 marks):**
An embedded system is a specialized computing subsystem designed to perform a dedicated function within a larger mechanical or electronic system. It is microprocessor/microcontroller-based, with software embedded permanently into non-volatile memory, operating under constraints of power, cost, size, and real-time response.

**(ii) Key Features (1.5 marks):**
- Single-functioned, task-specific.
- Real-time, deterministic response.
- Reactive to environment via sensors/actuators.
- Tight constraints (power, memory, cost).
- Firmware in ROM, not user-reprogrammable.

**(iii) Block Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the canonical block diagram (see Section 4.1) — must show **Sensor → ADC → CPU ↔ Memory → Output Port → Actuator**, with **Power Supply** and **Clock/Reset** sub-blocks.

**(iv) Three Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Consumer Electronics — Digital Camera:** Image sensor (CCD/CMOS) → Image signal processor (ISP) → JPEG encoder → Memory card storage. MCU orchestrates the pipeline.
2. **Robotics — Line-Follower Robot:** IR sensors detect line position → MCU computes correction → PWM to motor drivers adjusts wheel speeds.
3. **Automobiles — Anti-Lock Braking System (ABS):** Wheel-speed sensors → ECU processes → Hydraulic modulator pulses brake pressure to prevent wheel lockup (hard real-time).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: *Explain the hardware architecture of an embedded system. With the help of a block diagram, describe the role of: (i) Processor/Controller, (ii) SoC, (iii) Memory, and (iv) Peripherals.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Processor/Controller (1.75 marks):**
The processor/controller is the central decision engine. An **MPU** is a CPU-only IC requiring external memory; an **MCU** integrates CPU + RAM + ROM + peripherals on-chip. Common architectures include **von Neumann** (single bus) and **Harvard** (separate instruction/data buses). Example: STM32F407 (Cortex-M4) is a 32-bit MCU.

**(ii) SoC (1.75 marks):**
A **System on Chip (SoC)** integrates CPU + GPU + memory controllers + connectivity (WiFi/BT) + power management + accelerators on a single die. Examples: **Qualcomm Snapdragon 888** (smartphones), **ESP32** (IoT). SoCs are dominant in modern high-end embedded applications due to high integration, low power, and small form factor.

**(iii) Memory (1.75 marks):**
Memory stores program code and runtime data. **Non-volatile memory** (Flash, ROM, EEPROM) holds firmware. **Volatile memory** (SRAM, DRAM) holds runtime variables. For example, ATmega328 has 32 KB Flash + 2 KB SRAM. A 32-bit address bus can access up to $2^{32} = 4$ GB of memory.

**(iv) Peripherals (1.75 marks):**
Peripherals enable interaction with the external world. Categories:
- **Communication:** UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB.
- **Timing:** Timers, PWM, Watchdog, RTC.
- **Analog I/O:** ADC, DAC, Comparator.
- **Digital I/O:** GPIO, I/O Expanders.

Example: An **STM32** MCU integrates multiple ADCs, UARTs, SPI controllers, and PWM channels on-chip.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: *List and explain FIVE distinguishing features of embedded systems. How do they differ from general-purpose computers? Give TWO real-world examples.*

**Model Answer:**

**Five Features (3.5 marks):**
1. **Single-functioned:** Performs one dedicated task; not a multi-purpose machine.
2. **Real-time:** Must respond within deterministic deadlines.
3. **Reactive:** Interacts continuously with environment.
4. **Constrained:** Limited by cost, size, weight, power, and memory.
5. **Reliability:** Must operate without failure for years (no rebooting, no maintenance).

**Differences from General-Purpose Computers (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Embedded System | General-Purpose PC |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Single/dedicated | Multi-purpose |
| **OS** | Often none or RTOS | Full OS (Windows/Linux) |
| **User-reprogrammable** | No | Yes |
| **Power** | Low (mW) | High (100+ W) |
| **Cost** | $1–$50 | $300+ |
| **Update cycle** | Years | Months |

**Two Real-World Examples (1.5 marks):**
- **Microwave oven:** 8-bit MCU controls magnetron timing, turntable motor, and keypad.
- **Smartwatch:** SoC with BT, IMU, AMOLED driver, RTOS, and battery management.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: *Describe in detail the HW components of an embedded system: Processor, Controller, SoC, Memory, and Peripherals. Draw a labeled diagram showing their interconnections.*

**Model Answer:**

**Processor (1.5 marks):** CPU executing instructions. Variants: MPU (CPU only), MCU (CPU+Memory+Peripherals), DSP (signal processing optimized), ASIC (custom).

**Controller (1 mark):** A microcontroller — single IC containing CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O + peripherals. Example: 8051, PIC, ATmega.

**SoC (1.5 marks):** Single-die integration of CPU + GPU + memory controller + connectivity + PMU. Examples: Snapdragon, ESP32, Broadcom BCM.

**Memory (1.5 marks):** Flash (firmware), SRAM (runtime), EEPROM (settings). Memory hierarchy follows size-vs-speed tradeoff.

**Peripherals (1.5 marks):** Communication (UART, SPI, I²C, CAN), timing (timers, PWM), analog (ADC, DAC), digital (GPIO).

**Interconnection Diagram (bonus mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram.

---

## 🔷 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 Answers (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Definition marks are lost** when students omit the *real-time* or *dedicated function* aspect. A definition that says *"embedded system is a computer inside a device"* is **incomplete** and may lose up to **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing MPU with MCU** is the most common error. Examiners specifically check if students understand the *on-chip memory + peripherals* nature of MCUs.
> 3. **Block diagrams missing power supply and clock/reset** sub-blocks are flagged as *incomplete*. Always include these.
> 4. **Real-time class is often missed** — students classify *ABS* as soft real-time when it is **hard real-time** (a missed deadline = fatality).
> 5. **Memory numericals** — using $1000$ instead of $1024$ for KB conversion is a frequent error costing 1 mark.
> 6. **SoC vs MCU confusion** — SoCs include *connectivity + accelerators + GPU*; MCUs do not. Examiners expect this distinction in 14-mark answers.
> 7. **No application example** — a definition without an *example* (e.g., digital thermometer) loses **0.5–1 mark** in 3-mark and 7-mark questions.

---

## 🔷 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist as your final-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Definition:** Embedded system = dedicated, real-time, reactive, constrained computing subsystem embedded within a larger device.
- [x] **Top 5 Features:** Single-functioned, real-time, reactive, tightly constrained, software in ROM.
- [x] **Simple Example:** Digital thermometer (sensor → ADC → MCU → display).
- [x] **Consumer Electronics Apps:** Smart TV, smartwatch, washing machine, microwave oven.
- [x] **Robotics Apps:** Motor control (PWM + PID), sensor fusion (IMU + encoders), RTOS-based task scheduling.
- [x] **Automobile Apps:** ECU, ABS, airbag, BMS, infotainment, ADAS, BCM — 80–100+ ECUs per modern car.
- [x] **HW Architecture Layers:** Sensor → Conditioning → ADC → CPU ↔ Memory → Output Driver → Actuator.
- [x] **Processor Types:** MPU (CPU only) vs. MCU (CPU + RAM + ROM + Peripherals) vs. SoC (entire system on one die).
- [x] **Memory Types:** Flash (firmware, non-volatile), SRAM (runtime, volatile), EEPROM (settings).
- [x] **Peripheral Categories:** Communication (UART, SPI, I²C, CAN), Timing (Timers, PWM, WDT), Analog (ADC, DAC), Digital (GPIO).
- [x] **Key Formula:** $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n$ (ADC conversion), $M = 2^N$ (memory addressability).
- [x] **Real-Time Classes:** Hard (missed deadline = failure) vs. Soft (missed deadline = degraded quality) vs. Firm.
- [x] **Architectures:** von Neumann (single bus) vs. Harvard (separate buses) vs. Modified Harvard.
- [x] **SoC Examples:** Qualcomm Snapdragon, ESP32, NXP i.MX, Broadcom BCM.
- [x] **MCU Examples:** ATmega328, STM32F4, PIC16F877A, MSP430.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Software Architecture of an Embedded System

An **Embedded System Software Stack** is a hierarchical organization of software layers, from the closest-to-hardware components (device drivers) to the user-facing logic (application software), that collectively enables a microcontroller/MCU to perform a dedicated function.

The generic software architecture of an embedded system, from top to bottom, is:

$$
\text{Application Layer} \rightarrow \text{Middleware Layer} \rightarrow \text{Operating System (RTOS)} \rightarrow \text{Device Drivers} \rightarrow \text{Hardware (MCU)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** You MUST remember the hierarchy — Application sits on top, Middleware provides reusable services, OS schedules tasks, and Device Drivers directly manipulate the hardware registers.

### 1.1.1 Application Layer (Software)

The **Application Layer** is the highest layer in the embedded software stack. It contains the **application-specific logic** that directly solves the user's problem. It is the only part of the system that is unique to the product.

* **Definition:** Set of programs written to accomplish the dedicated task of the embedded device (e.g., motor control algorithm, ECG signal processing, anti-lock braking logic).
* **Properties:** Tightly coupled to hardware; usually small in footprint (a few KB to MB); often written in C/C++.
* **Real-World Example:** In a digital washing machine, the application is the finite state machine that sequences Wash → Rinse → Spin based on user button input.

### 1.1.2 Middleware Layer

**Middleware** is a reusable, application-independent software framework that sits **between the application software and the operating system/hardware**. It provides common services so that application developers don't have to write them from scratch.

* **Definition:** A layer of software that provides common capabilities (protocol stacks, file systems, networking, GUI libraries) used across multiple embedded applications.
* **Examples:** TCP/IP stack (lwIP), USB host stack, FAT file system, POSIX libraries, Android HAL, AUTOSAR Run-Time Environment (RTE).
* **Why needed?** It hides the complexity of low-level hardware from the application programmer.

### 1.1.3 Operating System (RTOS)

An **Operating System (OS)** in embedded systems is typically a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** — software that manages the hardware resources and provides predictable, deterministic execution of tasks within strict timing deadlines.

* **Definition:** System software that manages processor time, memory, and peripherals while guaranteeing bounded response time.
* **Examples:** FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS, μC/OS-II, Linux (with PREEMPT_RT patch).
* **Core Functions:** Task scheduling, inter-process communication, memory management, interrupt handling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RTOS and not Windows/Linux in many cases?** Because in hard real-time systems (e.g., airbag deployment, pacemaker), a task MUST complete within a deadline. A general-purpose OS like Windows is not deterministic.

### 1.1.4 Device Drivers

A **Device Driver** is a specialized, low-level software component that directly controls and communicates with a specific hardware peripheral.

* **Definition:** Software that acts as a translator between the OS/application and the hardware device, exposing a clean API for read/write/control operations.
* **Examples:** UART driver, I2C driver, SPI driver, ADC driver, GPIO driver, LCD driver.
* **Responsibilities:** Register-level configuration, interrupt service routine (ISR) handling, data buffering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a device driver as a **translator at the United Nations**. The application speaks "English" (high-level commands like `read_temperature()`), but the hardware only speaks "binary register toggles." The driver translates high-level requests into register-level actions.

### 1.1.5 Hardware (MCU/MPU)

The physical silicon — microcontroller or microprocessor — that executes instructions. It is the bottom-most layer and is touched only via device drivers.

---

## 1.2 Embedded Software Tool Chain

A **Tool Chain** is the integrated set of software development tools used to convert human-readable source code into a binary executable that runs on the target embedded hardware.

$$
\text{Source Code} \rightarrow \text{Compiler/Assembler} \rightarrow \text{Object Code} \rightarrow \text{Linker} \rightarrow \text{Executable} \rightarrow \text{Loader} \rightarrow \text{Target Flash/RAM}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Trivia:** A question that asks "Arrange the tools in correct order" is worth 3 marks and almost always appears. Memorize: **Compiler → Assembler → Linker → Loader → Debugger**.

### Components of the Tool Chain:

| Tool | Function | Input | Output |
|------|----------|-------|--------|
| **Compiler** | Translates HIGH-LEVEL language (C/C++) into assembly | `.c` file | `.asm` / `.s` file |
| **Assembler** | Translates assembly mnemonics into machine code (object) | `.asm` file | `.obj` file |
| **Interpreter** | Executes HIGH-LEVEL code line-by-line at runtime | `.py` / script | None (no separate output) |
| **Linker** | Combines multiple object files + libraries into ONE executable | `.obj` files | `.elf` / `.out` file |
| **Loader** | Copies the executable into target memory (Flash/RAM) for execution | `.elf` / `.hex` | Running program in memory |
| **Debugger** | Stops, steps, inspects program execution on target | Executable + target | Debug session output |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Restaurant Kitchen):**
> Imagine you write a recipe in English (source code) for a French chef.
> * **Compiler** = translates your English recipe into French instructions (assembly).
> * **Assembler** = converts French instructions into the chef's muscle movements (machine code).
> * **Linker** = combines recipes from multiple cooks into one final menu.
> * **Loader** = places the menu on the kitchen counter so the chef can start cooking.
> * **Debugger** = the head chef who tastes each dish, stops a bad one, and tells the cook to redo it step by step.

---

## 1.3 Embedded System Design Process

The **Embedded System Design Process** is a structured, systematic methodology used by engineers to transform a customer need into a fully functional, tested embedded product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3 Mandate:** The five phases are: **(1) Requirement Analysis → (2) Specification → (3) HW-SW Co-Design → (4) Module Integration → (5) Testing**.

### 1.3.1 Requirement Analysis

The process of gathering, understanding, and documenting **what the system must do** (functional requirements) and **under what constraints** (non-functional requirements like cost, power, size, timing).

* **Functional:** "System shall display temperature every 500 ms."
* **Non-Functional:** "System shall run on a coin cell battery for 1 year."

### 1.3.2 Specification Development

Translating the requirements into **precise, measurable, testable technical specifications**. Specifications answer **"HOW WELL?"** while requirements answer **"WHAT?"**

* Hardware Specification: MCU clock = 72 MHz, ADC resolution = 12-bit, supply = 3.3 V.
* Software Specification: Task period = 10 ms, response time ≤ 2 ms, code size ≤ 32 KB.

### 1.3.3 Hardware-Software Co-Design

The **concurrent design** of both hardware and software components, where system partitioning decisions (what runs in hardware vs. software) are made early to optimize performance, cost, and time-to-market.

* **Partitioning Example:** AES encryption — done in software (CPU) OR hardware (crypto accelerator)?
* **Tools Used:** SystemC, MATLAB/Simulink, LabVIEW, UML.

### 1.3.4 Module Integration

Combining individually designed and unit-tested hardware modules and software modules into a complete, working system.

* Hardware modules: Sensor board, MCU board, power board, display board.
* Software modules: Drivers, RTOS, application, middleware.

### 1.3.5 Testing

The systematic verification that the integrated system meets its original specifications.

* **Levels of Testing:** Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance.
* **Types:** Functional, Performance, Stress, Regression, Conformance, Safety.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive — Software Stack Layers

### 2.1.1 Application Layer (In-Depth)

The application layer is the **most project-specific** layer. It does NOT interact with hardware directly. Instead, it calls APIs provided by the middleware or RTOS.

**Key responsibilities:**
* Implements the core product logic (control algorithms, UI logic, state machines).
* Consumes sensor data via middleware/driver APIs.
* Publishes actuator commands via middleware/driver APIs.
* May use RTOS primitives (semaphores, queues, tasks).

**Example API call from an application:**

```c
// Application code
temperature = read_temperature_sensor(); // calls middleware/driver
if (temperature > THRESHOLD) {
    set_buzzer(ON);                      // calls middleware/driver
}
```

### 2.1.2 Middleware (In-Depth)

Middleware **abstracts** the underlying OS and hardware so the application becomes portable. If you migrate from one RTOS to another, only the middleware port changes — the application remains untouched.

**Common categories of middleware in embedded systems:**
1. **Communication protocol stacks:** TCP/IP (lwIP), CAN, BLE, Zigbee, LoRaWAN.
2. **File systems:** FATFS, LittleFS.
3. **Graphics libraries:** LVGL, TouchGFX.
4. **Database engines:** SQLite, BerkeleyDB.
5. **Security libraries:** mbedTLS, WolfSSL.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is middleware critical in IoT?** An IoT device using MQTT middleware can talk to AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud by simply changing the middleware's broker address — the application logic does not change.

### 2.1.3 Operating System / RTOS (In-Depth)

A real-time operating system guarantees **timing correctness** in addition to functional correctness. Two flavors:

* **Hard Real-Time:** Missing a deadline = SYSTEM FAILURE (e.g., anti-lock brakes, aircraft control).
* **Soft Real-Time:** Missing a deadline = DEGRADED QUALITY (e.g., video streaming).

**Core RTOS Components:**
| Component | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| **Scheduler** | Decides which task runs at any moment (preemptive, cooperative, time-slice) |
| **Task/Thread** | Independent unit of execution with its own stack |
| **Mutex** | Mutual exclusion to protect shared resources |
| **Semaphore** | Signaling/synchronization between tasks |
| **Queue** | Inter-task message passing |
| **Timer** | Periodic or one-shot software timer |
| **Memory Manager** | Allocates fixed-size memory blocks to avoid fragmentation |

### 2.1.4 Device Drivers (In-Depth)

A driver typically exposes a standard set of functions: `open()`, `close()`, `read()`, `write()`, `ioctl()`. The internals perform **register-level programming** of peripherals.

**Driver Workflow Example (UART Transmit):**
1. Application calls `uart_send(byte)`.
2. Driver checks if UART TX buffer is full.
3. Driver writes `byte` to UART data register (`USART->DR`).
4. Driver enables TX interrupt (if using DMA/IRQ).
5. On TX-complete interrupt, driver signals the application via callback/semaphore.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine driving a manual car (bare hardware) vs. an automatic (with a driver). The driver (device driver) handles the clutch, gear shifts, and RPM matching so the user just presses "go" and "stop." That's exactly what a software driver does for hardware.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Tool Chain Components

### 2.2.1 Compiler

A compiler is a **translator** that reads the entire source program, performs lexical/syntax/semantic analysis, optimizes, and emits target code (usually assembly for that specific MCU — e.g., ARM, AVR, MSP430).

**Phases of Compilation:**

$$
\text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Lexical Analysis (Tokens)} \rightarrow \text{Syntax Analysis (Parse Tree)} \rightarrow \text{Semantic Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Intermediate Code (IR)} \rightarrow \text{Optimization} \rightarrow \text{Target Code (Assembly)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Compiler in Embedded:** Most embedded tool chains use a **cross-compiler** that runs on an x86 host PC but produces code for a different target architecture (e.g., ARM Cortex-M). Example: `arm-none-eabi-gcc`.

### 2.2.2 Assembler

The assembler converts **assembly mnemonics** (e.g., `MOV R0, #5`, `ADD R1, R2`) into **machine code** (binary opcodes).

**Two-pass assembler logic:**
1. **Pass 1:** Build the **symbol table** by resolving all labels and their addresses.
2. **Pass 2:** Generate **machine code** by substituting actual addresses for labels.

**Output:** A relocatable **object file** (`.o` / `.obj`) with extension tables, symbol table, and unresolved references.

### 2.2.3 Interpreter

An interpreter executes high-level code **statement by statement at runtime**, without producing a separate executable.

| Property | Compiler | Interpreter |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| Translation Time | Before execution | During execution |
| Execution Speed | Fast (pre-compiled) | Slow (line-by-line) |
| Output | Standalone binary | No standalone output |
| Use in Embedded | C/C++ (Yes) | Python (MicroPython) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded Interpreters:** MicroPython, CircuitPython, Espruino (JavaScript) are interpreted languages running on microcontrollers. They trade execution speed for ease of prototyping.

### 2.2.4 Linker

The linker is responsible for:
1. **Resolving external references** between object files.
2. **Relocating** code and data to actual memory addresses.
3. **Combining** multiple object files + libraries into **one final executable** (`.elf` / `.out`).

**Two key concepts:**
* **Symbol Resolution:** If `main.c` calls `uart_init()` defined in `uart_driver.c`, the linker connects them.
* **Relocation:** Final addresses in Flash (e.g., `0x08000000`) and RAM (e.g., `0x20000000`) are assigned.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students confuse linker with loader. **Linker runs on HOST PC** to produce the executable. **Loader runs on TARGET** to place it in memory.

### 2.2.5 Loader

The loader is the part of the system (often a bootloader in embedded) that:
1. Takes the executable from the host.
2. Places it in the target's non-volatile memory (Flash).
3. Sets up the program counter (PC) to the entry point (`_start` / `main`).

In embedded, the "loader" is often the **In-System Programmer (ISP)** or a **Bootloader** (e.g., STM32 system bootloader, Arduino bootloader).

### 2.2.6 Debugger

A debugger is a tool that lets the developer **observe and control** program execution on the target. Two main mechanisms:

1. **JTAG / SWD Interface:** Hardware debug ports on the MCU.
2. **GDB (GNU Debugger):** Software front-end for breakpoint/single-step.

**Debug operations:**
* Set breakpoints (`break main.c:42`).
* Step line-by-line (`step`, `next`).
* Inspect variables and memory (`print var`, `x/16wx 0x20000000`).
* Watch register values.
* Trace execution flow.

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive — Design Process Phases

### 2.3.1 Requirement Analysis — Detailed

Use the **FURPS+ model** (Functional, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability) to structure requirements. Two critical types:

* **Functional Requirements (FR):** Behavior the system must exhibit.
  - *Example:* "The system shall read temperature from LM35 every 100 ms."
* **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR):** Constraints/qualities.
  - *Example:* "Power consumption ≤ 50 mW," "Operating temperature -20°C to +70°C."

### 2.3.2 Specification Development — Detailed

A **specification** is a contract that must be:
* **Unambiguous:** No room for interpretation.
* **Verifiable:** Can be tested.
* **Complete:** Covers all cases including errors.
* **Traceable:** Maps back to a requirement.

**Document types:**
* Hardware Specification: Block diagram, BOM, electrical characteristics.
* Software Specification: API contracts, task periods, state diagrams.
* Interface Specification: Pin assignments, communication protocols.

### 2.3.3 HW-SW Co-Design — Detailed

The fundamental question: **What should run in hardware and what in software?**

**Decision Criteria:**

$$
\text{Choose Hardware if: } \text{High Speed, Parallelism, Determinism, Low Power (for repeated ops)}
$$
$$
\text{Choose Software if: } \text{Flexibility, Complex Logic, Frequent Updates, Lower NRE Cost}
$$

**Co-design Tools:**
* **SystemC:** C++ library for hardware modeling.
* **Simulink + Embedded Coder:** MathWorks tool for model-based design.
* **Xilinx Vivado / Intel Quartus:** FPGA co-design with embedded soft cores.
* **UML/MARTE:** Modeling real-time embedded systems.

**Partitioning Example (Smart Sensor Node):**
| Function | Hardware | Software |
|----------|----------|----------|
| Signal amplification | Op-amp | — |
| ADC sampling | ADC peripheral | — |
| Digital filtering | — | FIR filter on CPU |
| Encryption | AES hardware accelerator | — |
| Network protocol | — | lwIP stack on CPU |
| Decision logic | — | State machine on CPU |

### 2.3.4 Module Integration — Detailed

Two integration strategies:

1. **Big-Bang Integration:** All modules combined at once and tested together. Risky.
2. **Incremental Integration:** Add one module at a time, test after each addition.
   * **Top-down:** Start with main module, add stubs for sub-modules.
   * **Bottom-up:** Start with drivers, build up to application.

> [!NOTE]
> **Best practice in KTU board answers:** Always recommend **incremental bottom-up integration** for embedded systems because drivers are hardware-dependent and need early validation.

### 2.3.5 Testing — Detailed

**V-Model Mapping:**

$$
\text{Requirements} \leftrightarrow \text{Acceptance Test} \quad ; \quad \text{System Design} \leftrightarrow \text{System Test}
$$
$$
\text{Module Design} \leftrightarrow \text{Integration Test} \quad ; \quad \text{Code} \leftrightarrow \text{Unit Test}
$$

**Testing types in embedded:**
| Test Type | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| Unit Test | Test individual functions/modules in isolation |
| Integration Test | Test interaction between modules |
| System Test | Test complete system against specifications |
| Performance Test | Timing, throughput, latency |
| Stress Test | Beyond normal operating limits |
| EMC/EMI Test | Electromagnetic compatibility (regulatory) |
| Environmental Test | Temperature, humidity, vibration |
| Regression Test | Re-test after bug fix to ensure no new bugs |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Save this table — it is exam gold for short-answer and 7-mark questions.

| Concept | Key Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---------|-------------------|--------------|
| Cross-compilation | $\text{Host (x86)} \rightarrow \text{Target (ARM/AVR)}$ | Toolchain: `arm-none-eabi-gcc` |
| Toolchain order | Source → Compiler → Assembler → Linker → Loader → Debug | Six steps |
| RTOS scheduling | $\text{Response Time} = \text{Interrupt Latency} + \text{Dispatch Time} + \text{Execution Time}$ | Milliseconds / microseconds |
| Hard real-time deadline | $\text{Missed deadline} = \text{System failure}$ | $0$ tolerance |
| Soft real-time deadline | $\text{Acceptable miss rate} \leq 1\%$ | Degraded quality |
| Memory partitioning | $\text{Flash: Code/RO} \quad ; \quad \text{RAM: Stack/Heap/Data}$ | Bytes / KB |
| Linker relocation | $\text{Final Address} = \text{Base Address} + \text{Offset}$ | Hexadecimal |
| CPU utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Where $C_i$ = computation, $T_i$ = period |
| Liu & Layland bound (Rate Monotonic) | $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | $n$ = number of tasks |
| Power estimation (CMOS) | $P = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ | Watts; reduce V, f for low power |
| Bootloader size | $\text{Bootloader} \leq 32\text{ KB}$ (STM32 typical) | Vector table at start |
| Testing coverage | $\text{Coverage} = \frac{\text{Executed Lines}}{\text{Total Lines}} \times 100\%$ | Target: $\geq 80\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The Liu & Layland bound is a **high-yield formula** for KTU questions on RTOS scheduling — write it if asked about Rate Monotonic Analysis.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example — Toolchain Flow on a Simple C Program

**Problem (KTU Model):** A developer writes the following C code for an STM32 (ARM Cortex-M) target. Trace the complete toolchain flow.

```c
// main.c
#include "uart.h"
int sum = 0;
int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
int main(void) {
    uart_init(9600);
    sum = add(5, 7);
    uart_send(sum);
    while(1);
}
```

### Step-by-Step Toolchain Trace

**Step 1 — Preprocessing**
The `#include "uart.h"` directive is resolved. The preprocessor expands macros and includes the UART header. Output: `main.i` (expanded C source).

**Step 2 — Compilation (HIGH-level → Assembly)**
The compiler (`arm-none-eabi-gcc`) translates `main.i` into ARM assembly.

$$
\text{Input: } \texttt{main.i} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{Output: } \texttt{main.s}
$$

Excerpt of generated assembly:
```asm
add:
    ADD R0, R0, R1    @ return a + b
    BX  LR
main:
    LDR R0, =9600
    BL  uart_init
    MOV R0, #5
    MOV R1, #7
    BL  add
    LDR R1, =sum
    STR R0, [R1]
    BL  uart_send
```

**Step 3 — Assembly (Assembly → Object Code)**
The assembler converts each instruction to its 32-bit ARM machine code (opcode).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{ADD R0, R0, R1} &\rightarrow \texttt{0xE0800001} \\
\texttt{BX LR} &\rightarrow \texttt{0xE12FFF1E} \\
\texttt{MOV R0, \#5} &\rightarrow \texttt{0xE3A00005}
\end{aligned}
$$

The output is a **relocatable object file** `main.o` containing:
* Machine code sections (`.text`).
* Data sections (`.data`, `.bss` for `sum`).
* **Symbol table** (e.g., `add`, `main`, `sum`, `uart_init` as undefined references).
* **Relocation entries** (placeholders for `sum` address and `uart_init`).

**Step 4 — Linking (Multiple Objects → Single Executable)**
Assume `uart.c` is compiled to `uart.o`. The linker (`arm-none-eabi-ld`) performs:

**(a) Symbol Resolution:** Finds `uart_init` defined in `uart.o` and links it with the call in `main.o`.

**(b) Relocation:** Assigns real memory addresses using a **linker script** (`STM32F4.ld`):
* `.text` (code) → Flash at `0x08000000`.
* `.data` (initialized) → RAM at `0x20000000`.
* `.bss` (zero-init) → RAM at `0x20000000` (after `.data`).

**(c) Output:** `firmware.elf` — the final executable with all symbols resolved.

**Step 5 — Loading (Executable → Target Memory)**
The loader (often a bootloader or programmer like `st-link`) writes the `.text` section into MCU Flash and copies the initial values of `.data` into RAM. The CPU reset vector jumps to `main()`.

**Step 6 — Debugging**
Using OpenOCD + GDB:
```
(gdb) target remote :3333
(gdb) break main
(gdb) continue
(gdb) print sum
```

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Rate Monotonic Scheduling (Liu & Layland)

**Problem (KTU Model):** A real-time system has 3 tasks with the following parameters:

| Task | Computation Time $C_i$ (ms) | Period $T_i$ (ms) |
|------|------------------------------|--------------------|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 10 |

**Check if the task set is schedulable using Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS).**

### Solution

**Step 1 — Assign priorities by period (shorter period = higher priority)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 = 4 \text{ ms} \rightarrow \text{Highest priority} \\
T_2 = 6 \text{ ms} \rightarrow \text{Middle priority} \\
T_3 = 10 \text{ ms} \rightarrow \text{Lowest priority}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CPU utilization**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \\
U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \\
U &= \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} \\
U &= 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.300 \\
U &= 0.883
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Liu & Layland bound for $n = 3$ tasks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{bound} &= n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \\
U_{bound} &= 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) \\
U_{bound} &= 3 \left( 1.2599 - 1 \right) \\
U_{bound} &= 3 \times 0.2599 \\
U_{bound} &= 0.7797
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare and Conclude**

$$
U = 0.883 \quad > \quad U_{bound} = 0.7797
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** Although $U < 1$, the task set **exceeds the Liu & Layland bound**, so RMS **does NOT guarantee** schedulability. We must perform an exact response time analysis to be certain.

**Step 5 — Exact Response Time Analysis (Iteration)**

For task $\tau_3$ (lowest priority), worst-case response time is:

$$
R_i^{n+1} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^n}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

Iteration 0: $R_3^0 = C_3 = 3$ ms

Iteration 1:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^1 &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\
R_3^1 &= 3 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 \\
R_3^1 &= 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Iteration 2:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^2 &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\
R_3^2 &= 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 \\
R_3^2 &= 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Iteration 3:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^3 &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 \\
R_3^3 &= 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converged at $R_3 = 7$ ms. Compare with deadline $D_3 = T_3 = 10$ ms:

$$
R_3 = 7 \text{ ms} \quad \leq \quad D_3 = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark \text{Schedulable}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The Liu & Layland bound is **sufficient but not necessary**. A task set exceeding the bound may still be schedulable — you must perform exact analysis to confirm.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — HW-SW Partitioning Decision

**Problem:** A wearable fitness tracker needs to:
1. Sample heart rate at 100 Hz from a PPG sensor.
2. Apply a 100-tap FIR filter to remove motion artifacts.
3. Detect step count using a 3-axis accelerometer at 50 Hz.
4. Display data on OLED and transmit over BLE every 1 s.

**Question:** Identify what runs in hardware vs. software. Justify each decision.

### Partitioning Table

| Function | Partition | Justification |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| PPG sensor sampling (100 Hz) | **Hardware (ADC + Timer)** | Deterministic sampling, offloads CPU |
| FIR filter (100-tap) | **Software (CPU)** | Flexibility to update filter coefficients, single thread |
| Step detection algorithm | **Software (CPU)** | Complex logic, may evolve with new ML models |
| BLE transmission | **Hardware (BLE radio)** | High-frequency, low-power radio requires dedicated silicon |
| OLED display update | **Software + Hardware DMA** | Driver uses DMA to avoid CPU blocking |
| Data buffer management | **Software (RTOS Queue)** | Needs synchronization between sensor task and BLE task |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU answers:** **Hardware handles repetitive, high-speed, low-power, time-critical tasks.** **Software handles flexible, complex, updatable logic.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Test Coverage Calculation

**Problem:** A C module has 200 lines of code. During testing, 170 lines were executed. Calculate code coverage. Comment on its adequacy for safety-critical systems (e.g., ISO 26262 automotive).

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Coverage} &= \frac{\text{Executed Lines}}{\text{Total Lines}} \times 100\% \\
\text{Coverage} &= \frac{170}{200} \times 100\% \\
\text{Coverage} &= 85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adequacy Analysis:**

| Domain | Required Coverage |
|--------|------------------|
| General embedded | $\geq 80\%$ |
| Medical (IEC 62304) | $\geq 90\%$ |
| Automotive (ISO 26262) | $\geq 100\%$ for ASIL D |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** 85% coverage is acceptable for general embedded but **NOT** for safety-critical systems like automotive airbag controllers.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between a compiler and an interpreter with two relevant examples in embedded systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Compiler | Interpreter |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| Translation | Translates ENTIRE program before execution | Translates and executes LINE-BY-LINE |
| Output | Generates standalone executable (`.elf`) | No separate executable; runs directly |
| Execution Speed | Fast (pre-compiled native code) | Slower (translation overhead at runtime) |
| Memory Footprint | Larger (full code in memory) | Smaller (one statement at a time) |
| Example (Embedded) | `gcc` for ARM Cortex-M | MicroPython on ESP32 |
| Example | `arm-none-eabi-gcc` | `CircuitPython` on SAMD21 |

> [!NOTE]
> Embedded interpreters trade raw performance for fast prototyping and ease of use.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of middleware in an embedded system with two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Middleware:**
Middleware is a software layer that sits between the application and the operating system/hardware. It provides reusable services (protocol stacks, file systems, GUI libraries) so the application developer does not have to write them from scratch. It enhances **portability** and **modularity**.

**Examples:**
1. **lwIP (lightweight IP):** A TCP/IP stack middleware used in IoT devices to enable Ethernet/Wi-Fi connectivity.
2. **FATFS:** A generic FAT file system module that allows an embedded device to read/write SD cards.
3. **mbedTLS:** A cryptographic library providing SSL/TLS for secure communication.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module 3 carries 14-mark questions. Each long answer has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** with internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the layered software architecture of an embedded system. Discuss the role of each layer in detail.**

**Model Answer:**

The layered software architecture of an embedded system is structured as a **stack**, with each layer providing services to the layer above it and consuming services from the layer below.

**Block Diagram:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         APPLICATION SOFTWARE                │
│  (Product-specific logic — motor control,   │
│   ECG processing, washing machine FSM)      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│              MIDDLEWARE                     │
│  (Protocol stacks, file systems, GUI libs)  │
│  Examples: lwIP, FATFS, mbedTLS, CAN stack  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│         OPERATING SYSTEM (RTOS)             │
│  (Task scheduling, IPC, memory management)  │
│  Examples: FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX           │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│           DEVICE DRIVERS                    │
│  (UART, SPI, I2C, ADC, GPIO, LCD drivers)  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│      HARDWARE (MCU / MPU / Peripherals)     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Role of Each Layer:**

**1. Application Software Layer:**
Contains the product-specific logic. It is the only layer unique to each product. It calls APIs exposed by the middleware or directly by drivers via the OS.

**2. Middleware Layer:**
Provides reusable, application-independent services such as communication protocols, file systems, and graphics libraries. It enables code portability across different hardware platforms.

**3. Operating System (RTOS) Layer:**
Manages system resources (CPU time, memory, peripherals) and provides deterministic, real-time task scheduling. Includes primitives like semaphores, queues, and mutexes for inter-task communication.

**4. Device Driver Layer:**
Contains software that directly manipulates hardware registers of peripherals. It translates high-level API calls (`uart_send()`) into register-level operations (`USART->DR = byte`).

**5. Hardware Layer:**
The physical silicon — microcontroller, memory, sensors, and actuators. The lowest layer that executes the binary instructions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always draw the diagram vertically with arrows showing data flow. Mentioning specific examples (FreeRTOS, lwIP) earns extra marks.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**List and explain the components of an embedded software tool chain. Draw the block diagram showing the flow of execution.**

**Model Answer:**

**Components of the Tool Chain:**

1. **Compiler:** Translates high-level language (C/C++) into assembly code specific to the target MCU. Example: `arm-none-eabi-gcc`.

2. **Assembler:** Converts assembly mnemonics into machine code (object files, `.o`). Example: `arm-none-eabi-as`.
