3. **Linker:** Combines multiple object files and libraries, resolves external references, performs relocation, and produces a single executable. Example: `arm-none-eabi-ld`.

4. **Loader:** Loads the executable into target memory (Flash/RAM) and starts execution. Embedded loaders include bootloaders and ISP programmers.

5. **Debugger:** Allows the developer to inspect and control program execution using breakpoints, single-stepping, and register/memory inspection. Uses JTAG/SWD interfaces with GDB.

6. **Interpreter (optional):** Executes high-level code line-by-line at runtime without producing a separate binary. Example: MicroPython.

**Block Diagram:**

```
   ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
   │ Compiler │ -> │Assembler │ -> │  Linker  │
   │ (.c -> .s)│    │(.s -> .o)│    │(.o -> .elf)│
   └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └─────┬────┘
                                          │
                                          v
   ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
   │ Debugger │ <- │  Target  │ <- │  Loader  │
   │ (GDB)    │    │  Memory  │    │(Bootldr) │
   └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write "linker loads the program" — WRONG. The **linker creates** the executable on the host. The **loader places** it into target memory.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the five phases of the embedded system design process with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

The embedded system design process is a structured methodology consisting of five sequential phases:

**Phase 1 — Requirement Analysis:**
Identify and document what the system must do (functional requirements) and under what constraints (non-functional). Use the FURPS+ model (Functional, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability).
* Example FR: "Display temperature every 500 ms."
* Example NFR: "Battery life ≥ 1 year."

**Phase 2 — Specification Development:**
Translate requirements into precise, measurable, testable technical specifications. Specifies MCU clock, ADC resolution, task periods, response times, memory sizes, etc.

**Phase 3 — Hardware-Software Co-Design:**
Concurrent design of HW and SW. Decide what runs in hardware (FPGA, ASIC, dedicated peripherals) and what runs in software (firmware on CPU). Optimize for performance, power, cost.

**Phase 4 — Module Integration:**
Combine individually designed and unit-tested hardware and software modules into a complete system. Use incremental bottom-up integration to minimize risk.

**Phase 5 — Testing:**
Systematic verification against specifications. Includes unit, integration, system, performance, and acceptance testing. May use the V-Model to map each design phase to its corresponding test phase.

**Block Diagram (V-Model Style):**

```
  Requirements ────────────► Acceptance Test
       │                          ▲
       ▼                          │
  Specifications ─────────► System Test
       │                          ▲
       ▼                          │
  HW/SW Co-Design ────────► Integration Test
       │                          ▲
       ▼                          │
  Module Integration ──────► Unit Test
       │                          ▲
       ▼                          │
    Testing ──────────────────────┘
```

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss Hardware-Software Co-Design in detail. Explain the partitioning decisions with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Hardware-Software Co-Design is the **concurrent design** of hardware and software components of an embedded system, where partitioning decisions (HW vs. SW) are made early to optimize for performance, cost, power, and time-to-market.

**Why Co-Design is Needed:**
* Traditional sequential design (HW first, then SW) is slow and may lead to suboptimal partitioning.
* Modern embedded products (smartphones, IoT, automotive ECUs) demand tight HW-SW integration.

**Partitioning Decision Criteria:**

| Criterion | Prefer Hardware | Prefer Software |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Speed | Required | Tolerable |
| Power (repetitive ops) | Lower (dedicated) | Higher (CPU active) |
| Flexibility | Low (fixed function) | High (updatable) |
| NRE Cost | High (mask) | Low (compile/flash) |
| Unit Cost | Low (after NRE) | High (CPU + memory) |
| Parallelism | Naturally parallel | Sequential |

**Example: Smart Camera System**

| Function | Partition | Reason |
|----------|-----------|--------|
| Image sensor capture | **Hardware** (CMOS sensor + ISP) | High-speed parallel pixel data |
| JPEG compression | **Hardware** (dedicated encoder) | Real-time, low-power |
| Face detection algorithm | **Software** (CPU) | Evolving ML model, needs updates |
| User interface / display | **Software** (firmware) | Frequent UI changes |
| Storage (SD card write) | **Hardware DMA + Software driver** | DMA offloads CPU |
| Network streaming (Wi-Fi) | **Hardware** (Wi-Fi module) | RF requires dedicated silicon |

**Co-Design Tools:** SystemC, MATLAB/Simulink, LabVIEW, UML/MARTE, Xilinx Vivado HLS.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Phrase to Use:** "Hardware-Software partitioning is the most critical decision in co-design because it directly impacts performance, power, cost, and time-to-market."

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
> 
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 
> 1. **Toolchain Order Confusion:** Writing "Linker → Compiler → Assembler" — WRONG ORDER. Correct: **Compiler → Assembler → Linker → Loader**.
> 
> 2. **Loader vs. Linker:** Linker runs on the **host PC** to create the executable. Loader runs on the **target** to place it in memory. These are NOT the same.
> 
> 3. **Compiler Output:** Many students think the compiler produces the final `.hex` file. WRONG. The compiler produces assembly. The assembler produces object. The linker produces executable.
> 
> 4. **HW-SW Co-Design Misconception:** Co-design does NOT mean "design HW and SW in parallel" only — it specifically includes the **partitioning decision** of which functions go to HW vs. SW.
> 
> 5. **Testing Levels Skipped:** Students often mention only "system testing" without distinguishing unit, integration, and acceptance tests. KTU expects all levels.
> 
> 6. **No Examples Given:** A 7-mark question without specific examples (e.g., FreeRTOS, lwIP, `arm-none-eabi-gcc`) usually loses 1-2 marks. Always cite at least one real-world example.
> 
> 7. **Vague Definitions:** Writing "Middleware is software" without explaining its role between application and OS — partial credit at best.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Exam!**

### ✅ Software Stack Layers
- **Application:** Product-specific logic, top of stack, uses middleware/RTOS APIs.
- **Middleware:** Reusable services (lwIP, FATFS, mbedTLS), enhances portability.
- **OS/RTOS:** Manages resources, provides determinism (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX).
- **Device Drivers:** Translate high-level API to register-level operations.
- **Hardware:** Physical MCU/MPU and peripherals.

### ✅ Toolchain Order (HIGH-YIELD!)
- **Order:** Source → **Compiler** (C → asm) → **Assembler** (asm → object) → **Linker** (object → .elf) → **Loader** (place in memory) → **Debugger** (inspect execution).
- **Cross-compiler:** Runs on x86 host, targets ARM/AVR/MSP430.
- **Two-pass assembler:** Pass 1 builds symbol table, Pass 2 emits machine code.
- **Linker does:** Symbol resolution + Relocation.
- **Loader does:** Places program in Flash/RAM, sets PC to entry point.

### ✅ Design Process Phases
- **Five Phases:** Requirement Analysis → Specification → HW-SW Co-Design → Module Integration → Testing.
- **FURPS+ Model:** Functional, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability.
- **V-Model:** Each design phase maps to a corresponding test phase.
- **Co-Design Rule:** Hardware = speed/power, Software = flexibility/updatability.
- **Integration Strategy:** **Incremental bottom-up** is best for embedded.

### ✅ High-Yield Formulas
- $\text{CPU Utilization } U = \sum \frac{C_i}{T_i}$
- $\text{Liu \& Layland Bound: } U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$
- $\text{Code Coverage} = \frac{\text{Executed Lines}}{\text{Total Lines}} \times 100\%$
- $\text{Response Time} = \text{Interrupt Latency} + \text{Dispatch Time} + \text{Execution Time}$

### ✅ Must-Remember Examples
- **RTOS:** FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, μC/OS-II, RTEMS.
- **Middleware:** lwIP, FATFS, mbedTLS, LVGL, CAN stack.
- **Cross-compilers:** `arm-none-eabi-gcc`, `avr-gcc`, `msp430-gcc`.
- **Debug tools:** GDB, OpenOCD, JTAG, SWD.

### ✅ Key Terminology
- **Hard real-time:** Missed deadline = system failure.
- **Soft real-time:** Missed deadline = degraded quality.
- **Bootloader:** Embedded equivalent of a loader.
- **JTAG/SWD:** Hardware debug interfaces on MCUs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, ALWAYS start with a labeled block diagram (2-3 marks), then list components with definitions (4-5 marks), provide examples (1-2 marks), and conclude with a real-world application (1 mark). This structure is exactly what KTU examiners reward.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Board Study (Board Level Block Schematic) - Chip (Features Only - Architecture Not Needed)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Board Study (Board Level Block Schematic) - Chip (Features Only - Architecture Not Needed)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Development Board (Evaluation Board / Starter Kit)** is a printed circuit board (PCB) that integrates a target **microcontroller chip** (here, the NXP **LPC2148**) along with all essential **support circuitry** — clock, reset, power regulation, communication interfaces, I/O peripherals, and debugging connectors — required to rapidly prototype, test, and validate embedded applications without the designer having to build a custom board from scratch.

The **Board-Level Block Schematic** is a high-level functional diagram that depicts the major hardware blocks of the board, the interconnections between the **chip** and **on-board peripherals**, and the **external I/O headers**, without diving into the internal CPU/ALU/pipeline architecture of the chip itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Scope Clarification (Dec 2024 Scheme):**
> For this topic, the student is expected to know **only the features** of the LPC2148 chip (memory size, peripheral count, bus width, etc.). The **internal CPU architecture** (pipeline stages, register file, AMBA bus matrix, AHB/APB bridges) is **NOT** part of this section. It is a "chip datasheet snapshot" view, not a "core architecture" view.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the development board as a **fully-furnished studio apartment** and the LPC2148 chip as the **central air-conditioning unit** inside it.

- The **chip (LPC2148)** is the "brain" — it processes instructions, but it cannot work in isolation. It needs power, a heartbeat (clock), a way to communicate with the outside, and devices to control.
- The **board** is the apartment — it provides power sockets (voltage regulators), a wall clock (crystal oscillator), a doorbell (UART/USB), windows (GPIO/headers), a thermometer (ADC), a music system (DAC/PWM), and even a security guard (Reset + Watchdog).
- The **block schematic** is the **floor plan** — it doesn't show the wiring inside the walls, just where each functional block sits and how it connects to the central unit.

So when KTU asks you to "draw the board level block schematic and list the chip features", they want:
1. A **floor plan** (which peripherals exist on the board and how they tap into the chip).
2. A **spec sheet of the AC unit** (chip's memory, peripherals, speed, package).

### 1.3 Key Physical & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V** (with **5 V** tolerant I/O on most pins)
- **CPU Core:** **ARM7TDMI-S** (32-bit, von Neumann-style modified Harvard)
- **Max CPU Clock:** **60 MHz**
- **Flash Memory:** **512 KB** on-chip
- **SRAM:** **40 KB** on-chip (32 KB + 8 KB USB accessible)
- **I/O Pin Count:** up to **45 GPIO** (5 V tolerant)
- **ADC Resolution:** **10-bit**, up to **14 analog input channels**
- **DAC Resolution:** **10-bit**, **1 channel**
- **Package:** **LQFP64** (64-pin Low-profile Quad Flat Pack)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip:** Remember the "**512-40-60**" mnemonic for KTU exams — *Flash 512 KB, SRAM 40 KB, Clock 60 MHz*. This is a high-yield board question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary Sheet

### 2.1 Development Board — Major Functional Blocks (Theory Breakdown)

A typical **LPC2148 development board** integrates the following functional blocks. The "**Why**" and "**How**" are explained below for each, as KTU frequently tests the *reasoning* behind block selection.

| # | Block on the Board | Why it Exists (The "Why") | How it Interacts with the Chip |
|---|--------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Power Supply Section** (7805 + LM1117-3.3 or direct 3.3 V regulator) | Converts external **+5 V / +9 V DC** adapter to clean **+3.3 V** required by the chip | Supplies **Vdd/Vss** pins; decoupling capacitors filter noise |
| 2 | **Crystal Oscillator** (typically **12.000 MHz**) | Provides a stable reference frequency; required because LPC2148 lacks an internal high-accuracy oscillator for PLL | Drives **XTAL1** and **XTAL2** pins; internal PLL multiplies it up to 60 MHz |
| 3 | **Reset Circuit** (RC network + push-button + 74HC125 or manual) | Forces the chip into a known initial state on power-up or on user demand | Connected to **RESET** pin (active LOW) |
| 4 | **JTAG / SWD Debug Connector** (20-pin IDC header) | Allows in-circuit programming and step-by-step debugging via tools like **Keil ULINK2** or **J-Link** | Taps **TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST** pins |
| 5 | **LED Indicators** (typically 8 LEDs) | Visual status indicators for GPIO testing | Connected to **P0.0 – P0.7** (or similar) via current-limiting resistors |
| 6 | **Push-Button Switches** (typically 4–8) | Digital inputs to test GPIO polling / interrupts | Connected to **P0.15 – P0.18** typically, debounced via hardware or software |
| 7 | **16×2 LCD Interface Header** | For alphanumeric display of data | Maps to **P0.0–P0.7** (data) and **P0.8, P0.9** (RS, EN) |
| 8 | **UART0 / UART1 Connectors** (DB9 + MAX232 level shifter) | Serial communication with PC (RS-232) or with another MCU | **P0.0 (TXD0), P0.1 (RXD0)**; MAX232 converts **3.3 V TTL ↔ ±12 V RS232** |
| 9 | **USB 2.0 Connector** (Type-B + on-chip PHY) | Full-speed USB device communication with PC | Uses **D+, D−** pins (P0.23, P0.24 soft-connect via P0.31) |
| 10 | **I²C Header** (with pull-up resistors **4.7 kΩ**) | To connect EEPROM, RTC, sensors (e.g., LM75, DS1307) | **P0.2 (SCL0), P0.3 (SDA0)** |
| 11 | **SPI Header** | For high-speed synchronous peripherals (SD card, MMC) | **P0.4 (SCK0), P0.5 (MISO0), P0.6 (MOSI0), P0.7 (SSEL0)** |
| 12 | **ADC Input Header** (potentiometer + test pins) | Analog signal input (e.g., from sensor or variable resistor) | **P0.28–P0.30, P0.25–P0.27** (AD0.0–AD0.4) |
| 13 | **DAC Output Header** | Generate analog waveform (sine, audio) | **P0.25 (AOUT)** |
| 14 | **PWM Output Header** | Motor speed control, LED dimming | **PWM1–PWM6** on **P0.0 / P0.7 / P0.1 / P0.8 / P0.21 / P0.9** |
| 15 | **Buzzer / Speaker** | Audible output | Driven by a PWM or GPIO via a transistor driver |
| 16 | **Expansion Headers** (male berg strip on all GPIO) | Access to every pin of Port 0 and Port 1 for custom interfacing | Mirrors all P0.x and P1.x pins |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Schematic Memorization Hack:** Group the board blocks into **4 quadrants** for exam recall:
> 1. **Power & Clock** (Regulator, Crystal, Reset, Decoupling caps)
> 2. **Digital I/O** (LEDs, Switches, LCD, Buzzer, Expansion header)
> 3. **Communication** (UART, USB, I²C, SPI, JTAG)
> 4. **Analog** (ADC input, DAC output, PWM)

### 2.2 Chip Features Table — LPC2148 (Without Architecture)

This is the **high-yield KTU 2024 feature table**. Memorize this — board questions often ask to "list the salient features of LPC2148" for 7 marks.

| Feature Category | Specification | Value / Count |
|------------------|---------------|---------------|
| Core | ARM7TDMI-S | 32-bit RISC |
| Max Clock | From PLL | **60 MHz** |
| On-chip Flash | In-system programmable | **512 KB** |
| On-chip SRAM | Total | **40 KB** (32 KB general + 8 KB USB) |
| GPIO | 5 V tolerant | Up to **45 pins** (Port 0 + Port 1) |
| UART | 16-byte FIFO | **2** (UART0, UART1) |
| I²C | Standard / Fast mode | **2** (I²C0, I²C1) |
| SPI / SSP | Synchronous serial | **1 SPI + 1 SSP** |
| Timers | 32-bit, with capture/match registers | **2** (T0, T1) |
| PWM | 32-bit, single-edge / double-edge | **6 outputs** (PWM1–PWM6) |
| ADC | 10-bit, ≥ 2.44 µs conversion | **8 channels** (some boards expose 14) |
| DAC | 10-bit, current-mode output | **1 channel** |
| RTC | With separate power domain | **Yes** |
| Watchdog Timer | Locked once enabled | **Yes** (WDT) |
| USB | 2.0 Full-speed (12 Mbps), with on-chip PHY | **1** |
| Interrupt Controller | Vectored IRQ (VIC) | **16 vectored IRQ slots** |
| PLL | Input 10–25 MHz, Output up to 60 MHz | **Yes** |
| Power Modes | Idle, Power-down, Deep Power-down | **3** |
| Operating Voltage | Core and I/O | **3.0 – 3.6 V** (3.3 V typical) |
| Package | LQFP | **64-pin** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Note:** The datasheet says **"up to 14 ADC channels"** because some are multiplexed with digital I/O. The **board typically exposes 6–8 channels** through the analog input header. Quote **"14 analog inputs"** in exam, but mention **"6–8 are usually accessible on the board"** for accuracy.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Education:** LPC2148 board is the de-facto learning platform for ARM7 in Indian universities.
- **Industrial Prototyping:** USB device firmware (HID, CDC), sensor data loggers, motor controllers.
- **Cost-Sensitive Embedded Products:** Legacy designs in printers, point-of-sale terminals, low-end industrial controllers.
- **Academic Research:** Real-time OS porting (FreeRTOS, μC/OS-II) on the board.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis: Tracing Signal Flow on the Board

This section is **not a mathematical derivation**, but a **signal-tracing walk-through** of the block schematic — equivalent in rigor to a derivation because every connection must be justified pin-by-pin.

### 3.1 Power-Up Signal Flow (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** External **+9 V DC adapter** → plugged into **DC barrel jack** on the board.

**Step 2:** Adapter voltage → fed to **LM7805** (linear regulator) → output becomes a stable **+5 V DC**.

**Step 3:** The **+5 V** line is further fed to **LM1117-3.3** → output becomes **+3.3 V** (the chip's core and I/O voltage).

**Step 4:** The **+3.3 V** rail goes to **Vdd (Pin 23)** and **Vss (Pin 8, 19, 43, 50)** of the LPC2148. Decoupling capacitors (typically **100 nF ceramic + 10 µF electrolytic**) are placed close to each Vdd pin to suppress noise transients.

**Step 5:** Simultaneously, a **Power-On-Reset (POR)** RC network (typically **10 kΩ + 1 µF** with $\tau = RC$) charges up.

**Step 6:** When the capacitor charges to ≈ 0.8 × Vdd, the internal POR circuit releases the chip from reset. The chip begins fetching its vector table from address **0x00000000** (top of on-chip Flash, by default).

### 3.2 Clock Generation Signal Flow

**Step 1:** **12.000 MHz crystal** is connected between **XTAL1 (Pin 22)** and **XTAL2 (Pin 21)** of LPC2148, with two load capacitors (typically **18 pF – 33 pF**) to ground.

**Step 2:** On power-up, the internal **oscillator circuit** amplifies the crystal oscillation, producing a stable **12 MHz** square wave.

**Step 3:** This 12 MHz is fed to the **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** block inside the chip. The PLL multiplies this frequency by a programmable integer (M) and divides by a divisor (P) to produce **CCLK (CPU Clock)**.

**Step 4:** For **60 MHz** operation, the typical PLL setting is **M = 5, P = 2**:

$$
f_{CCLK} \;=\; f_{OSC} \times \frac{M}{P} \;=\; 12 \text{ MHz} \times \frac{5}{2} \;=\; 60 \text{ MHz}
$$

> **Logic check:** $\frac{12 \times 5}{2} = 30 \times 1 = 30$... wait, that yields 30 MHz.
>
> **Correction:** The LPC2148 PLL uses a different formula. The actual formula is:

$$
f_{CCLK} \;=\; f_{OSC} \times M
$$

where M is the **PLL multiplier**. To get 60 MHz from 12 MHz, set **M = 5** directly. The **PLL Feed sequence** must be written to PLLFEED register to commit the change.

**Step 5:** The Peripheral Clock (**PCLK**) is derived from CCLK by a **VPB divider** (1, 2, 4, or 8). The default divider is 4, giving:

$$
f_{PCLK} \;=\; \frac{f_{CCLK}}{4} \;=\; \frac{60 \text{ MHz}}{4} \;=\; 15 \text{ MHz}
$$

This **PCLK = 15 MHz** is what timers, UART, ADC, and most peripherals see.

### 3.3 Reset and Debug Signal Flow

**Step 1:** **RESET** pin (Pin 57) is held LOW at power-up by the RC network.

**Step 2:** As the capacitor charges above the **VIL (Input Low Voltage ≈ 0.8 V)** threshold, the chip exits reset and starts execution.

**Step 3:** The **JTAG connector** allows an external debugger to take control of the chip's **Test Access Port (TAP)** controller. The four mandatory signals are:

$$
\{\text{TCK}, \text{TMS}, \text{TDI}, \text{TDO}\} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{chip pins } 11, 12, 14, 15
$$

**Step 4:** The debugger resets the chip via **TRST (Pin 13)** and then drives the boundary-scan chain to perform in-circuit programming and stepping.

### 3.4 Communication Bus Mapping Table (Pin-Level)

| Bus/Interface | Chip Pin | Port Pin | Board Connector |
|---------------|----------|----------|------------------|
| UART0 TX | Pin 19 (P0.0) | P0.0 | DB9 Pin 3 (via MAX232) |
| UART0 RX | Pin 21 (P0.1) | P0.1 | DB9 Pin 2 (via MAX232) |
| I²C0 SCL | Pin 22 (P0.2) | P0.2 | I²C header (4.7 kΩ pull-up) |
| I²C0 SDA | Pin 26 (P0.3) | P0.3 | I²C header (4.7 kΩ pull-up) |
| SPI0 SCK | Pin 27 (P0.4) | P0.4 | SPI header |
| SPI0 MISO | Pin 28 (P0.5) | P0.5 | SPI header |
| SPI0 MOSI | Pin 29 (P0.6) | P0.6 | SPI header |
| USB D+ | Pin 10 (P0.23) | P0.23 | USB Type-B Pin 3 |
| USB D− | Pin 11 (P0.24) | P0.24 | USB Type-B Pin 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin numbering note:** Pin numbers above are for **LQFP64** package. KTU sometimes uses **PDIP (Pins 1–64, the same physical numbering)** or the **port-based naming P0.x**. Always specify *both* in answers for full credit.

### 3.5 Summary of Board Signal Flow Logic

The general pattern on the board is:
**External Stimulus → Connector/Header → Level Shifter/Buffer → Chip Pin → Internal Peripheral → Software**

For example, an analog sensor connected to the **ADC header** produces 0–3.3 V → enters **AD0.1 (P0.28)** → the 10-bit ADC digitizes it (resolution = 3.3 V / 1024 ≈ 3.22 mV per LSB) → result stored in **AD0DR1 register** → read by firmware.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: LPC2148 Development Board Architecture

The diagram below shows the **board-level block schematic** with the LPC2148 chip at the center and its on-board peripherals around it. The arrows indicate **signal flow direction** (data/control lines).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[DC Adapter 9V] --> REG7805[LM7805 5V Regulator]
    REG7805 --> REG1117[LM1117 3.3V Regulator]
    REG1117 --> CHIPLPC[LPC2148 ARM7TDMI-S CHIP]

    XTAL[12 MHz Crystal Oscillator] --> CHIPLPC
    RESET[Reset RC Network with Push Button] --> CHIPLPC
    JTAG[JTAG 20-pin Debug Header] --> CHIPLPC

    CHIPLPC -- P0.0 to P0.7 --> LEDBAR[8 LED Indicators]
    CHIPLPC -- P0.15 to P0.18 --> SWBAR[4 Push Button Switches]
    CHIPLPC -- P0.8 P0.9 P0.10 to P0.15 --> LCDPORT[16x2 LCD Header]
    CHIPLPC -- P0.0 P0.1 via MAX232 --> DB9[UART0 DB9 Connector]
    CHIPLPC -- P0.23 P0.24 --> USBPORT[USB Type B Connector]
    CHIPLPC -- P0.2 P0.3 with 4.7k pullup --> I2CHDR[I2C Header]
    CHIPLPC -- P0.4 P0.5 P0.6 P0.7 --> SPIHDR[SPI Header]

    CHIPLPC -- P0.28 to P0.30 --> ADCPOT[ADC Potentiometer Input]
    CHIPLPC -- P0.25 --> DACOUT[DAC Output BNC]
    CHIPLPC -- PWM1 to PWM6 --> PWMOUT[PWM Output Header]
    CHIPLPC -- GPIO via transistor --> BUZZ[Buzzer]

    CHIPLPC --> EXPIO[Expansion Headers for all P0 and P1 pins]
    CHIPLPC --> FLASHMEM[On chip 512 KB Flash]
    CHIPLPC --> SRAMMEM[On chip 40 KB SRAM]
    CHIPLPC --> VICBLK[Vectored Interrupt Controller VIC]
    CHIPLPC --> PLLLP[PLL Block 60 MHz]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram Legend & Reading Guide

- **Solid arrows** → physical PCB trace / wire connection.
- **Central yellow box (CHIPLPC)** → the LPC2148 chip.
- **Top row** → power, clock, reset, debug infrastructure (the "**lifeline**" of the board).
- **Left column** → digital I/O (LEDs, switches, LCD, buzzer).
- **Right column** → communication peripherals (UART, USB, I²C, SPI).
- **Bottom row** → analog and timing peripherals (ADC, DAC, PWM).
- **Right-side expansion** → on-chip memory and core support (Flash, SRAM, VIC, PLL).

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic of Pin Groupings (For Quick Exam Recall)

```
                +---------------------------+
   Vdd 3.3V --- | 23                       57| --- RESET
   Vss GND  --- |  8  LPC2148 (LQFP64)    19| --- P0.0 (TXD0)
   XTAL1   --- | 21                       11| --- P0.23 (USB D+)
   XTAL2   --- | 22                       12| --- P0.24 (USB D-)
   P0.1 RX --- |  2                       10| --- P0.2 (SCL0)
   P0.3 SD --- |  3                       27| --- P0.4 (SCK0)
   P0.5 MI --- |  4                       28| --- P0.6 (MOSI0)
                +---------------------------+
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board diagram questions, always draw the **chip as a central block** with **functional groupings** of pins (Power, Communication, Analog, Digital I/O) — this matches the official board schematic style and earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any six salient features of the LPC2148 microcontroller.*

**Model Answer:**

The LPC2148 is a 32-bit ARM7TDMI-S based microcontroller. The salient features are:

1. **CPU Core:** 32-bit ARM7TDMI-S, operating at a maximum clock of **60 MHz**.
2. **Memory:** **512 KB** on-chip in-system programmable Flash and **40 KB** on-chip SRAM (32 KB + 8 KB USB accessible).
3. **Communication Peripherals:** Two **UARTs**, two **I²C** buses (fast-mode, 400 kHz), one **SPI** and one **SSP** controller.
4. **USB 2.0:** One full-speed (12 Mbps) USB device port with **on-chip PHY**.
5. **Analog:** A 10-bit **ADC** with up to 14 analog input channels and a 10-bit **DAC** with one output channel.
6. **Timers/PWM:** Two **32-bit timers** with capture/match registers, and a **6-channel PWM** unit for motor control.
7. **GPIO:** Up to **45 general-purpose I/O pins**, all 5 V tolerant.
8. **Other:** Real-Time Clock (RTC), Watchdog Timer, Vectored Interrupt Controller (VIC) with 16 vectored IRQ slots, and three power-saving modes (Idle, Power-down, Deep Power-down).

*(Any 6 features — ½ mark each, neatly listed.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the function of the crystal oscillator and the MAX232 level shifter in an LPC2148 development board.*

**Model Answer:**

**Crystal Oscillator (12 MHz):**
- **Function:** Provides a precise and stable **external reference clock** to the LPC2148 chip via its **XTAL1 and XTAL2** pins.
- The 12 MHz signal is fed to the internal **PLL**, which multiplies it to generate the **CCLK (60 MHz)** and **PCLK** for the CPU and peripherals respectively.
- Without a crystal, the chip has no heartbeat and cannot execute instructions correctly.

**MAX232 Level Shifter:**
- **Function:** Converts the **3.3 V TTL logic levels** of the LPC2148's UART pins to the **±12 V RS-232 logic levels** required by the standard **DB9 serial port** of a PC.
- It also performs the reverse conversion from PC → LPC2148.
- The MAX232 works with four external **1 µF charge-pump capacitors** to generate the required ±10 V rails from a single **+5 V** supply.
- It is essentially a **logic-level bridge** between the chip and the PC's COM port.

*(2 marks: 1 for crystal function, 1 for MAX232 function. 1 mark for technical detail.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model Paper – Dec 2024, Module 4]*

**a)** With the help of a neat block schematic, explain the major functional blocks of an **LPC2148 development board**. **(7 marks)**

**b)** List any **14 salient features** of the LPC2148 microcontroller chip. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

A typical LPC2148 development board consists of the following major functional blocks, grouped as shown:

**1. Power Supply Section (1 mark)**
- A DC barrel jack accepts **+9 V** input.
- An **LM7805** regulator converts it to **+5 V**.
- An **LM1117-3.3** regulator further converts +5 V to **+3.3 V** for the chip's Vdd.
- Decoupling capacitors (100 nF + 10 µF) suppress noise near the Vdd pins.

**2. Clock Section (1 mark)**
- A **12.000 MHz crystal** with two load capacitors (18–33 pF) is connected between **XTAL1** and **XTAL2** of the LPC2148.
- The internal PLL multiplies this to generate the **60 MHz CCLK**.

**3. Reset Section (0.5 mark)**
- An **RC network** (10 kΩ + 1 µF) holds the **RESET** pin LOW on power-up, then releases it to start execution.
- A manual **push button** allows user-triggered reset.

**4. Debug / Programming Section (0.5 mark)**
- A **20-pin JTAG header** connects TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST to an external debugger (Keil ULINK2 / J-Link).

**5. Digital I/O Section (1.5 marks)**
- **8 LEDs** (with 470 Ω series resistors) connected to **P0.0–P0.7** for visual output.
- **4–8 push buttons** (with pull-ups) connected to **P0.15–P0.18** for digital input.
- A **16×2 LCD header** mapped to **P0.8–P0.15** for alphanumeric display.
- A **buzzer** (via driver transistor) on a GPIO pin for audible output.

**6. Communication Section (1.5 marks)**
- **UART0** routed through a **MAX232** level shifter to a **DB9 connector** (using P0.0 TXD0, P0.1 RXD0).
- **USB 2.0** Type-B connector using **P0.23 (D+)** and **P0.24 (D−)**.
- **I²C header** with **4.7 kΩ pull-up resistors** on SCL0/SDA0 (P0.2, P0.3).
- **SPI header** with SCK0/MISO0/MOSI0/SSEL0 (P0.4–P0.7).

**7. Analog & PWM Section (0.5 mark)**
- **ADC input header** with a potentiometer and test pins for analog inputs (e.g., P0.28–P0.30).
- **DAC output BNC** connector for analog waveform output (P0.25).
- **PWM output header** for the 6 PWM channels (PWM1–PWM6).

**8. Expansion Headers (0.5 mark)**
- Berg strip headers exposing **all P0.x and P1.x pins** for custom interfacing.

A block diagram should be drawn showing the LPC2148 chip at the center with all the above sections connected to it via labeled arrows.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

| # | Feature | Specification |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Core | ARM7TDMI-S (32-bit RISC) |
| 2 | Max Clock | 60 MHz |
| 3 | Flash | 512 KB |
| 4 | SRAM | 40 KB |
| 5 | GPIO | 45 (5 V tolerant) |
| 6 | UART | 2 |
| 7 | I²C | 2 |
| 8 | SPI / SSP | 1 + 1 |
| 9 | Timers | 2 × 32-bit |
| 10 | PWM | 6 outputs |
| 11 | ADC | 10-bit, up to 14 channels |
| 12 | DAC | 10-bit, 1 channel |
| 13 | USB | USB 2.0 Full-speed with on-chip PHY |
| 14 | RTC | Yes (with separate power pin) |
| 15 | Watchdog Timer | Yes |
| 16 | VIC | Vectored, 16 IRQ slots |
| 17 | PLL | Yes (up to 60 MHz) |
| 18 | Package | LQFP64 |
| 19 | Operating Voltage | 3.0 – 3.6 V |
| 20 | Power Modes | Idle, Power-down, Deep Power-down |

*(½ mark each × 14 = 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice for Same Module]*

**a)** Draw the **board-level block schematic** of the LPC2148 development board and explain the function of any **three on-board communication blocks**. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Differentiate between **UART and USB** interfaces of the LPC2148 in terms of **speed, signal type, voltage level, and use-case**. State the chip pins used for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

*(Drawing — 4 marks; Explanation — 1.5 marks × 2 communication blocks = 3 marks.)*

**Block Diagram** (must show LPC2148 chip in center with labelled arrows to):
- UART0 → MAX232 → DB9 connector
- USB D+/D− → Type-B USB port
- I²C0 SCL/SDA → header with 4.7 kΩ pull-ups
- SPI0 → header
- JTAG → 20-pin header

**Function of Three Communication Blocks:**

**1. UART0 Block (1.5 marks)**
- The **UART0** block enables **asynchronous serial communication** with a PC or another microcontroller.
- The chip's TXD0 (P0.0) and RXD0 (P0.1) pins operate at **3.3 V TTL levels**.
- A **MAX232** IC converts these to **±12 V RS-232 levels** to drive the **DB9** connector.
- Default baud rate: 9600 bps; programmable up to 115200 bps in LPC2148.
- **Use case:** Sending sensor data to a PC's HyperTerminal / Tera Term.

**2. USB 2.0 Block (1.5 marks)**
- The **USB Device** port (with **on-chip PHY**) provides **full-speed (12 Mbps)** communication.
- Uses **D+ (P0.23)** and **D− (P0.24)** lines plus a **1.5 kΩ pull-up** on D+ to signal "device attached" to the host.
- The **Vbus** pin receives 5 V from the host, used for soft-connect detection.
- **Use case:** Emulating a USB HID device (mouse/keyboard) or CDC (virtual COM port).

**3. I²C Block (1.5 marks)**
- The **I²C0** bus is a **2-wire synchronous** interface (**SCL + SDA**) for short-distance, low-speed peripherals.
- Both **SCL (P0.2)** and **SDA (P0.3)** are open-drain and require **external pull-up resistors (4.7 kΩ)** to Vdd.
- Supports **Standard mode (100 kHz)** and **Fast mode (400 kHz)**.
- **Use case:** Interfacing **EEPROM (24Cxx)**, **RTC (DS1307)**, or **temperature sensor (LM75)**.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

| Parameter | UART0 | USB 2.0 (Device) |
|-----------|-------|------------------|
| **Speed** | Programmable, typically up to 115200 bps (max ≈ 1 Mbps with oversampling) | 12 Mbps (Full-speed) |
| **Signal Type** | Asynchronous, full-duplex | Synchronous, half-duplex (with packet-based protocol) |
| **Voltage Level** | 3.3 V TTL (chip-side); ±12 V RS232 (after MAX232) | Differential 3.3 V on D+/D− |
| **Number of Wires** | 2 (TXD, RXD) | 2 data lines (D+, D−) + Vbus + GND |
| **Topology** | Point-to-point | Star (host + multiple devices via hub) |
| **Max Devices** | 1-to-1 | Up to 127 devices |
| **Use-Case** | Debug logging, GPS module, GSM module, point-to-point MCU link | PC ↔ Device communication: mass storage, HID, CDC, DFU programming |
| **LPC2148 Pins** | P0.0 (TXD0), P0.1 (RXD0) | P0.23 (D+), P0.24 (D−) |
| **Clock Source** | Programmable baud-rate generator (from PCLK) | 48 MHz internal clock (derived from PLL) for USB |
| **PHY** | None (logic-level only) | **On-chip USB PHY** (no external PHY IC needed) |

*(3.5 marks for the comparison table; 2 marks for correct pin identification; 1.5 marks for use-case clarity.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Board-Level Schematic:**
>
> 1. **Don't draw internal CPU architecture** (ALU, decoder, pipeline, AMBA bus) — that's for Module 3, not this topic. KTU deducts **2 marks** if you confuse "chip features" with "core architecture."
> 2. **Always label the chip as the central block** with named arrows to peripheral blocks. A "loose collection of blocks without a central chip" is treated as incomplete — **–3 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting MAX232** when describing UART communication is a very common mistake. Examiners explicitly look for "level shifting" in UART description. **–1 mark** if missing.
> 4. **Voltage confusion:** LPC2148 runs on **3.3 V**, NOT 5 V. Students often write "5 V" — this is a factual error and costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **Pin numbering ambiguity:** Always mention both the **LQFP64 pin number** AND the **P0.x port name**. Writing only "P0.0" is considered incomplete in board diagrams.
> 6. **Crystal frequency:** KTU's standard board uses **12 MHz** crystal. Writing "11.0592 MHz" (8051 frequency) by mistake is a giveaway of rote memorization without understanding — **–1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The board is NOT the chip.** Board = chip + supporting circuitry. Chip = just the silicon.
- **LPC2148** is the canonical chip for KTU Module 4 board study. Know its **"512-40-60"** triplet: *Flash 512 KB, SRAM 40 KB, Clock 60 MHz*.
- **Supply voltage is 3.3 V** (NOT 5 V). I/O pins are **5 V tolerant** but core is 3.3 V.
- **Three power modes:** Idle, Power-down, Deep Power-down.
- **Two UARTs, two I²C, one SPI, one SSP, one USB, two timers, six PWM, 10-bit ADC, 10-bit DAC** — memorize this peripheral count.
- **MAX232** is a **level shifter**, not a protocol converter. It only changes voltage levels.
- **Crystal** (12 MHz) is multiplied by **PLL** to generate CCLK = 60 MHz; PCLK = CCLK/4 = 15 MHz by default.
- **JTAG** is for **debug + flash programming**; uses TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO, TRST signals.
- **Board sections** are: Power, Clock, Reset, Debug, Digital I/O, Communication, Analog/PWM, Expansion.
- **Architecture is NOT** part of this section — only **features** of the chip.
- **LQFP64 package** has **45 GPIO** pins distributed across **Port 0 (P0.0–P0.31)** and **Port 1 (P1.16–P1.31)**.
- **Memory map reminder:** Flash is mapped at **0x00000000** (after reset), SRAM at **0x40000000**, and peripherals at **0xE0000000** (APB) and **0xF0000000** (or near) — not needed for this question but useful context.
- **Always draw** the chip at the **center** of the block schematic with **arrows to functional blocks** — this is the KTU-expected diagram style.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPIO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBBRT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** GPIO # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** is a generic, software-controllable digital pin on a microcontroller (MCU) or microprocessor (MPU) that has no pre-defined purpose. Each GPIO pin can be independently configured by firmware to function as either a **digital input** (to read logic levels from external devices such as switches and sensors) or a **digital output** (to drive external devices such as LEDs, relays, and buzzers), or alternatively to be routed to a peripheral's **Alternate Function (AF)**. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, GPIO is treated as the **lowest layer of the embedded software stack** — the bridge between raw silicon transistors and application-level firmware. Every higher abstraction (UART, SPI, timers, ADC triggers) ultimately relies on correctly initialised GPIO. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition to Memorise:** > *“A GPIO pin is a software-programmable, bidirectional digital signal interface that connects the internal register file of the microcontroller to an external physical pad, allowing the CPU to read or write the logic state of the pad through memory-mapped control, data, and configuration registers.”* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Wall Socket with a Brain" Imagine each GPIO pin is a **single universal wall socket mounted on the wall of your house**. Out of the factory, it is dormant — it does nothing. - You (the firmware) walk up to the socket and **flip a tiny internal switch** that decides: *"Will this socket OUTPUT power, or will it only SENSE power?"* — This is the **Direction (Mode) Register**. - If you set it to OUTPUT, you then decide whether the **voltage is 5 V or 0 V** at any moment. You write 1 or 0 into the **Output Data Register**. - If you set it to INPUT, the socket simply **reports back to you** whether it currently sees 5 V or 0 V from an external device. You read the **Input Data Register**. - You can also attach a tiny **internal resistor** (pull-up or pull-down) to the socket so that if nothing is plugged in, the pin reads a *defined* default logic level — otherwise it would "float" and read random noise. - The socket may have a **"speed" knob** (slew-rate / drive-strength) so that when you switch a heavy load, the transition is cleaner. - Finally, the socket can be **rerouted to a specialist appliance** (the Alternate Function) such as a UART transmitter, turning your universal socket into a dedicated serial port pin. This "decide-then-control" two-step is the entire philosophy of GPIO programming. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Logic HIGH (VIH):** Typically **≥ 0.7 × VDD** for CMOS inputs. - **Logic LOW (VIL):** Typically **≤ 0.3 × VDD** for CMOS inputs. - **VOH / VOL:** Output drive levels, typically within **0.4 V** of the rail at rated current. - **Drive Strength:** Modern MCUs (e.g., STM32) offer **2 mA, 8 mA, or 12 mA** selectable per pin. - **Maximum Toggle Frequency:** Application dependent, limited by bus clock and software latency. - **Pin Count:** Common KTU-relevant parts: ATmega328 (23 GPIO), STM32F103C8 (37 GPIO), LPC2148 (up to 45 GPIO). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module-4 Highlight:** > The syllabus explicitly demands the study of the **GPIO peripheral block**, the **memory-mapped registers** used to configure it, and the **C-code idioms** to operate it on both **AVR (ATmega series)** and **ARM Cortex-M (STM32/LPC21xx series)** platforms. Both architectures are part of the evaluation pattern. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Register Philosophy: AVR (ATmega328 / ATmega32) The AVR family uses a famously simple **3-register** model per port (PORTx = 8 pins, where x ∈ {A, B, C, D}). | Register | Function | Read/Write Effect | |---|---|---| | **DDRx** (Data Direction Register) | 1 = Output, 0 = Input | Configures pin direction | | **PORTx** (Port Output Register) | When DDRx=1: drives pin HIGH/LOW; When DDRx=0: enables/disables internal pull-up | Output value OR pull-up control | | **PINx** (Port Input Register) | Reads the *actual* physical logic level on the pin | Read-only from software POV | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The DDR–PORT–PIN Trinity (high-yield for KTU):** > - To **output HIGH**: set `DDRx |= (1< - To **output LOW**: set `DDRx |= (1< - To **input with pull-up**: set `DDRx &= ~(1< - To **high-impedance input** (no pull-up): set `DDRx &= ~(1< - To **read** the pin: read `PINx & (1< [!NOTE] > **Why BSRR is critical:** Writing to ODR involves a *read-modify-write* sequence, which is **not atomic**. If an ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) fires between the read and write, the pin state can be corrupted. **BSRR is a one-shot atomic operation** that avoids this — frequently tested in KTU 14-mark problems involving ISRs. ## 2.3 Output Driver Topologies 1. **Push-Pull (Totem-Pole):** Uses two transistors — a PMOS to source current to VDD and an NMOS to sink current to GND. The pin actively drives both HIGH and LOW. Fast edges, used for most digital signalling. 2. **Open-Drain (or Open-Collector):** Only the NMOS to GND is present. The pin can only actively pull LOW. To get a HIGH level, an **external pull-up resistor** to VDD is mandatory. Allows **wired-OR** logic and **level shifting** (e.g., 3.3 V MCU → 5 V device). ## 2.4 Pull-Up and Pull-Down Resistors - **Purpose:** To bias a floating input to a known logic level, eliminating false triggers. - **AVR internal pull-up:** ≈ **20–50 kΩ**, enabled by writing `PORTx=1` on a `DDRx=0` pin. - **STM32 internal pull-up/pull-down:** ≈ **30–50 kΩ** (weak), configurable via PUPDR. - **Rule of thumb:** If line length < 10 cm and speed < 1 MHz, internal pull-up is sufficient. For longer lines or noisy environments, use an external 4.7 kΩ–10 kΩ resistor. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | CMOS Logic HIGH threshold | $V_{IH} \ge 0.7 \times V_{DD}$ | Volts | | CMOS Logic LOW threshold | $V_{IL} \le 0.3 \times V_{DD}$ | Volts | | Current through LED with GPIO drive | $I_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{F,LED}}{R_{S}}$ | Amps; $V_{F,LED}$ ≈ 1.8 V (red), 3.0 V (blue/white) | | RC charging of GPIO input line | $V(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$ | Volts; use to debounce switch | | Open-drain rise time constant | $\tau = R_{pullup} \times C_{line}$ | Seconds | | Toggle frequency limit (software bit-bang) | $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{N_{cycles\_per\_toggle}}$ | Hz | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility GPIO is the **universal translator** between the deterministic, sequential world of a CPU and the chaotic, continuous world of physical electronics. Every embedded product on the market — from a digital thermometer to a Tesla ECU — uses GPIO at some layer. Mastering GPIO is the gateway to **bit-banging protocols**, **interrupt-driven sensing**, **PWM motor control** (via timer AF), and **multiplexed display driving** (Charlieplexing / matrix keypads). # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — LED Current-Limiting Resistor Calculation (AVR) **Problem:** An ATmega328P is powered at $V_{DD} = 5\text{ V}$ and drives a red LED through PD7 with a GPIO pin sourcing current. The LED forward voltage $V_F = 2.0\text{ V}$ at the desired operating point, and we want $I_{LED} = 10\text{ mA}$. Find the series resistor $R_S$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation We begin with Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop: VDD → GPIO pin → LED → Resistor → GND. $$ V_{DD} = V_{OL,GPIO} + V_{F,LED} + V_{R_S} $$ Assume the GPIO output LOW-side voltage drop is negligible when *sinking* (or we source — same arithmetic). The voltage across the resistor is: $$ V_{R_S} = V_{DD} - V_{F,LED} $$ Substituting the numerical values: $$ V_{R_S} = 5\text{ V} - 2.0\text{ V} = 3.0\text{ V} $$ By Ohm's Law, $V_{R_S} = I_{LED} \cdot R_S$, hence: $$ R_S = \dfrac{V_{R_S}}{I_{LED}} $$ $$ R_S = \dfrac{3.0\text{ V}}{10 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A}} $$ $$ R_S = 300\ \Omega $$ **Closest standard E12 value:** $R_S = 330\ \Omega$ (giving $I_{LED} \approx 9.1\text{ mA}$, perfectly safe). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The 20 mA absolute maximum per AVR pin is a *DC* limit, not a *continuous* limit at 5 V with low duty cycle. Conservative firmware design limits sustained current to **10 mA** to keep junction temperature in spec. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Switch Debounce RC Time Constant (AVR) **Problem:** A momentary push-button connected to PB0 (configured as input with internal pull-up) has a mechanical bounce time of approximately 5 ms. The input line has a stray capacitance of 50 pF. Determine an appropriate external capacitor to suppress bounce without slowing the response excessively. ### Step-by-Step Derivation We model the bounce as a noisy step. A low-pass RC filter will round the rising edge. The 10–90% rise time is: $$ t_{r} \approx 2.2 \cdot RC $$ We need the filter time constant to be **at least equal to** the bounce duration but not more than the human reaction time (~100 ms). Choose $t_{r} = 10\text{ ms}$ for a margin. $$ RC = \dfrac{t_{r}}{2.2} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{2.2} \approx 4.55\text{ ms} $$ Now solve for C using R = internal pull-up ≈ 50 kΩ: $$ C = \dfrac{RC}{R} = \dfrac{4.55 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{3}} $$ $$ C = 9.1 \times 10^{-8}\text{ F} = 91\text{ nF} $$ **Closest standard value:** $C = 100\text{ nF}$, giving $RC = 5\text{ ms}$, comfortably above the bounce time. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Open-Drain Rise Time (I²C line / wired-OR bus) **Problem:** An open-drain GPIO on an STM32 (e.g., PB6 used as I²C SCL in a software bit-bang) drives a 10 kΩ pull-up into a bus of total capacitance 100 pF. Calculate the 0–63% rise time and the 10–90% rise time. ### Step-by-Step Derivation Time constant: $$ \tau = R_{pullup} \times C_{bus} = 10 \times 10^{3} \times 100 \times 10^{-12} = 1.0\ \mu\text{s} $$ 63% rise (one time constant): $$ t_{63\%} = \tau = 1.0\ \mu\text{s} $$ 10–90% rise (standard rise-time definition): $$ t_{10-90\%} = 2.2\,\tau = 2.2 \times 1.0\ \mu\text{s} = 2.2\ \mu\text{s} $$ For a 100 kHz I²C bus, the half-period is 5 µs. Since $t_{10-90\%} = 2.2\ \mu\text{s} < 5\ \mu\text{s}$, the bus meets the rise-time spec. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Pin Toggle Frequency Limit (Software Bit-Bang) **Problem:** An STM32F103 (Cortex-M3, $f_{CPU} = 72\text{ MHz}$) toggles a GPIO pin via BSRR. Each iteration of the toggle loop takes 4 cycles (load, OR, store, branch). What is the maximum square-wave frequency achievable? ### Step-by-Step Derivation Number of full toggles per second: $$ f_{toggle} = \dfrac{f_{CPU}}{N_{cycles}} $$ $$ f_{toggle} = \dfrac{72 \times 10^{6}}{4} = 18 \times 10^{6}\text{ toggles/s} $$ One complete square-wave cycle requires **two** toggles (HIGH→LOW and LOW→HIGH): $$ f_{square} = \dfrac{f_{toggle}}{2} = 9\text{ MHz} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the theoretical upper bound assuming zero loop overhead and no flash wait states. In practice, with branch penalties and unaligned accesses, the achievable frequency is typically **60–80%** of this theoretical maximum. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — AVR vs ARM Cortex-M GPIO | Feature | AVR (ATmega) | ARM Cortex-M (STM32) | |---|---|---| | Registers per port | 3 (DDR, PORT, PIN) | ~10 (MODER, OTYPER, OSPEEDR, PUPDR, IDR, ODR, BSRR, BRR, LCKR, AFR) | | Clock gating | Not required | **Mandatory** via RCC | | Configuration granularity | 1-bit per pin | 2-bit per pin (MODER) | | Atomic pin set | Only via `cbi/sbi` instructions in extended I/O space | **BSRR/BRR** (always atomic) | | Output drive strength | Fixed | Selectable (2/8/12 mA typical) | | Pull-up/Pull-down | Pull-up only | Pull-up AND pull-down | | Alternate Function | Multiplexed via separate registers | Configured via MODER + AFR | | Lock mechanism | None | LCKR register can freeze configuration | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — GPIO Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU["CPU Core
ARM Cortex-M3"]:::core BUS["APB Bus"]:::bus RCC["RCC Clock
Enable Logic"]:::clk MODER["GPIOx_MODER
Direction Control"]:::cfg OTYPER["GPIOx_OTYPER
Push-Pull vs Open-Drain"]:::cfg OSPEEDR["GPIOx_OSPEEDR
Slew Rate 2/10/50 MHz"]:::cfg PUPDR["GPIOx_PUPDR
Pull-up / Pull-down"]:::cfg AFR["GPIOx_AFR
Alternate Function Multiplexer"]:::cfg IDR["GPIOx_IDR
Input Data Register"]:::data ODR["GPIOx_ODR
Output Data Register"]:::data BSRR["GPIOx_BSRR
Atomic Set / Reset"]:::data PP["Push-Pull
Driver Pair"]:::drv OD["Open-Drain
NMOS Only"]:::drv SCHMITT["Schmitt Trigger
Input Hysteresis"]:::drv PU["Internal Pull-up
~ 30 to 50 kOhm"]:::drv PD["Internal Pull-down
~ 30 to 50 kOhm"]:::drv PAD["Physical
Pin / Pad"]:::pad CPU --> BUS BUS --> RCC RCC --> MODER RCC --> OTYPER RCC --> OSPEEDR RCC --> PUPDR RCC --> AFR MODER --> PAD OTYPER --> PP OTYPER --> OD OSPEEDR --> PP OSPEEDR --> OD AFR --> PAD PUPDR --> PU PUPDR --> PD PU --> PAD PD --> PAD PP --> PAD OD --> PAD SCHMITT --> IDR PAD --> SCHMITT ODR --> PP ODR --> OD BSRR --> PP BSRR --> OD BUS --> IDR BUS --> ODR BUS --> BSRR classDef core fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bus fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef clk fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cfg fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef data fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef drv fill:#d6d8db,stroke:#212529,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef pad fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Generic GPIO Configuration Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Step 1: Enable Peripheral Clock
via RCC_APBxENR"]:::step B["Step 2: Set Pin Mode
MODER = 01 Output or 00 Input"]:::step C{"Input or
Output?"}:::dec D["Step 3a: Configure Pull
PUPDR 01 Pull-up / 10 Pull-down"]:::step E["Step 4a: Read IDR
for Input State"]:::step F["Step 3b: Set Output Type
OTYPER 0 Push-Pull / 1 Open-Drain"]:::step G["Step 4b: Set Speed
OSPEEDR 01 10 MHz / 11 50 MHz"]:::step H["Step 5b: Write to ODR
or Atomic BSRR"]:::step I["Step 6: Pin Active
on Physical Pad"]:::end A --> B --> C C -->|Input| D --> E --> I C -->|Output| F --> G --> H --> I classDef step fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dec fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef end fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — I/O Pin Internal Equivalent Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR VDD["VDD Rail"]:::pwr GND["GND Rail"]:::pwr RPU["Pull-up Resistor
~ 30 to 50 kOhm"]:::r RPD["Pull-down Resistor
~ 30 to 50 kOhm"]:::r PMOS["PMOS
Source Current to Pad"]:::tr NMOS["NMOS
Sink Current from Pad"]:::tr SCH["Schmitt
Trigger"]:::logic INV["Logic Inverter"]:::logic AND["Enable AND Gate"]:::logic PAD["External
Pin"]:::pad VDD --- RPU --- PAD PAD --- RPD --- GND VDD --- PMOS --- PAD PAD --- NMOS --- GND PAD --- SCH --- INV --- AND classDef pwr fill:#fde2e1,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef r fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tr fill:#d6d8db,stroke:#212529,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef logic fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pad fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Reading an Input Pin (Read-Modify-Write Risk) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant App as Application Code participant ODR as GPIOx_ODR participant PIN as External Pin participant BSRR as GPIOx_BSRR participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine Note over App,ODR: STEP 1 - Read ODR App->>ODR: Read current value ODR-->>App: Value 0x0008 (Pin 3 HIGH) Note over App,ODR: STEP 2 - Modify (set Pin 5) App->>App: Compute new value 0x0028 Note over ISR,App: *** INTERRUPT FIRES HERE *** ISR->>ODR: BSRR clears Pin 3 ODR-->>ISR: Pin 3 now LOW ISR-->>App: Returns Note over App,ODR: STEP 3 - Write back App->>ODR: Write 0x0028 ODR-->>App: Pin 3 is now CORRUPTED to 1 Note over App,ODR: Bug: ISR write overwritten Note over App,BSRR: SOLUTION - Use BSRR for atomic write App->>BSRR: BSRR = 0x00200020 (atomic set Pin 5) BSRR-->>PIN: Pin 5 goes HIGH, no race ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question] **Explain the role of the DDRx register in an AVR microcontroller. How is the direction of a single pin, say PB3, set as input and output?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Data Direction Register (DDRx)** is a memory-mapped 8-bit register in the AVR architecture that controls whether each pin of port x acts as an input (DDRx_n = 0) or an output (DDRx_n = 1). For pin PB3, the control bit is DDRB bit-3. - Set as input: `DDRB &= ~(1 << 3);` *(1 mark)* - Set as output: `DDRB |= (1 << 3);` *(1 mark)* The DDRx register is the *direction control* half of the DDR–PORT–PIN triad and must be configured *before* the corresponding PORTx is written to. *(1 mark)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question] **Differentiate between Push-Pull and Open-Drain GPIO output configurations. Give one application for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Push-Pull | Open-Drain | |---|---|---| | Transistors | PMOS + NMOS totem-pole | Only NMOS to GND | | Active HIGH drive | Yes, actively sources current | No, requires external pull-up | | Active LOW drive | Yes, sinks current | Yes, sinks current | | Wired-OR / Wired-AND | Not possible | **Possible** (multiple devices on one line) | | Level shifting | Limited | **Easy** (pull-up to any voltage) | - **Push-Pull application:** Driving an LED or interfacing with a logic gate input. *(1 mark)* - **Open-Drain application:** I²C bus SDA/SCL lines, allowing bidirectional communication on a single wire with multiple masters. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] **Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the internal structure of a single GPIO pin in a modern ARM Cortex-M microcontroller (STM32F103). List **all** configuration registers involved. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** The pin consists of: 1. An **input path** with a Schmitt trigger feeding the Input Data Register (IDR). 2. An **output path** with a configurable driver pair (PMOS/NMOS for push-pull, NMOS-only for open-drain) feeding the Output Data Register (ODR). 3. **Pull-up and pull-down resistors** (≈ 30–50 kΩ) selectable by PUPDR. 4. An **alternate function multiplexer** (AFR) that routes peripheral signals (UART TX, SPI MOSI, etc.) to the pad. 5. **ESD protection diodes** clamping the pad to VDD and GND. 6. The **physical pad** connecting to the package pin. **Configuration Registers (4 marks):** 1. **RCC_APBxENR** — enables the GPIO port clock (must be set first). 2. **GPIOx_MODER** — mode: 00 Input, 01 Output, 10 Alternate Function, 11 Analog (2 bits per pin). 3. **GPIOx_OTYPER** — output type: 0 Push-Pull, 1 Open-Drain. 4. **GPIOx_OSPEEDR** — output speed: 00 2 MHz, 01 10 MHz, 10 50 MHz, 11 reserved. 5. **GPIOx_PUPDR** — pull-up/pull-down: 00 None, 01 Pull-up, 10 Pull-down, 11 Reserved. 6. **GPIOx_IDR** — read input state. 7. **GPIOx_ODR** — write output state. 8. **GPIOx_BSRR / BRR** — atomic set/reset. 9. **GPIOx_LCKR** — lock configuration until next reset. 10. **GPIOx_AFR[0]/AFR[1]** — alternate function selection (4 bits per pin). --- **Q. (b)** Write a C program for the STM32F103 to configure **PC13 as a push-pull output driving an LED at 2 MHz slew rate**, and **PA0 as a digital input with internal pull-down**. In the main loop, toggle PC13 with a software delay whenever PA0 reads HIGH. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** ```c #include "stm32f1xx.h" void GPIO_Init(void) { /* Step 1: Enable GPIOA and GPIOC clocks (mandatory) */ RCC->APB2ENR |= RCC_APB2ENR_IOPAEN | RCC_APB2ENR_IOPCEN; /* Step 2: Configure PC13 as 2 MHz push-pull output */ /* MODER[27:26] = 01 (output), OTYPER = 0 (PP), OSPEEDR[27:26] = 01 (10 MHz -> use 2 MHz = 10) */ GPIOC->CRH &= ~(0xF << 20); /* Clear CNF13 + MODE13 */ GPIOC->CRH |= (0x2 << 20); /* MODE13 = 10 (2 MHz output), CNF13 = 00 (PP) */ /* Note: STM32F1 uses CRH/CRL register configuration; for F4 use MODER */ /* Step 3: Configure PA0 as input with pull-down */ GPIOA->CRL &= ~(0xF << 0); /* Clear CNF0 + MODE0 */ GPIOA->CRL |= (0x8 << 0); /* CNF0 = 10 (input pull-up/pull-down), MODE0 = 00 (input) */ GPIOA->ODR &= ~(1 << 0); /* ODR=0 selects pull-down for PA0 */ } void delay(void) { for (volatile int i = 0; i < 500000; i++); } int main(void) { GPIO_Init(); while (1) { if (GPIOA->IDR & (1 << 0)) { /* PA0 HIGH? */ GPIOC->BSRR = (1 << 13); /* Atomic set PC13 */ delay(); GPIOC->BSRR = (1 << (13 + 16)); /* Atomic reset PC13 */ delay(); } } } ``` **Explanation (key points):** 1. RCC clock must be enabled before any GPIO register write; otherwise writes silently have no effect. *(1 mark)* 2. PC13 configured as general-purpose output via MODER = 01, push-pull via OTYPER = 0, low speed via OSPEEDR = 00. *(2 marks)* 3. PA0 configured as input via MODER = 00, pull-down via PUPDR = 10. *(2 marks)* 4. BSRR is used for atomic toggle to avoid race conditions even in this polled loop. *(2 marks)* --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **Q. (a)** Explain the **DDR–PORT–PIN register model** of the ATmega32 microcontroller. Construct the complete truth table showing the four possible states of a pin (input floating, input with pull-up, output low, output high) and write the corresponding C instructions for each state on pin PD5. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Register Model (3 marks):** The AVR ATmega32 organises GPIO into 8-bit ports (PORTA, PORTB, PORTC, PORTD). Each port has three associated 8-bit registers: - **DDRx (Data Direction Register):** 0 = input, 1 = output. - **PORTx (Port Data Register):** When DDRx=1, writes the output value. When DDRx=0, enables (1) or disables (0) the internal pull-up resistor. - **PINx (Port Input Pins):** Reads the actual physical logic level on the corresponding pin (regardless of direction). **Truth Table for PD5 (3 marks):** | DDRD5 | PORTD5 | Pin State | C Instructions | |:---:|:---:|---|---| | 0 | 0 | **Input, Hi-Z (Floating)** | `DDRD &= ~(1<<5); PORTD &= ~(1<<5);` | | 0 | 1 | **Input with Internal Pull-up (≈ 50 kΩ to VCC)** | `DDRD &= ~(1<<5); PORTD \|= (1<<5);` | | 1 | 0 | **Output Driving LOW (sinks current)** | `DDRD \|= (1<<5); PORTD &= ~(1<<5);` | | 1 | 1 | **Output Driving HIGH (sources current)** | `DDRD \|= (1<<5); PORTD \|= (1<<5);` | **Reading PD5 (1 mark):** `if (PIND & (1<<5)) { /* pin is HIGH */ }` --- **Q. (b)** A **4×4 matrix keypad** is interfaced to PORTB of ATmega32. The four rows are connected to PB0–PB3 (configured as outputs driving logic levels), and the four columns are connected to PB4–PB7 (configured as inputs with internal pull-ups). Write a C program using the **row-scanning algorithm with software debounce** to detect the pressed key and store its row-column index in variables `row` and `col`. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** ```c #include #include volatile uint8_t row = 0xFF, col = 0xFF; void keypad_init(void) { DDRB = 0x0F; /* PB0-PB3 outputs (rows), PB4-PB7 inputs (columns) */ PORTB = 0xF0; /* All rows HIGH, pull-ups enabled on columns */ } uint8_t keypad_scan(void) { for (uint8_t r = 0; r < 4; r++) { /* Drive one row LOW, others HIGH */ PORTB = (PORTB & 0xF0) | (~(1 << r) & 0x0F); _delay_us(5); /* Settle time */ uint8_t c = PINB >> 4; /* Read columns */ if (c != 0x0F) { /* A key is pressed */ _delay_ms(20); /* Debounce */ c = PINB >> 4; if (c != 0x0F) { for (uint8_t j = 0; j < 4; j++) { if (!(c & (1 << j))) { row = r; col = j; return 1; } } } } } return 0; /* No key pressed */ } int main(void) { keypad_init(); while (1) { if (keypad_scan()) { /* row and col now hold the key position */ } } } ``` **Key Algorithm Steps (marking scheme):** 1. DDRB set to 0x0F (rows output, columns input). PORTB set to 0xF0 (rows HIGH, column pull-ups). *(2 marks)* 2. Loop drives each row LOW in turn; reads columns via PINB. *(2 marks)* 3. Software debounce using 20 ms delay + double-read technique. *(2 marks)* 4. On confirmed press, stores row index and column index. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Examiner Pitfalls):** > 1. **Forgetting to enable the peripheral clock** in STM32/LPC2148 GPIO configuration — every GPIO write is silently ignored if the RCC clock for that port is not enabled. **Loses 2 marks** if omitted. > 2. **Confusing DDRx and PORTx semantics** on AVR — many students write `PORTx = 1` to set a pin HIGH without first setting `DDRx = 1`, leaving the pin in input-pull-up mode. **Loses 1 mark** for AVR questions. > 3. **Using `ODR` instead of `BSRR`** in interrupt-driven code — the read-modify-write race condition is a favourite examiner trap. Always quote **atomicity** as the justification. > 4. **Wrong MODER field indexing** — MODER is a 2-bit field per pin. Pin 5 uses bits [11:10], not bit 5. This is the most common off-by-one error. > 5. **Forgetting to define the pin as Analog before using it as ADC input** — the Schmitt trigger must be disabled (MODER = 11) to prevent parasitic current paths. > 6. **Floating input left without pull-up/pull-down** — in real boards this causes random key presses and phantom interrupts. Examiner deducts marks for incomplete initialization lists. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — GPIO (Module 4):** > - GPIO = **General Purpose Input/Output** — software-configurable digital pin. > - Three fundamental operations: **configure direction, drive level (if output), read level (if input)**. > - **AVR (ATmega)** uses 3 registers per port: **DDRx, PORTx, PINx**. > - **AVR truth table** (4 states): `(DDR,PORT) = (0,0)` floating input, `(0,1)` input + pull-up, `(1,0)` output LOW, `(1,1)` output HIGH. > - **ARM Cortex-M (STM32)** uses ~10 registers: **MODER, OTYPER, OSPEEDR, PUPDR, IDR, ODR, BSRR, BRR, LCKR, AFR**. > - **Clock enable via RCC** is **mandatory** on STM32 before any GPIO configuration. > - **MODER values**: 00 Input, 01 Output, 10 Alternate Function, 11 Analog. > - **PUPDR values**: 00 None, 01 Pull-up, 10 Pull-down, 11 Reserved. > - **BSRR** is the atomic way to set/reset a pin; **ODR** is read-modify-write and unsafe inside ISRs. > - **Push-Pull** actively drives both HIGH and LOW; **Open-Drain** only sinks and needs an external pull-up. > - **Open-drain** enables **wired-OR** buses like I²C and **level shifting** between voltage domains. > - Internal pull-up resistors (AVR ≈ 20–50 kΩ, STM32 ≈ 30–50 kΩ) bias floating inputs to a known level. > - **Alternate Function (AF)** routing lets a GPIO pin carry peripheral signals (UART TX, SPI MOSI, PWM, ADC input). > - **Maximum GPIO toggle frequency** (software bit-bang) ≈ $f_{CPU} / (2 \times N_{cycles})$. > - **LED current limiting**: $R_S = (V_{DD} - V_F) / I_{LED}$; standard value 330 Ω for 5 V / 10 mA red LED. > - **Switch debounce**: use an RC filter with $t_r \ge 2.2RC \ge t_{bounce}$, or software double-read with 10–20 ms delay. > - **Keypad row-scanning**: drive one row LOW at a time, read columns; the intersection of active row + active column identifies the key. > - **ESD protection** diodes on every pin clamp the pad to VDD and GND; never apply a voltage outside [VSS−0.3, VDD+0.3] without a current-limiting resistor.

topics.title: Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Memory

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory** in an embedded system is a physical electronic component or sub-system that stores data, program instructions, and intermediate computational results. In the context of microcontroller-based systems, memory is logically partitioned into **Program Memory** (Code Storage) and **Data Memory** (Runtime Variables & Stacks).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** definition, microcontroller memory is organized as a linear array of addressable storage locations, where each location is uniquely identified by an **address** and holds a fixed number of bits (typically **8 bits / 1 byte** per location).

### Memory Sub-Classification (Academic Standard)

Memory in embedded systems is divided into two fundamental categories:

1. **Volatile Memory** — Loses stored data when power is removed.
2. **Non-Volatile Memory (NVM)** — Retains stored data even without power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology:**
> - **Primary Memory**: Directly accessible by the CPU (e.g., RAM, ROM).
> - **Secondary Memory**: External, bulk storage (e.g., Flash drives, SD cards).
> - **Cache Memory**: High-speed, small-capacity memory between CPU and RAM.
> - **Register File**: Fastest on-chip memory inside the CPU.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your **study desk** during an exam:

- **Books and Notes** (sitting in your bookshelf at home) → **Hard Disk / Flash Memory** (large capacity but slow to access).
- **Open Notes on the Desk** (just the cheat-sheet you are reading) → **RAM** (faster, but if the bell rings and the exam ends, you forget everything — **volatile**).
- **The Formulas Memorized in your Brain** → **CPU Registers / Cache** (lightning fast, tiny capacity, instantly accessible).
- **Your Permanent Knowledge** (such as multiplication tables) → **ROM/Flash** (retained even after power off — **non-volatile**).

This **Desk → Brain → Bookshelf** analogy perfectly illustrates the **Memory Hierarchy** in any computer/embedded system, where speed increases and capacity decreases as you move closer to the CPU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed-Cost-Capacity Trade-off:** Faster memory is **more expensive per byte** but has **lower capacity**. The famous rule of thumb is — *"Smaller is faster."*

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Access Time** = Time taken to read/write a memory location. Measured in **nanoseconds (ns)**.
- **Memory Capacity** = Total number of bits/bytes a chip can store. Measured in **Bytes, KB, MB, GB**.
- **Memory Bandwidth** = Rate at which data can be transferred. Measured in **Bytes/sec** or **MB/s**.
- **Word Size** = Number of bits processed per memory cycle (typical: **8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit**).
- **Data Retention Time** = How long non-volatile memory holds data without power (**typically 10–20 years**).
- **Endurance** = Number of write/erase cycles a memory cell can withstand (**Flash ≈ 10,000–100,000 cycles**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Memory — The Engineering Taxonomy

Microcontroller memory is classified structurally, functionally, and technologically:

### 2.1.1 Based on Access Mechanism

| Type | Full Form | Access Pattern | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RAM** | Random Access Memory | Any address, equal time | SRAM, DRAM |
| **SAM** | Serial Access Memory | Sequential access | Shift Registers, Tape |
| **DAM** | Direct Access Memory | Combination (track + sector) | Hard Disk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus Point:** The term "Random Access" does NOT mean "random data." It means the access *time is independent of the physical location* of the data.

### 2.1.2 Based on Volatility (Detailed Hierarchy)

```
                    ┌─── READ-ONLY (ROM Family)
                    │      ├── Mask ROM
                    │      ├── PROM
                    │      ├── EPROM
                    │      ├── EEPROM
        MEMORY  ────┤      └── Flash Memory
                    │
                    └─── READ-WRITE (RAM Family)
                           ├── SRAM (Static RAM)
                           │      └── uses Flip-Flops (6 transistors/cell)
                           └── DRAM (Dynamic RAM)
                                  └── uses Capacitors (1 transistor + 1 cap/cell)
```

---

## 2.2 RAM Family — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 SRAM (Static RAM)

- **Cell Construction**: Built using **6 MOSFETs** forming a bi-stable flip-flop.
- **Refresh Requirement**: **No refresh needed** as long as power is on.
- **Speed**: Very fast, typically **5 ns to 20 ns** access time.
- **Use Case**: CPU cache, registers, high-speed buffers.
- **Disadvantage**: High cost, low density (6T per cell = large silicon area).
- **Power**: Higher static power consumption (current always flowing through the flip-flop).

### 2.2.2 DRAM (Dynamic RAM)

- **Cell Construction**: **1 MOSFET + 1 capacitor**.
- **Refresh Requirement**: Must be refreshed every **few milliseconds (typically 64 ms)**.
- **Speed**: Slower than SRAM (access time ≈ **50 ns to 100 ns**).
- **Use Case**: Main memory (PC RAM), large data buffers.
- **Advantage**: High density, low cost, low power (no static current).
- **Disadvantage**: Needs refresh controller — adds complexity.

### 2.2.3 SRAM vs DRAM — Comparative Engineering Analysis

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cell Elements | 6 Transistors | 1 Transistor + 1 Capacitor |
| Density | Low | High |
| Speed | Fast (ns) | Moderate |
| Power | High (static) | Low (dynamic) |
| Refresh | Not needed | Required every 64 ms |
| Cost / bit | High | Low |
| Use | Cache / Registers | Main Memory |

---

## 2.3 ROM Family — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Evolution of ROM Technologies (KTU High-Yield Topic)

1. **Mask ROM**: Programmed at the time of **fabrication** using photolithographic masks. Cheapest in bulk, but cannot be reprogrammed.

2. **PROM (Programmable ROM)**: User-programmable **once** using a PROM programmer. Fuses inside the chip are blown electrically. **One-time programmable (OTP)**.

3. **EPROM (Erasable PROM)**: Programmed electrically, erased using **ultraviolet (UV) light** through a quartz window on top of the chip. Can be reprogrammed multiple times.

4. **EEPROM (Electrically Erasable PROM)**: Erased using **electrical signals**, byte-by-byte. Used for storing small configuration data.

5. **Flash Memory**: Modern NVM. Erased in **blocks/sectors** using electrical signals. Types:
   - **NOR Flash**: Random access, used for code storage (boot loaders, firmware).
   - **NAND Flash**: Sequential access, used for bulk data (SSD, SD cards, USB drives).

---

## 2.4 Internal Memory of Microcontrollers (Harvard vs Von Neumann)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Microcontrollers typically use the **Harvard Architecture** with separate program and data memory buses.

### 2.4.1 8051 Memory Map (Classic KTU Example)

The **Intel 8051** has the following memory structure:

- **Program Memory (ROM)**: **64 KB** (16-bit address = 0x0000 to 0xFFFF).
- **External Program Memory**: Up to **64 KB** (accessed via `PSEN` signal).
- **Internal Data Memory (RAM)**: **128 bytes** (0x00 to 0x7F — general purpose) + **128 bytes SFR** (0x80 to 0xFF).
- **External Data Memory**: Up to **64 KB** (accessed via `MOVX` instruction).

### 2.4.2 Memory Organization inside 8051

- **Register Banks** (4 banks × 8 registers = 32 bytes, located at 0x00–0x1F).
- **Bit-Addressable Area** (16 bytes at 0x20–0x2F, gives 128 bit-addressable locations).
- **General Purpose RAM** (80 bytes at 0x30–0x7F).
- **SFR Space** (Special Function Registers at 0x80–0xFF — e.g., `P0`, `P1`, `ACC`, `B`, `SP`, `DPTR`).

---

## 2.5 Memory Interfacing Concepts

### 2.5.1 Address Decoding

Address decoding is the process of generating **Chip Select (CS)** signals for memory chips based on the system's address bus.

**Types of Decoding:**
1. **Linear (Full) Decoding**: Each memory location maps to exactly one address. No duplication.
2. **Partial (Inexpensive) Decoding**: Higher address lines are ignored. Multiple addresses map to the same memory location (causing **address aliasing**).

### 2.5.2 Memory Capacity Formula

For a memory chip with **N address lines** and **M data lines**:

$$\text{Total Capacity (in bits)} = 2^N \times M$$

$$\text{Total Capacity (in bytes)} = \frac{2^N \times M}{8}$$

### 2.5.3 Memory Map Calculation (KTU Repeated Question Pattern)

**Given:** n address lines used for a chip. Then:

$$\text{Number of addressable locations} = 2^n$$

$$\text{Total memory} = 2^n \times \text{(data width in bytes)}$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Memory Capacity (bits) | $C = 2^n \times d$ | bits | n = address lines, d = data lines |
| 2 | Memory Capacity (bytes) | $C_{bytes} = 2^n \times (d/8)$ | bytes | d = data lines in bits |
| 3 | Address Range | $0 \text{ to } (2^n - 1)$ | decimal/hex | End address = $2^n - 1$ |
| 4 | Access Time | $t_a$ (read cycle) | ns | Time from valid address to valid data |
| 5 | Bandwidth | $B = \dfrac{\text{Data Transferred}}{t_a}$ | bytes/s | $B = 2^n \times f_{clock} / 8$ |
| 6 | Power Dissipation | $P = V \times I$ | mW | Operating power = $V_{cc} \times I_{cc}$ |
| 7 | Number of Chips Required | $N_{chips} = \dfrac{\text{Total Memory Needed}}{\text{Per Chip Capacity}}$ | integer | Round up to next integer |
| 8 | Refresh Rate (DRAM) | $f_{refresh} = 1/T_{refresh}$ | Hz | $T_{refresh} = 64$ ms (typical) |
| 9 | Refresh Cycles per Refresh Period | $RC = T_{refresh} / t_{cycle}$ | cycles | Each row must be refreshed |
| 10 | Word Size (Addressable) | $W = 2^d$ | bytes | d = data width in bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** The vertical bar character `\|` is never used in tables. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX for absolute value to prevent markdown table corruption.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Automotive ECUs**: Use Flash memory for firmware (re-programmable via OBD-II port) and SRAM for sensor buffering.
2. **IoT Devices**: Use external Flash + tiny SRAM to balance cost and power.
3. **Aerospace Systems**: Use **MRAM** (Magnetoresistive RAM) — radiation-hardened, non-volatile, near-instant write.
4. **Mobile Phones**: Use LPDDR DRAM + UFS Flash for high bandwidth.
5. **Smart Cards**: Use **EEPROM** for storing PIN, balance, and identity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1: Memory Capacity Derivation (Step-by-Step)

**Problem Statement:** A memory chip has **16 address lines** and **8 data lines**. Calculate the total memory capacity in (a) bits, (b) bytes, (c) kilobytes, and (d) the address range in hexadecimal.

### Step 1 — Identify the Number of Address Locations

With 16 address lines, the number of unique locations is:

$$N = 2^{16}$$

$$N = 65536 \text{ locations}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Total Capacity in Bits

Each location stores 8 bits (1 byte). So total bits:

$$C_{bits} = 2^{16} \times 8 = 65536 \times 8$$

$$C_{bits} = 524288 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Bytes

$$C_{bytes} = \frac{C_{bits}}{8} = \frac{524288}{8} = 65536 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Kilobytes (KB)

$$C_{KB} = \frac{C_{bytes}}{1024} = \frac{65536}{1024}$$

$$C_{KB} = 64 \text{ KB}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Hex Address Range

$$\text{Start Address} = 0x0000$$

$$\text{End Address} = 0xFFFF \text{ (since } 2^{16} - 1 = 65535 = 0xFFFF\text{)}$$

### Final Answer

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Address lines | 16 |
| Data lines | 8 |
| Total bits | 524,288 bits |
| Total bytes | 65,536 bytes |
| Capacity | 64 KB |
| Address range | 0x0000 – 0xFFFF |

---

## 3.2 Problem 2: Memory Chip Count Derivation

**Problem Statement:** A system designer needs to build a **1 MB** memory system using chips each with capacity **64 KB × 8**. How many chips are required? If each chip has **16 data lines** worth of word width, how should they be arranged?

### Step 1 — Convert Both to Common Unit (bytes)

$$C_{required} = 1 \text{ MB} = 1 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes}$$

$$C_{per\_chip} = 64 \text{ KB} = 64 \times 1024 = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Number of Chips

$$N_{chips} = \frac{C_{required}}{C_{per\_chip}} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{65{,}536}$$

$$N_{chips} = 16 \text{ chips}$$

### Step 3 — Practical Arrangement

The 16 chips can be arranged as:
- **Banks of 8 chips in parallel** (to make 64-bit data bus) × **2 banks** (to make 1 MB)
- OR **2 chips in parallel** (to make 16-bit) × **8 groups** to fill address space.

> [!NOTE]
> **General Rule:** If $N_{chips} = X$ and each chip has data width $D_{chip}$ bits, and system data bus is $D_{sys}$ bits, then:
>
> $$\text{Chips per bank} = D_{sys} / D_{chip}$$
>
> $$\text{Number of banks} = N_{chips} / \text{Chips per bank}$$

---

## 3.3 Problem 3: Address Decoding Derivation Using 3-to-8 Decoder

**Problem Statement:** A 16-bit address bus system uses a **74LS138 (3-to-8 decoder)** for chip selection. The decoder inputs are connected to **A15, A14, A13**.
