The first memory chip should be enabled when these three bits equal **000**. Calculate the address range of this chip, assuming the chip itself uses the remaining address lines (A0 to A12).

### Step 1 — Decoder Input Assignment

The 74LS138 inputs: A = A15, B = A14, C = A13 (per datasheet, where C is MSB). Output Y0 is active LOW when CBA = 000.

$$A_{15}A_{14}A_{13} = 000$$

### Step 2 — Determine Address Range

For Y0 to be selected:
$$A_{15} = 0, \quad A_{14} = 0, \quad A_{13} = 0$$

The remaining 13 lines (A12 to A0) can be anything from 0 to 1.

### Step 3 — Calculate Start and End Address

$$\text{Start} = A_{15}A_{14}A_{13} A_{12} A_{11} \cdots A_0 = 000\ 0 0000\ 0000\ 0000 = 0x0000$$

$$\text{End} = A_{15}A_{14}A_{13} A_{12} A_{11} \cdots A_0 = 000\ 1 1111\ 1111\ 1111 = 0x1FFF$$

### Step 4 — Final Address Range

$$\text{Address Range} = 0x0000 \text{ to } 0x1FFF = 8 \text{ KB}$$

### Step 5 — Capacity Verification

$$C = 2^{13} \times 8 \text{ bits} = 2^{13} \text{ bytes} = 8 \text{ KB} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Problem 4: Address Aliasing in Partial Decoding

**Problem Statement:** A memory chip has **4 address lines** (A3–A0). It is connected to a system with **8 address lines** (A7–A0), but only A3–A0 are decoded (A7–A4 ignored). How many addresses map to the same physical location?

### Step 1 — Identify Unused Lines

Unused lines: **A7, A6, A5, A4** → 4 lines are ignored.

### Step 2 — Calculate Number of Duplicates

$$N_{aliases} = 2^{4} = 16$$

This means each physical location responds to **16 different addresses**, an 8-bit address space of which only 16 addresses are unique to this chip.

### Step 3 — Consequence

This is the **"Address Mirror"** or **"Foldback Memory"** problem. Partial decoding is **cheaper** (fewer gates) but **unsafe** because software bugs may accidentally access the mirror region.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Capacity | Address lines, Data lines | Total memory | $2^n \times d$ bits |
| Address Range | Chip select equation | Start/End address | Substitute min/max values |
| Chip Count | Required size, chip size | No. of chips | $N = \text{Total / Per chip}$ |
| Refresh Count | Refresh period, Row count | Rows per refresh | $R = T_r / t_{cycle}$ |
| Decoder Output | Address lines, select pattern | Active chip | Match the binary pattern |
| Data Bus Width | Parallel chips | System data width | $D_{sys} = N_{parallel} \times D_{chip}$ |
| Access Time | Clock period | Memory cycle | $t_{acc} = n \times T_{clock}$ |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part A questions test direct conceptual recall and definitions, mapped to **Remember/Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define the following terms related to microcontroller memory:**
**(a)** Volatile memory
**(b)** Static RAM
**(c)** Memory access time

### Model Answer:

**(a) Volatile Memory:** Volatile memory is a type of computer memory that requires a continuous supply of electrical power to retain the stored data. When the power is switched off, all stored information is lost. Examples: SRAM, DRAM, Cache memory.

**(b) Static RAM (SRAM):** SRAM is a type of volatile random-access memory that uses **6 MOSFETs** arranged as a bi-stable flip-flop to store each bit. It does not require periodic refreshing to maintain its contents, and provides faster access times compared to DRAM. It is commonly used for CPU cache, registers, and high-speed buffers in embedded systems.

**(c) Memory Access Time:** Access time is the total time taken by a memory device to perform a read or write operation, measured from the moment a valid address is presented on the address bus to the moment valid data appears on the data bus. It is measured in **nanoseconds (ns)**. For example, an SRAM with 10 ns access time completes one read operation in 10 nanoseconds.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between Mask ROM, PROM, EPROM, and EEPROM. (Any 3 differences)**

### Model Answer:

| Parameter | Mask ROM | PROM | EPROM | EEPROM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Programming | At fabrication | User, once only | User, multiple times | User, multiple times |
| Erasing | Not possible | Not possible | UV light (20–30 min) | Electrically (byte-level) |
| Cost | Lowest in bulk | Low | Moderate | Higher |
| Speed | Fast | Fast | Fast | Slower (write) |
| Use | Bulk firmware | Prototypes | Development | Config storage |

**Key Distinctions:**
1. **Mask ROM vs PROM**: Mask ROM is programmed during IC fabrication using masks, while PROM is programmed by the user using a special device called a PROM programmer. Mask ROM cannot be erased, while PROM can be programmed only once.
2. **EPROM vs EEPROM**: EPROM requires UV light exposure (20–30 minutes) through a quartz window for erasure, while EEPROM can be erased electrically in bytes, making EEPROM more convenient for in-system updates.
3. **PROM vs EPROM**: PROM is one-time programmable (fuses are blown permanently), while EPROM can be reprogrammed multiple times after UV erasure.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** between two questions. The 14 marks are typically split as **7 + 7** across two sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the internal memory organization of the **Intel 8051 microcontroller** with a neat diagram. Mention the role of **SFRs**, **register banks**, and the **bit-addressable area**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A microcontroller system uses a **16-bit address bus**. The system designer connects a memory chip using address lines **A15–A10** for chip select and the remaining lines **A9–A0** for internal addressing. Calculate:
**(i)** The number of unique memory locations inside the chip.
**(ii)** Total capacity in KB (assuming 8-bit data width).
**(iii)** The starting and ending address of the chip if the active chip select condition is **A15–A10 = 110010**.

---

### Model Answer for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Internal Memory Organization of 8051 (7 Marks)

The **Intel 8051** microcontroller has a Harvard architecture with **separate program and data memory spaces**, both addressable using a 16-bit address bus.

**Program Memory (64 KB, address 0x0000–0xFFFF):**
- This is **read-only** during normal operation and stores the executable code.
- The **reset vector** is located at address **0x0000**, where the CPU begins execution after a reset.
- The **interrupt vectors** are located at addresses **0x0003** to **0x002B** (5 interrupt sources: External 0, Timer 0, External 1, Timer 1, Serial Port).
- The remaining area (0x002C to 0xFFFF) is used for user code.

**Data Memory (Internal + External):**

**A. Internal RAM — 128 bytes (0x00 to 0x7F):**
1. **Register Banks** (0x00–0x1F): Four banks of 8 registers each (R0–R7). Active bank is selected using bits **RS0 and RS1** in the **PSW** (Program Status Word) register.
2. **Bit-Addressable Area** (0x20–0x2F): 16 bytes that can be addressed either as bytes or as 128 individual bits (bit addresses 0x00 to 0x7F).
3. **General Purpose RAM** (0x30–0x7F): 80 bytes used for the stack and user variables.

**B. SFR Space (0x80 to 0xFF):**
The **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** are the control and configuration registers of the 8051. Key SFRs include:
- **Port Registers**: P0 (0x80), P1 (0x90), P2 (0xA0), P3 (0xB0).
- **Accumulator and B register**: ACC (0xE0), B (0xF0).
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** (0x81).
- **Data Pointer (DPTR)** (0x82 + 0x83 — two 8-bit registers forming a 16-bit pointer).
- **Control Registers**: PCON, TCON, TMOD, SCON, IE, IP.

*(SFR Diagram: Refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 3)*

**Role Summary:**
- **SFRs**: Configure and control peripherals (timers, serial port, interrupts, I/O ports).
- **Register Banks**: Fast temporary storage for arithmetic operations.
- **Bit-Addressable Area**: Allows individual bit manipulation (essential for control logic).

---

#### Part (b) — Memory Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Address bus = 16 bits (A15 to A0)
- Chip select lines: A15–A10 (6 lines)
- Internal address lines: A9–A0 (10 lines)
- Data width: 8 bits (1 byte)

**Step (i): Number of Unique Memory Locations**

$$N_{locations} = 2^{\text{internal address lines}} = 2^{10}$$

$$N_{locations} = 1024 \text{ locations}$$

**Step (ii): Total Capacity in KB**

$$C_{bytes} = 2^{10} \times 1 = 1024 \text{ bytes}$$

$$C_{KB} = \frac{1024}{1024} = 1 \text{ KB}$$

**Step (iii): Address Range Calculation**

Active chip select condition: **A15–A10 = 110010**

The 6-bit chip select pattern means the chip is selected when the upper 6 bits equal **110010₂**.

**Start Address:** A9 to A0 = 0000000000 (all zeros), A15–A10 = 110010
$$A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}A_{12}A_{11}A_{10}\,A_{9}A_{8}\cdots A_{0} = 110010\ 00\ 0000\ 0000$$

$$= 1100\ 1000\ 0000\ 0000_2 = 0xC800$$

**End Address:** A9 to A0 = 1111111111 (all ones), A15–A10 = 110010
$$A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}A_{12}A_{11}A_{10}\,A_{9}A_{8}\cdots A_{0} = 110010\ 11\ 1111\ 1111$$

$$= 1100\ 1011\ 1111\ 1111_2 = 0xCBFF$$

**Final Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Unique locations | 1024 |
| Total capacity | 1 KB |
| Start address | 0xC800 |
| End address | 0xCBFF |
| Address range | 0xC800 – 0xCBFF |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the **memory hierarchy** in a microcontroller-based embedded system. List the levels with their **typical capacity, access time, and cost-per-byte**, and discuss why this hierarchy exists.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A system requires **2 MB of RAM** and **512 KB of ROM**. Available chips are **256 KB × 8 RAM** and **64 KB × 8 ROM**.
**(i)** Calculate the number of RAM and ROM chips needed.
**(ii)** Show the address mapping if the RAM starts at **0x00000** and ROM starts at **0x80000** (24-bit address bus).
**(iii)** Briefly explain the difference between **linear decoding** and **partial decoding** with an example.

---

### Model Answer for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Memory Hierarchy (7 Marks)

The **memory hierarchy** is a structured arrangement of different memory types in an embedded system, organized to balance **speed, cost, and capacity**. The principle is: **fastest, smallest, most expensive memory** is placed closest to the CPU, while **slower, larger, cheaper memory** is placed farther away.

**Levels of Memory Hierarchy (from CPU outward):**

| Level | Memory Type | Capacity | Access Time | Cost per Byte | Volatility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 (Highest) | CPU Registers | 32–256 bytes | ≈ 1 ns | Very High | Volatile |
| 2 | L1 Cache | 4–64 KB | 2–5 ns | Very High | Volatile |
| 3 | L2 Cache | 64 KB – 1 MB | 5–20 ns | High | Volatile |
| 4 | Main RAM (SRAM/DRAM) | Few MB – Few GB | 50–100 ns | Moderate | Volatile |
| 5 | Flash/EEPROM | Few MB – GB | 50–200 ns | Low | Non-Volatile |
| 6 (Lowest) | External Storage (SD/HDD) | GB – TB | ms range | Very Low | Non-Volatile |

**Why This Hierarchy Exists:**

1. **Cost Constraint**: High-speed memory (SRAM) costs **~$5–$10 per MB**, while Flash costs **$0.10 per MB** and HDD costs **$0.001 per MB**. Building 1 GB of CPU-grade memory would be prohibitively expensive.
2. **Performance Requirement**: The CPU runs at GHz speeds. If all memory were Flash (200 ns), the CPU would idle for hundreds of cycles waiting for data — destroying performance.
3. **Locality of Reference**: Programs exhibit **temporal locality** (recently accessed data is likely to be accessed again) and **spatial locality** (data near recently accessed data will likely be accessed). This allows small, fast memory to "cache" the working set effectively.
4. **Power Optimization**: Smaller memory cells toggle faster and use less power per access — critical for battery-operated embedded devices.

**KTU Conclusion:** A well-designed memory hierarchy achieves an **"illusion"** of having the speed of SRAM with the capacity of HDD at a reasonable cost.

---

#### Part (b) — Memory Interfacing Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Required RAM: **2 MB**
- Required ROM: **512 KB**
- Available RAM chip: **256 KB × 8**
- Available ROM chip: **64 KB × 8**
- Address bus: **24 bits** (A23 to A0)
- RAM starts at **0x00000**
- ROM starts at **0x80000**

**Step (i): Number of Chips Required**

**For RAM:**
$$N_{RAM} = \frac{2 \text{ MB}}{256 \text{ KB}} = \frac{2048 \text{ KB}}{256 \text{ KB}} = 8 \text{ RAM chips}$$

**For ROM:**
$$N_{ROM} = \frac{512 \text{ KB}}{64 \text{ KB}} = 8 \text{ ROM chips}$$

**Step (ii): Address Mapping**

Each RAM chip has 256 KB = $2^{18}$ bytes, so it needs **18 address lines (A0–A17)**.
Each ROM chip has 64 KB = $2^{16}$ bytes, so it needs **16 address lines (A0–A15)**.

**RAM Region: 0x00000 to 0x1FFFF (2 MB region)**

| Chip | Address Range | Active Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| RAM 0 | 0x00000 – 0x03FFF | A23–A18 = 000000 |
| RAM 1 | 0x04000 – 0x07FFF | A23–A18 = 000001 |
| RAM 2 | 0x08000 – 0x0BFFF | A23–A18 = 000010 |
| RAM 3 | 0x0C000 – 0x0FFFF | A23–A18 = 000011 |
| RAM 4 | 0x10000 – 0x13FFF | A23–A18 = 000100 |
| RAM 5 | 0x14000 – 0x17FFF | A23–A18 = 000101 |
| RAM 6 | 0x18000 – 0x1BFFF | A23–A18 = 000110 |
| RAM 7 | 0x1C000 – 0x1FFFF | A23–A18 = 000111 |

**ROM Region: 0x80000 to 0x9FFFF (512 KB region)**

Each ROM chip = 64 KB = 2¹⁶ bytes (needs A0–A15 internally).

| Chip | Address Range | Active Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ROM 0 | 0x80000 – 0x8FFFF | A23–A16 = 10000000 |
| ROM 1 | 0x90000 – 0x9FFFF | A23–A16 = 10000001 |

**Step (iii): Linear vs Partial Decoding**

**Linear Decoding (Full Decoding):**
- All address lines are used to generate the chip select signal.
- Each memory location has a **unique address** in the system.
- **Example**: For a chip with 4 address lines (A0–A3), the CS is generated using all 4 lines → no aliasing.
- **Advantage**: No ambiguity; **Disadvantage**: Requires more decoding logic (more gates).

**Partial Decoding (Inexpensive Decoding):**
- Some higher address lines are **ignored** in generating CS.
- **Multiple addresses** map to the same physical location (address aliasing).
- **Example**: Chip uses only A0–A3, but system has A0–A7. Lines A4–A7 are ignored. Each location appears **16 times** in the address space (mirror addresses).
- **Advantage**: Simpler, cheaper decoder; **Disadvantage**: Address aliasing, may cause software bugs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing bits vs bytes**: A frequent error is reporting memory capacity in bits when the question asks for bytes (or vice versa). Always check units first.
>
> 2. **Wrong hex range calculation**: When finding the **end address**, students often write $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$. The correct end address of an n-line address chip is **$2^n - 1$**, not $2^n$.
>
> 3. **Mixing SRAM and DRAM characteristics**: Examiners frequently award partial credit for incorrectly stating that DRAM does not need refresh (the opposite is true — DRAM **needs** periodic refresh, SRAM does not).
>
> 4. **Ignoring chip select logic in address mapping**: Many students calculate the address range but forget to mention the **chip select condition** (e.g., "A15–A13 = 000"). This loses 2–3 marks easily.
>
> 5. **Confusing Harvard and Von Neumann**: Harvard has **separate** program and data memories with **separate** buses; Von Neumann has **shared** memory and bus. Mixing these up is a 3-mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Not specifying register bank selection**: In 8051 questions, students often forget to mention that **PSW bits RS0 and RS1** select the active register bank. Examiners expect this detail.
>
> 7. **Skipping unit conversion**: Writing "65536" instead of "64 KB" or "0xFFFF" without showing hex conversion. Always show **both decimal and hex** forms in address mapping answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Memory**

**1. Memory Classification (Must-Know):**
- **Volatile**: SRAM, DRAM (loses data on power-off)
- **Non-Volatile**: ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash (retains data)

**2. SRAM vs DRAM (Most Repeated Comparison):**
- SRAM = 6 transistors, no refresh, fast, expensive, used for cache/registers
- DRAM = 1T + 1C, needs refresh every 64 ms, slow, cheap, used for main RAM

**3. ROM Family (Evolution Order — Memorize This):**
- Mask ROM → PROM (OTP) → EPROM (UV erase) → EEPROM (electrical, byte erase) → Flash (block erase)
- Flash types: **NOR** (random access, code storage) and **NAND** (sequential, bulk data)

**4. 8051 Memory Map (Critical for KTU):**
- Program Memory: 64 KB (0x0000–0xFFFF)
- Internal RAM: 128 bytes (0x00–0x7F)
- SFR Space: 128 bytes (0x80–0xFF)
- Register Banks: 4 banks × 8 registers (0x00–0x1F)
- Bit-Addressable Area: 16 bytes (0x20–0x2F) = 128 bit locations
- External Data Memory: 64 KB (accessed via MOVX)

**5. Memory Capacity Formula (MUST Memorize):**
- $C = 2^n \times d$ bits
- $C = 2^n$ bytes (if d = 8)
- Address range: $0$ to $2^n - 1$

**6. Address Decoding (High-Yield Topic):**
- **Linear Decoding**: All address lines used → no aliasing
- **Partial Decoding**: Some lines ignored → address aliasing (mirrors)
- **74LS138**: 3-to-8 decoder, active-LOW outputs, used for chip select

**7. Memory Hierarchy (Memorize Order Top to Bottom):**
- Registers → L1 Cache → L2 Cache → Main RAM → Flash → External Storage
- Speed decreases downward, capacity increases downward, cost-per-byte decreases downward

**8. Key Constants to Remember:**
- 1 KB = 1024 bytes = $2^{10}$ bytes
- 1 MB = 1024 KB = $2^{20}$ bytes
- 8051 PSEN = Program Store Enable
- DRAM refresh = 64 ms
- Flash endurance = 10,000–100,000 cycles

**9. Common Pitfalls:**
- End address = $2^n - 1$, not $2^n$
- Harvard ≠ Von Neumann (separate vs shared buses)
- DRAM refreshes, SRAM does not
- EPROM erases with UV, EEPROM with electricity
- SFRs are in the 0x80–0xFF range, NOT general-purpose

**10. Exam Pattern (Most Frequently Asked):**
- "Explain memory hierarchy" — 7–10 marks
- "Calculate address range given chip select" — 7 marks
- "Differentiate SRAM/DRAM/ROM variants" — 3–7 marks
- "Explain 8051 memory organization with diagram" — 14 marks
- "Find number of chips to design a memory system" — 7 marks

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 4 — Memory Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> Total Marks Weightage: This topic typically contributes 1 full question in ESE (14 marks) + 1 Part A question (3 marks) = **17 marks per exam cycle**.
> Recommended Study Time: **8–10 hours** for deep understanding + 4 hours for numerical practice.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Interface Programming: Arduino IDE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programming Interface Programming: Arduino IDE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Arduino Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** is an open-source, cross-platform application (Windows, macOS, Linux) that provides a unified textual editor, syntax-aware compiler, board-specific toolchain manager, and a serial communication console, all bundled into a single Graphical User Interface (GUI). It is the canonical programming interface for the Arduino ecosystem and supports the *Arduino Programming Language* — a thin, hardware-oriented abstraction layer built directly on top of **C / C++** with pre-processor directives and a set of standardized Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) for GPIO, ADC, PWM, and timing operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 scheme, the Arduino IDE is treated as the *de-facto introductory toolchain* for embedded systems. The board examiner expects students to know the **sketch lifecycle**, the **setup()/loop() contract**, and the **compilation-to-flash pipeline** (`.ino → .cpp → .elf/.hex → bootloader → flash memory`).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the Arduino IDE as a **modern electronic workshop desk** that an electronics hobbyist would use.

| Workshop Item | Equivalent in Arduino IDE |
|---|---|
| Workbench with tools | The IDE Window (Editor + Toolbar) |
| Screwdriver / Pliers | Built-in Functions (`digitalWrite()`, `analogRead()`) |
| Tool Wall (Hooks) | Library Manager (Plug-in toolkits) |
| Blueprint Sheet | The Sketch (`.ino` file) |
| Power Outlet & Voltage Settings | Board Manager (Selects toolchain per MCU) |
| Smoke Vent / Inspection Lamp | Serial Monitor (Real-time debug output) |
| Sending a parcel (post office) | Compiler + Uploader (delivers code to the chip) |

The **sketch** is the "blueprint" — a plain text file ending in `.ino` that the user writes. The IDE checks the grammar (verify), translates it into machine code (compile), and then physically burns that code into the microcontroller's **non-volatile flash memory** using a built-in *bootloader* — all without the user needing to invoke a separate assembler, linker, or programmer hardware.

### 1.3 Core Architectural Properties

- **Language:** C/C++ with Arduino-specific macros (`HIGH`, `LOW`, `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, `true`, `false`, `LED_BUILTIN`).
- **Compilation Pipeline:** Uses `avr-gcc` (for AVR boards like Uno) or `arm-none-eabi-gcc` (for ARM boards like Due, Zero).
- **Standard Sketch Entry Points:** `void setup()` and `void loop()` — both **mandatory** in every sketch.
- **File Extensions Generated Internally:** `.cpp`, `.h`, `.elf`, `.hex`.
- **Default Baud Rate for Serial Monitor:** **9600 bps** (configurable).

> [!NOTE]
> **Did You Know?** The word "sketch" was chosen by the Arduino founders (Massimo Banzi et al., 2005) to lower the psychological barrier for non-programmers — the same way a designer "sketches" a concept, you "sketch" hardware behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Arduino IDE Window

The IDE is logically partitioned into **five functional regions**. Understanding these regions is a guaranteed **3-mark short question** in KTU ESE.

1. **Editor Pane (Center-Left):** Plain-text editor with C++ syntax highlighting, auto-indent, and brace matching. Multiple tabs allow simultaneous editing of multiple files of the same sketch.
2. **Output Pane (Bottom):** Black console window that reports compilation errors, warnings, upload progress, and hex file size in bytes.
3. **Toolbar (Top):** Five iconic buttons: *Verify (✓)*, *Upload (→)*, *New (📄)*, *Open (📂)*, *Save (💾)*. Modern IDE 2.x also adds *Serial Plotter* and *Debug*.
4. **Menu Bar (Top):** File, Edit, Sketch, Tools, Help. The **Tools** menu is the operational heart — it exposes *Board*, *Port*, *Programmer*, *CPU Clock*, etc.
5. **Status Bar (Bottom):** Displays the currently selected board name and the serial port (e.g., `Arduino Uno on COM5`).

### 2.2 The Mandatory Sketch Contract

Every valid Arduino sketch must contain **two functions** in the global scope:

```cpp
void setup() {
    // Runs ONCE after every power-up or reset.
    // Used for: pin configuration, Serial.begin(), library init.
}

void loop() {
    // Runs INFINITELY in a tight cycle after setup() returns.
    // Used for: main control logic, polling, state machines.
}
```

The runtime semantic is best summarized as:
1. Microcontroller powers ON / RESET is asserted.
2. The **bootloader** (a small pre-burned program) checks for new code on the serial/USB line for ~2 seconds.
3. Control transfers to `main()` (auto-generated), which calls `setup()` exactly once.
4. `loop()` is invoked repeatedly — the implicit scheduler is a single-threaded cooperative `while(1)` superloop.
5. Each `loop()` iteration must complete before the next begins; **blocking calls like `delay()` stall the entire MCU**.

### 2.3 Toolchain Pipeline (Why & How)

The IDE performs six logical steps when you press **Upload (→)**:

| Step | Tool | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Pre-processing | `arduino-builder` | `.ino` | Copy to temp dir, generate function prototypes |
| 2. Compilation | `avr-gcc` | `.cpp` + `.h` + libraries | `.o` object files |
| 3. Linking | `avr-gcc` | `.o` files + core library | `.elf` (executable) |
| 4. Extraction | `avr-objcopy` | `.elf` | `.hex` (Intel HEX format) |
| 5. Upload (Serial) | `avrdude` | `.hex` over UART/USB | Writes to flash via bootloader |
| 6. Reset & Run | Bootloader | — | Jumps to address 0x0000, executes user code |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked "What happens when you click Upload?", do **not** answer "it sends the code". The correct phrasing is: *"The IDE compiles the sketch into an Intel HEX file and uses `avrdude` to send it over the serial/USB port. The bootloader, which is a small pre-existing program in the MCU's flash, receives this hex record stream and writes it into the application flash region, after which it vector-resets to address 0x0000."*

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are tested repeatedly in KTU ESE numerical/analytical questions on Module 4.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADC Digital Value (10-bit) | $\text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{ref}}} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor$ | Volts, default $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0$ V |
| 2 | Voltage from ADC reading | $V_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{\text{ADC}}{1023} \cdot V_{\text{ref}}$ | Volts |
| 3 | PWM Duty Cycle (8-bit) | $\text{Duty \%} = \dfrac{\text{analogWrite}(p)}{255} \cdot 100$ | $p \in [0, 255]$ |
| 4 | Average PWM Voltage | $V_{\text{avg}} = V_{\text{cc}} \cdot \dfrac{p}{255}$ | Volts |
| 5 | Serial Bit Duration | $T_{\text{bit}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | Seconds per bit |
| 6 | Serial Byte Time (8N1) | $T_{\text{byte}} = \dfrac{10}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop |
| 7 | Time per ADC sample | $T_{\text{ADC}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{ADC}}}$ | Default prescaler = 128 |
| 8 | Instruction Cycle Time | $T_{\text{cyc}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}$ | 16 MHz → 62.5 ns |
| 9 | Delay Resolution | $\Delta t_{\min} = 1\ \mu\text{s}$ | For `delayMicroseconds()` |
| 10 | Flash Endurance | $N_{\text{erase/write}} \approx 10{,}000$ cycles | Per sector, ATMega328P |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX / Markdown Pitfall:** In all markdown tables, do **not** write the absolute-value bar as `|x|`. Use the LaTeX escape `\vert x \vert` or `\left\vert x \right\vert` to prevent the parser from misreading it as a column separator.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Arduino IDE is the de-facto *on-ramp* for nearly every modern embedded toolchain. Industrial variants such as **PlatformIO**, **Energia** (for MSP430/Tiva), **Mbed Studio**, and even the **STM32CubeIDE** inherit its project structure (`.ino` → `.cpp`). Mastery of the Arduino workflow is therefore a *transferable* skill: a student who understands `setup()/loop()`, `pinMode()`, and `Serial.print()` can transition to bare-metal STM32 or ESP-IDF with minimal friction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

This section is **exhaustive by mandate** — no shortcut phrasing, no truncation. Every algebraic transition is fully elaborated.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Converting an ADC Reading to Voltage

> A thermistor is connected to `A0` of an Arduino Uno (5 V reference). The `analogRead()` returns a value of `731`. Compute the corresponding input voltage and the duty cycle required on Pin 9 to mirror the same fractional level via PWM.

**Step 1 — Identify the constants.**

$$V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0\ \text{V}, \quad \text{Resolution} = 10\ \text{bit} \Rightarrow 2^{10} = 1024\ \text{levels}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage-from-ADC formula.**

$$V_{\text{in}} = \frac{\text{ADC}}{1023} \cdot V_{\text{ref}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{in}} &= \frac{731}{1023} \cdot 5.0 \\
&= 0.71456 \cdot 5.0 \\
&= 3.5728\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to PWM duty cycle (8-bit, 0–255).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{ref}}} \cdot 255 \\
&= \frac{3.5728}{5.0} \cdot 255 \\
&= 0.71456 \cdot 255 \\
&= 182.21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Round and apply.**

$$p = 182 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \texttt{analogWrite(9, 182);}$$

**Step 6 — Compute resulting average PWM voltage (sanity check).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{avg}} &= V_{\text{cc}} \cdot \frac{p}{255} \\
&= 5.0 \cdot \frac{182}{255} \\
&= 3.5686\ \text{V} \quad (\text{error } \approx 4\ \text{mV, well within LSB})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **LSB Sanity Check:** One LSB in voltage is $\dfrac{5.0}{1023} \approx 4.886\ \text{mV}$. The 4 mV error above is less than 1 LSB — acceptable for any practical application.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Serial Communication Time Calculation

> The Serial Monitor is opened at **115200 baud**, 8N1 configuration. How long does it take to transmit the ASCII string `"HELLO\n"`?

**Step 1 — Determine the number of bits per character.**

For 8N1, every character is framed with **1 start bit + 8 data bits + 1 stop bit = 10 bits**.

**Step 2 — Count total characters.**

`"HELLO\n"` = 5 letters + 1 newline = 6 characters.

**Step 3 — Total bits.**

$$
N_{\text{bits}} = 6 \cdot 10 = 60\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply bit-time relation.**

$$
T_{\text{bit}} = \frac{1}{\text{Baud}} = \frac{1}{115200} = 8.6805\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Total transmission time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= N_{\text{bits}} \cdot T_{\text{bit}} \\
&= 60 \cdot 8.6805\ \mu\text{s} \\
&= 520.83\ \mu\text{s} \\
&\approx 0.521\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.** At 115200 baud, even a 100-byte payload (1000 bits) takes only $\approx 8.68$ ms — sufficient for real-time telemetry in robotic control loops (typical 10 ms update rate).

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Sketch Anatomy Walkthrough

Consider the canonical **"Blink"** sketch:

```cpp
// File: Blink.ino
#define LED 13           // Preprocessor macro substitution

void setup() {
    pinMode(LED, OUTPUT);  // Configure pin 13 as push-pull output
}

void loop() {
    digitalWrite(LED, HIGH); // Drive pin HIGH (~5 V)
    delay(1000);             // Block for 1000 ms
    digitalWrite(LED, LOW);  // Drive pin LOW (0 V)
    delay(1000);             // Block for 1000 ms
}
```

**Step-by-step execution analysis:**

1. **Pre-processing:** The preprocessor replaces every occurrence of `LED` with literal `13` before compilation. The `setup()` and `loop()` symbols are exposed in the global namespace.
2. **Auto-prototype generation:** `arduino-builder` scans the sketch and synthesizes forward declarations for any user-defined function that appears *after* its first call site, eliminating the C++ "implicit int" rule.
3. **`mainCRTStartup` (hidden):** The linker pulls in a `main()` function defined inside the Arduino core that invokes `init()` (timer/UART setup), then `setup()`, then a tight `for(;;)` loop calling `loop()`.
4. **First iteration:** `setup()` configures pin 13. `loop()` turns the LED on, blocks 1 s, turns it off, blocks 1 s. Total period = 2 s. The MCU spends ~100 % of one core's time inside `loop()` between `delay()` returns.
5. **Reset / power-cycle:** The bootloader re-runs, but since no new upload is detected, it immediately vectors to the existing application flash region at 0x0000.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Built-in Functions Quick Reference

| Function | Header | Arguments | Returns | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `pinMode(pin, mode)` | `Arduino.h` | `pin:int`, `mode:INPUT/OUTPUT/INPUT_PULLUP` | `void` | Configure GPIO direction |
| `digitalWrite(pin, val)` | `Arduino.h` | `pin:int`, `val:HIGH/LOW` | `void` | Drive output pin |
| `digitalRead(pin)` | `Arduino.h` | `pin:int` | `HIGH` or `LOW` | Sample input pin |
| `analogRead(pin)` | `Arduino.h` | `pin:int` (A0–A5) | `int` ∈ [0, 1023] | 10-bit ADC read |
| `analogWrite(pin, val)` | `Arduino.h` | `pin:int`, `val:int` ∈ [0, 255] | `void` | 8-bit PWM output |
| `delay(ms)` | `Arduino.h` | `ms:unsigned long` | `void` | Blocking millisecond delay |
| `delayMicroseconds(us)` | `Arduino.h` | `us:unsigned int` | `void` | Blocking microsecond delay |
| `millis()` | `Arduino.h` | — | `unsigned long` | Time since boot (ms) |
| `micros()` | `Arduino.h` | — | `unsigned long` | Time since boot (µs) |
| `Serial.begin(baud)` | `HardwareSerial.h` | `baud:long` | `void` | Initialize UART |
| `Serial.print(data)` | `HardwareSerial.h` | any | `size_t` | ASCII print |
| `Serial.write(buf, len)` | `HardwareSerial.h` | buffer, length | `size_t` | Binary write |
| `attachInterrupt(pin, ISR, mode)` | `Arduino.h` | pin, function pointer, trigger | `void` | External interrupt hookup |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Arduino IDE Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional subsystems of the IDE** and how data flows from the user keystroke to physical pin toggling on the ATmega328P.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Types Sketch in Editor] --> B[Verify: arduino-builder]
    B --> C{Syntax OK?}
    C -- No --> D[Output Pane: Error Log]
    C -- Yes --> E[Compile: avr-gcc Toolchain]
    E --> F[Link: Core + Libraries + User]
    F --> G[Generate Intel HEX File]
    G --> H[Upload: avrdude via USB-UART]
    H --> I[Bootloader on MCU Receives HEX]
    I --> J[Flash Memory Programming]
    J --> K[Vector Reset to 0x0000]
    K --> L[setup runs ONCE]
    L --> M[loop runs INFINITELY]
    M --> N[Peripheral Interaction: GPIO, ADC, PWM, UART]
    N --> O[External Hardware: LED, Sensor, Motor]
    O -. feedback .-> A
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram is cyclic — the **external hardware** may be sensed, and that data may re-inform the sketch in the next `loop()` iteration. This closed loop is the essence of any embedded control system.

### 4.2 Diagram B — Sketch Compilation State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PowerON
    PowerON --> Bootloader: Reset asserted
    Bootloader --> WaitForUpload: Check UART for 2s
    WaitForUpload --> UploadMode: New HEX detected
    WaitForUpload --> RunApp: Timeout / No data
    UploadMode --> FlashWrite: avrdude streams HEX
    FlashWrite --> VerifyFlash: CRC check
    VerifyFlash --> RunApp: Pass
    VerifyFlash --> UploadMode: Fail retry
    RunApp --> setup: Call setup() once
    setup --> loop: Begin infinite loop
    loop --> loop: Iterative execution
    loop --> [*]: Power off or WDT reset
```

**Interpretation:** This state machine is the **exact lifecycle** every Arduino board follows on power-up. Memorize the `WaitForUpload → UploadMode → RunApp` triplet — it is the most commonly asked conceptual question in KTU ESE.

### 4.3 Diagram C — Memory Map of ATmega328P (Programmer's View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FLASH["Flash Memory 32 KB"]
        F0["0x0000: Bootloader (512 B)"]
        F1["0x7E00: Application Start"]
        F2["0x7FFF: End of Flash"]
    end
    subgraph SRAM["SRAM 2 KB"]
        S0["0x0100: Registers and I/O"]
        S1["0x0100-0x08FF: Data, Stack, Heap"]
    end
    subgraph EEPROM["EEPROM 1 KB"]
        E0["0x0000: User persistent data"]
    end
    F1 -.compiled hex.-> SRAM
    SRAM -.volatile state.-> FLASH
    EEPROM -.long-term.-> SRAM
```

**Interpretation:** The bootloader occupies the **first 512 bytes** (lock-bit configured). The application — your sketch — lives from `0x7E00` upward. The lock bits prevent the bootloader from being overwritten by accident during an upload. This is why burning the wrong bootloader on a fresh ATmega328P chip requires a high-voltage parallel programmer to recover.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Module 4 typically contributes 15–20 % of the total marks. The Arduino IDE topic appears as either Part A (3 marks) or as a sub-part of a 14-mark Part B question. The two questions below are calibrated to the *actual* KTU board style.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 3 Marks
**Explain the structure of an Arduino sketch with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

An Arduino sketch is a plain-text source file with extension `.ino` that the Arduino IDE compiles and uploads to a microcontroller board. Every sketch must contain two **mandatory** functions:

1. **`void setup()`** — This function executes **exactly once** when the board is powered on or reset. It is conventionally used for one-time initializations such as configuring pin modes using `pinMode()`, starting serial communication with `Serial.begin()`, and initializing sensor and actuator libraries.

2. **`void loop()`** — After `setup()` returns, this function is called **repeatedly and infinitely** in a tight superloop. It contains the main control logic — reading sensors, toggling outputs, performing computations, and updating actuators.

The IDE automatically synthesizes a hidden `main()` function (in `main.cpp` of the Arduino core) that calls `init()` to configure the hardware timers and UART, then `setup()`, then enters a `for(;;)` loop that repeatedly invokes `loop()`. Sketch files may be split across multiple tabs in the IDE, but `setup()` and `loop()` must appear in the global scope of *one* `.ino` file.

**Example — LED Blink:**

```cpp
void setup() {
    pinMode(LED_BUILTIN, OUTPUT);
}
void loop() {
    digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, HIGH);
    delay(1000);
    digitalWrite(LED_BUILTIN, LOW);
    delay(1000);
}
```

Here `LED_BUILTIN` is a board-specific macro that resolves to `13` on the Uno, `9` on the Nano 33 IoT, etc.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 3 Marks
**What is the role of the bootloader in an Arduino board?**

**Model Answer:**

The **bootloader** is a small pre-burned program (typically 0.5 – 2 KB in size) residing in a protected region of the microcontroller's flash memory. It performs three critical functions on every power-up or reset:

1. **Self-Initialization:** It configures the UART and the watchdog timer immediately after reset.
2. **Handshake Window:** It waits for approximately **0.5 – 2 seconds** for an incoming HEX file from the host PC over the serial/USB link. The protocol used is the **STK500** protocol (or a derivative such as Optiboot).
3. **Flash Programming:** If a valid stream is detected, the bootloader erases the application flash region and writes the new program into it, then vector-resets the MCU to address `0x0000` to begin execution.

If no upload activity is detected within the handshake window, the bootloader exits and execution transfers to the **previously written application code** at the top of the application region (e.g., `0x7E00` on the ATmega328P). Thus, the bootloader eliminates the need for an external In-System Programmer (ISP), making Arduino boards "plug-and-play" programmable over a plain USB cable.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the major components of the Arduino IDE and the complete flow of compilation and uploading of a sketch. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the digital I/O functions `pinMode()`, `digitalWrite()`, and `digitalRead()`. Show how an external LED and a push-button are interfaced. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Components and Upload Flow [7 Marks]:**

The Arduino IDE consists of **five major components**:

1. **Text Editor** — A syntax-highlighted plain-text editor where the user writes the `.ino` sketch. Supports multiple tabs, auto-indent, brace matching, and search/replace (Ctrl+F).

2. **Menu Bar and Toolbar** — The toolbar has five iconic buttons: *Verify* (compile only), *Upload* (compile + flash), *New*, *Open*, *Save*. The *Tools* menu exposes board selection, port selection, programmer type, and CPU clock configuration.

3. **Output / Console Pane** — Displays compilation diagnostics: errors with line numbers, warnings, hex file size in bytes, and upload progress messages from `avrdude`.

4. **Library Manager** — A built-in package manager that fetches and integrates third-party libraries from the official Arduino repository. Imported libraries are listed under *Sketch → Include Library*.

5. **Serial Monitor and Serial Plotter** — A bidirectional terminal window (Tools → Serial Monitor, default **9600 8N1**) for sending and receiving ASCII or binary data over the USB-UART bridge. The *Serial Plotter* renders numeric values as a real-time strip chart.

**Upload Flow — Six Logical Stages:**

| Stage | Tool | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Pre-process | `arduino-builder` | Expands macros, generates function prototypes, copies files to temp dir |
| 2. Compile | `avr-gcc` (AVR boards) or `arm-none-eabi-gcc` (ARM boards) | Translates `.cpp`/`.h` to `.o` object files |
| 3. Link | `avr-gcc` / `arm-none-eabi-gcc` | Combines objects with Arduino core library; produces `.elf` |
| 4. Convert | `avr-objcopy` | Strips ELF metadata → Intel `.hex` |
| 5. Upload | `avrdude` (AVR) or `bossac` / `st-flash` (ARM) | Streams HEX over USB-UART to bootloader |
| 6. Reset & Run | Bootloader | Verifies flash, vector-resets to `0x0000`, `setup()` runs once, then `loop()` |

---

**Model Solution (b) — Digital I/O and External Interfacing [7 Marks]:**

**Theoretical Background:** The ATmega328P has **14 digital I/O pins** (D0–D13), each of which can be configured as input or output. The `pinMode()`, `digitalWrite()`, and `digitalRead()` functions are the three primitives the Arduino API exposes for bit-level control.

**Hardware Setup:**
- **LED** wired between D13 and GND through a **220 Ω current-limiting resistor**.
- **Push-button** between D2 and GND (using internal pull-up) — no external resistor needed.

**Example Sketch — Button-Controlled LED:**

```cpp
#define LED  13
#define BTN   2

void setup() {
    pinMode(LED, OUTPUT);
    pinMode(BTN, INPUT_PULLUP);   // Enable internal 20-50 kOhm pull-up
    Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
    bool pressed = (digitalRead(BTN) == LOW);   // Active-low
    digitalWrite(LED, pressed ? HIGH : LOW);
    Serial.println(pressed ? "PRESSED" : "RELEASED");
    delay(50);   // 50 ms software debounce
}
```

**Function Reference:**

- `pinMode(pin, mode)` — Configures the Data Direction Register (DDR) of `pin`. `OUTPUT` sets the bit, `INPUT` clears it, `INPUT_PULLUP` clears it and enables the internal pull-up by writing to the PORT register.
- `digitalWrite(pin, value)` — Writes `HIGH` (5 V) or `LOW` (0 V) to the PORT register of `pin`. Internally maps to `PORTB`, `PORTC`, or `PORTD` bit manipulation.
- `digitalRead(pin)` — Samples the PIN register of `pin` and returns `HIGH` or `LOW`. Reading is atomic per pin in the Arduino API.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — KTU Valuation:** Students often write `pinMode(pin, INPUT_PULLUP)` for a button wired to **+5 V** (active-high). This **inverts** the logic and leaves the input floating when the button is open. Always confirm: pull-up → button to GND → reads LOW when pressed.

---

#### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the `analogRead()` and `analogWrite()` functions of Arduino. Discuss the 10-bit ADC and 8-bit PWM resolution with a numerical example of converting an analog sensor value to a corresponding PWM duty cycle. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain how the Arduino `Serial` library is used to establish UART communication with a host PC. Discuss baud rate, `Serial.print()` vs `Serial.write()`, and the role of `Serial.begin()`. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a) — analogRead and analogWrite [7 Marks]:**

**`analogRead(pin)` — 10-bit ADC:**
- The ATmega328P contains a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** with **10-bit resolution**, yielding integer values in the range **[0, 1023]**.
- Default reference voltage is $V_{\text{ref}} = 5.0$ V, set by `analogReference(DEFAULT)`.
- The conversion takes approximately **100 µs** per channel (ADC clock = 125 kHz, 13 cycles per conversion).
- The mapping formula is: $\text{ADC} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{ref}}} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor$.

**`analogWrite(pin, value)` — 8-bit PWM:**
- Generates a Pulse-Width Modulated square wave on pins **3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11** (Timer0, Timer1, Timer2 controlled).
- `value` is an 8-bit integer in **[0, 255]**; `0` = 0 % duty cycle (always LOW), `255` = 100 % duty cycle (always HIGH).
- Default PWM frequency is approximately **490 Hz** on most pins (Timer0 pins D5, D6 run at 980 Hz).
- Mapping formula: $\text{Duty \%} = \dfrac{\text{value}}{255} \cdot 100$.

**Numerical Example:**

A photoresistor (LDR) is connected to `A0`. The `analogRead()` returns `512`. The user wants to drive a DC motor via PWM on pin 9 such that the motor's average voltage matches the LDR's fractional level.

**Step 1 — Convert ADC to a fractional level.**

$$\text{Level} = \frac{512}{1023} = 0.5005$$

**Step 2 — Compute PWM value.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= 0.5005 \cdot 255 \\
  &= 127.62 \\
  &\approx 128
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply in code.**

```cpp
int p = 128;
analogWrite(9, p);
```

**Step 4 — Verify average voltage.**

$$V_{\text{avg}} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{128}{255} = 2.510\ \text{V}$$

This matches the LDR's measured level of $5.0 \cdot 0.5005 = 2.502$ V within $\sim$ 8 mV (≈ 2 LSBs of the 10-bit ADC).

---

**Model Solution (b) — UART Communication with Serial Library [7 Marks]:**

**Theoretical Foundation:** The ATmega328P has a single hardware UART (USART0). On the Arduino Uno, this is exposed on digital pins **D0 (RX)** and **D1 (TX)** and is also internally bridged to the USB-UART converter (ATmega16U2 on R3 boards).

**Role of `Serial.begin(baud)`:**
- Configures the baud-rate registers (UBRR), frame format (default 8N1: 8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit), and enables the transmitter and receiver.
- Example: `Serial.begin(9600)` sets the bit rate to 9600 bps. The **bit duration** is:
$$T_{\text{bit}} = \frac{1}{9600} = 104.17\ \mu\text{s}$$

**`Serial.print()` vs `Serial.write()`:**

| Feature | `Serial.print()` | `Serial.write()` |
|---|---|---|
| Argument Type | Any (int, float, char*, String) | Byte / buffer / `char*` |
| Conversion | ASCII text representation | Raw binary bytes |
| Use Case | Human-readable debug output | Binary protocols, image/audio |
| Default Format | Decimal for numbers | None — sends raw byte |
| Example | `Serial.print(65);` → outputs `65` | `Serial.write(65);` → outputs `A` |

**Example Sketch — Serial Echo with Formatted Output:**

```cpp
void setup() {
    Serial.begin(115200);
    while (!Serial) { /* wait for native USB boards */ }
    Serial.println("Arduino Ready");
}

void loop() {
    if (Serial.available() > 0) {
        char c = Serial.read();
        Serial.print("Got: ");
        Serial.print(c);
        Serial.print("  HEX: 0x");
        Serial.println(c, HEX);
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**

1. `Serial.begin(115200)` initializes the UART at 115200 bps.
2. `while(!Serial)` is a USB-CDC specific guard for boards like Leonardo / Micro.
3. `Serial.println("Arduino Ready")` sends the ASCII bytes plus `\r\n` line endings.
4. Inside `loop()`, `Serial.available()` returns the number of bytes waiting in the receive buffer (default 64 bytes).
5. `Serial.read()` fetches **one byte** (returns -1 if empty).
6. `Serial.print(c, HEX)` formats the byte as uppercase hex (e.g., `0x41` for `'A'`).
7. `Serial.println` appends `\r\n` for terminal-style newlines.

**Engineering Caveat:** At 115200 baud, the **maximum throughput** is approximately $11.52$ KB/s. For real-time applications, you must ensure the `loop()` body finishes before the 64-byte hardware FIFO overruns. If it does, characters are silently dropped — a notorious source of bugs in robotics and sensor-fusion projects.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The most common mistake students make in KTU Module-4 questions is **confusing `Serial.print()` with `Serial.println()`** — the former does not add a newline, the latter does. Additionally, students often forget that `Serial.write(int)` sends the **byte-level** value, not the ASCII representation. If the question explicitly asks for *hex dump* or *binary protocol*, the answer must use `write()`, not `print()`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**

- ✅ Arduino IDE is **open-source, cross-platform**, distributed under LGPL.
- ✅ Every sketch has **two mandatory functions**: `setup()` (runs once) and `loop()` (runs forever).
- ✅ Default sketch extension is **`.ino`**.
- ✅ Compilation pipeline: `.ino` → `.cpp`/`.h` → `.o` → `.elf` → **`.hex`** → `avrdude` → bootloader → flash.
- ✅ Compiler for AVR boards: **`avr-gcc`**. Uploader: **`avrdude`**. Protocol: **STK500 / Optiboot**.
- ✅ The **bootloader** waits ~0.5–2 s for a new upload; if none, it jumps to the application.
- ✅ ADC is **10-bit** → range `[0, 1023]`; formula $V_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{\text{ADC}}{1023} \cdot V_{\text{ref}}$.
- ✅ PWM is **8-bit** → range `[0, 255]`; duty cycle $\% = \dfrac{p}{255} \cdot 100$.
- ✅ `analogWrite()` works only on pins **3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11** of the Uno.
- ✅ `Serial.begin(baud)` initializes UART in **8N1** by default.
- ✅ Default Serial Monitor baud rate is **9600**; the code's `Serial.begin()` argument **must match** the monitor's selection.
- ✅ `delay()` is **blocking** and uses Timer0; do not call `analogWrite()` on pins 5 or 6 while changing `delay()` behavior in advanced sketches.
- ✅ `INPUT_PULLUP` enables an internal ~20–50 kΩ pull-up — eliminates the need for an external resistor on a button to GND.
- ✅ The IDE's *Verify* button only compiles; *Upload* compiles + flashes.
- ✅ KTU ESE almost always asks one of: (1) sketch structure, (2) bootloader role, (3) compilation pipeline, (4) ADC/PWM numerical.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sample Code (LED
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBBRT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Sample Code (LED # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **LED Interfacing with Microcontrollers** is the process of configuring a microcontroller's General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pins to drive a Light Emitting Diode (LED) using embedded C or assembly code, ensuring that the current through the LED is limited to a safe value through a series resistor calculated using Ohm's Law. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, sample code for LED control is treated as the foundational *Hello World* program of embedded systems. It validates the complete toolchain: editor $\rightarrow$ compiler $\rightarrow$ assembler $\rightarrow$ linker $\rightarrow$ programmer/debugger $\rightarrow$ target hardware. The student is expected to demonstrate competence in: 1. Configuring the **Data Direction Register (DDR)** or **GPIO Mode Register (GPIOx_MODER)**. 2. Writing to the **Port Output Register (PORT)** or **GPIO Output Data Register (GPIOx_ODR)**. 3. Inserting software delays calibrated to the system clock. 4. Toggling bits using bitwise XOR, AND, OR, and NOT operations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Building Manager and the Lobby Lights** > Think of the microcontroller as a **building manager** sitting in a control room. The manager cannot directly illuminate the *lobby LED*; instead, the manager controls a **wall switch** (the GPIO pin). The switch has two stable states: **UP = ON (logic 1, e.g., 3.3 V)** and **DOWN = OFF (logic 0, 0 V)**. A *current-limiting resistor* acts as a fuse box that prevents the wires from catching fire if the manager leaves the switch UP for too long. Writing to the GPIO register is the manager **flipping the switch**, while reading the same register later tells the manager *which way the switch is currently pointing*. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Standard Red LED) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Forward Voltage | $V_f$ | **1.8 – 2.2** | V | | Forward Current | $I_f$ | **10 – 20** | mA | | Microcontroller Logic High | $V_{OH}$ | **3.3** (or 5.0) | V | | Microcontroller Logic Low | $V_{OL}$ | **0.0 – 0.4** | V | | Source/Sink Current per pin | $I_{IO}$ | **$\pm$ 8 – 25** | mA | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of an LED-Blink Program A typical LED sample code executes the following sequence at runtime: 1. **Disable Interrupts (Optional but recommended)**: The program masks all maskable interrupts during the critical initialization phase to ensure deterministic pin behavior. 2. **Enable Peripheral Clock**: For ARM Cortex-M based MCUs, the relevant GPIO port clock must be enabled in the **RCC (Reset and Clock Control)** register, e.g., `RCC->AHB1ENR |= (1 << 0);` for GPIOA. 3. **Configure Pin as Output**: Set the corresponding bit in the DDR (8051) or MODER register (ARM). 4. **Initialize Pin State**: Drive the pin LOW (LED OFF) or HIGH (LED ON) by writing to the PORT/ODR register. 5. **Enter Infinite Loop**: Toggle the pin and call a software delay function. > [!TIP] > **Why do we need a current-limiting resistor?** > An LED is a *non-ohmic* device. Once the applied voltage exceeds $V_f$, its internal resistance collapses to nearly zero. Without an external resistor, the current would rise until either the LED or the microcontroller pin is destroyed. The series resistor restores linearity and protects the system. ## 2.2 Current-Limiting Resistor Derivation (Ohm's Law Application) Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the loop: $V_{CC} = V_R + V_f$. Solving for the resistance: $$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_f}$$ ### KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Always replace absolute-value bars with `\vert` in markdown tables. The vertical pipe `|` breaks the table syntax.** | Formula | Description | Variables | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | $R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_f}$ | Series resistor for LED | $V_{CC}$, $V_f$, $I_f$ | $\Omega$ | | $P_R = I_f^2 \cdot R$ | Power dissipated by resistor | $I_f$, $R$ | W | | $P_{LED} = V_f \cdot I_f$ | Power consumed by LED | $V_f$, $I_f$ | W | | $T_{delay} = N \cdot \frac{C}{f_{CLK}}$ | Software delay (loop-based) | $N$, $C$, $f_{CLK}$ | s | | $f_{blink} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot T_{delay}}$ | Blinking frequency | $T_{delay}$ | Hz | | $D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}$ | PWM duty cycle (brightness) | $T_{ON}$, $T_{OFF}$ | unitless | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility LED interfacing underpins almost every commercial embedded product: - **Status Indicators**: Power-ON, charging, fault, and link-state LEDs in routers and IoT devices. - **User Feedback**: HMI panels in washing machines, microwave ovens, and automotive dashboards. - **Visual Debugging**: Embedded developers use "blink-based debugging" — toggling an LED with different cadences to signal error codes in the absence of a debugger (the so-called *printf-of-the-embedded-world*). - **Trickle Diagnostics**: Heartbeat LEDs on server motherboards confirm kernel liveness. ## 2.4 Bitwise Operations Used in Toggling | Operation | C Operator | Effect on pin bit | |---|---|---| | Set bit HIGH | `OR = (1 << n)` | Force pin to logic 1 | | Clear bit LOW | `AND = ~(1 << n)` | Force pin to logic 0 | | Toggle bit | `XOR = (1 << n)` | Flip current state | | Read bit | `AND` then shift | Isolate current state | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Current-Limiting Resistor Calculation **Problem Statement:** A red LED ($V_f = 2.0$ V, $I_f = 15$ mA) is connected to a **3.3 V** GPIO pin of an ARM Cortex-M0 microcontroller. Compute the value of the current-limiting series resistor and the power dissipated by it. State the nearest standard E12 resistor value. ### Step 1: Identify the knowns We extract the parameters from the problem: $$V_{CC} = 3.3 \text{ V}, \quad V_f = 2.0 \text{ V}, \quad I_f = 15 \text{ mA} = 0.015 \text{ A}$$ ### Step 2: Apply the KVL-derived formula We substitute the values into the governing equation: $$\begin{aligned} R &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_f} \\ &= \frac{3.3 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V}}{0.015 \text{ A}} \\ &= \frac{1.3 \text{ V}}{0.015 \text{ A}} \\ &= 86.67 \text{ }\Omega \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3: Convert to a practical E12 value The next higher standard E12 value is **$R = 100 \text{ }\Omega$**, which guarantees $I_f$ remains below the safe maximum while being inexpensive and widely available. ### Step 4: Recompute the actual current at $R = 100 \text{ }\Omega$ We reapply Ohm's law to verify: $$\begin{aligned} I_{actual} &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{R} \\ &= \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{100} \\ &= \frac{1.3}{100} \\ &= 0.013 \text{ A} = 13 \text{ mA} \end{aligned}$$ This value lies comfortably within the safe operating range of **10 – 20 mA**. ### Step 5: Power dissipated by the resistor $$\begin{aligned} P_R &= I_{actual}^2 \cdot R \\ &= (0.013)^2 \cdot 100 \\ &= 0.000169 \cdot 100 \\ &= 0.0169 \text{ W} \approx 17 \text{ mW} \end{aligned}$$ A standard **$1/8$ W (125 mW) resistor** provides a safety margin of roughly $7\times$. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Software Delay Calibration **Problem Statement:** A 8051 microcontroller runs at $f_{CLK} = 11.0592$ MHz. Each machine cycle is 12 clock cycles. An LED is connected to P1.0. Write and analyze a delay routine to produce approximately **500 ms** of delay using nested C loops. ### Step 1: Determine instruction time The period of one machine cycle is: $$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$ ### Step 2: Determine number of iterations Assuming a simple decrement-and-branch loop consuming approximately **8 machine cycles per iteration**: $$\text{Iterations} = \frac{T_{delay}}{8 \cdot T_{MC}} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 57,605$$ ### Step 3: Nest the loops We split the count into two nested loops of $\sim 240 \times 240 \approx 57,600$ iterations to fit within 8-bit counters. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Code-to-Hardware Mapping | Code Construct | Hardware Effect | Register Touched | Voltage Observed at Pin | |---|---|---|---| | `GPIOA->MODER \|= (1 << 0);` | Set PA0 as output | GPIOA_MODER | — (mode set) | | `GPIOA->BSRR = (1 << 0);` | Atomic set PA0 HIGH | GPIOA_BSRR | $\approx 3.3$ V | | `GPIOA->BSRR = (1 << 16);` | Atomic reset PA0 LOW | GPIOA_BSRR | $\approx 0$ V | | `GPIOA->ODR ^= (1 << 0);` | Toggle PA0 | GPIOA_ODR | Flips 0 $\leftrightarrow$ 3.3 V | | `RCC->AHB1ENR \|= (1 << 0);` | Enable GPIOA clock | RCC_AHB1ENR | — (bus active) | | `__NOP();` | Consume 1 cycle | — | — | ## 3.4 Full Reference Sample Code (ARM Cortex-M / 8051 Hybrid Analysis) Below is the canonical KTU-style sample code for blinking an LED on PA5 (STM32) and P1.0 (8051), followed by an annotated walkthrough. **C code for ARM Cortex-M (STM32 HAL-style):** ```c #include "stm32f4xx.h" void delay_ms(uint32_t ms) { for (uint32_t i = 0; i < ms * 16000; i++) { __NOP(); } } int main(void) { RCC->AHB1ENR |= (1 << 0); // Enable GPIOA clock GPIOA->MODER &= ~(3 << (5 * 2)); // Clear MODER bits for PA5 GPIOA->MODER |= (1 << (5 * 2)); // Set PA5 as output (01) while (1) { GPIOA->BSRR = (1 << 5); // Set PA5 HIGH (LED ON) delay_ms(500); GPIOA->BSRR = (1 << (5 + 16)); // Reset PA5 LOW (LED OFF) delay_ms(500); } } ``` **C code for 8051 (Keil-style):** ```c #include sbit LED = P1^0; void delay(void) { unsigned int i, j; for (i = 0; i < 200; i++) for (j = 0; j < 250; j++); } void main(void) { LED = 0; // Initialize LED OFF while (1) { LED = ~LED; // Toggle LED delay(); // ~500 ms } } ``` # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hardware Connection Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU[Microcontroller
GPIO Pin PA5] R[Current Limiting
Resistor R = 100 ohm] LED[LED
Vf = 2.0 V If = 15 mA] GND[Ground
0 V Reference] VCC[Supply Rail
3.3 V DC] MCU -- Logic HIGH 3.3 V --> R R -- Drop 1.3 V --> LED LED -- Cathode return --> GND VCC -.Internal rail.-> MCU ``` ## 4.2 Program Execution Flow Chart ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Power On Reset]) --> initClk[Enable GPIOA Clock
RCC AHB1ENR] initClk --> initMode[Configure PA5 as Output
MODER 5 to 01] initMode --> initLow[Drive PA5 LOW
BSRR bit 21] initLow --> loopStart{Loop Forever} loopStart --> setHigh[BSRR bit 5 set HIGH] setHigh --> d1[Delay ~500 ms] d1 --> setLow[BSRR bit 21 set LOW] setLow --> d2[Delay ~500 ms] d2 --> loopStart ``` ## 4.3 Register-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph RCC_Block[Reset and Clock Control] AHBEN[RCC AHB1ENR] end subgraph GPIOA_Block[GPIOA Peripheral] MODER[GPIOA MODER] ODR[GPIOA ODR] BSRR[GPIOA BSRR] end PAD[Physical Pad PA5] AHBEN -- Bit 0 enables --> GPIOA_Block MODER -- Mode bits 11 10 --> PAD ODR -- Data bit 5 --> PAD BSRR -- Atomic set reset --> PAD ``` ## 4.4 LED Blinking State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> LED_OFF LED_OFF --> LED_ON : Set pin HIGH
Delay 500 ms LED_ON --> LED_OFF : Set pin LOW
Delay 500 ms LED_OFF --> LED_OFF : System reset LED_ON --> LED_ON : System reset ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** ### Question A1 **Explain the role of a current-limiting resistor in an LED interfacing circuit. Derive the formula used to compute its value for a red LED ($V_f = 2$ V, $I_f = 10$ mA) connected to a 5 V GPIO pin.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) The current-limiting resistor restricts the current through the LED to a safe value, preventing thermal damage to the LED and excessive loading of the GPIO pin. - (1 mark) Applying KVL: $V_{CC} = V_R + V_f \Rightarrow R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_f}$ - (1 mark) Substituting: $R = \frac{5 - 2}{0.010} = 300 \text{ }\Omega$. The nearest E12 standard value is **$330 \text{ }\Omega$**. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** ### Question A2 **Differentiate between `BSRR` and `ODR` registers in an STM32 microcontroller. Why is `BSRR` preferred for atomic bit manipulation?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) `ODR` is the *Output Data Register* that holds the current state of all GPIO pins of a port. `BSRR` is the *Bit Set/Reset Register* used to set or reset individual bits atomically in a single write cycle. - (1 mark) Writing to `ODR` requires a *read-modify-write* sequence, which is non-atomic and vulnerable to interrupt race conditions. - (1 mark) `BSRR` writes a `1` in the lower 16 bits to **set** and a `1` in the upper 16 bits to **reset** the corresponding pin, completing the operation in one bus cycle without any risk of read-modify-write hazards. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern: Internal Choice Provided]** ### Question A (14 Marks) — LED Interfacing and Brightness Control via PWM **(a)** Design a circuit to interface **8 LEDs** to Port B of an ATmega328P microcontroller. Compute the current-limiting resistor for each LED given $V_f = 2.1$ V, $I_f = 12$ mA, $V_{CC} = 5$ V. Also, calculate the total worst-case current sourced by the port and verify whether it exceeds the ATmega328P's absolute maximum per-pin and per-port ratings. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a complete embedded C program to produce a **PWM signal on PB1 (OC1A pin)** with a frequency of **500 Hz** and a duty cycle of **30%** to control LED brightness. Assume the system clock is 16 MHz. Show the relevant Timer/Counter1 register configurations. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1: Compute the series resistor** (1 mark) $$\begin{aligned} R &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_f} \\ &= \frac{5.0 - 2.1}{0.012} \\ &= \frac{2.9}{0.012} \\ &= 241.67 \text{ }\Omega \end{aligned}$$ Nearest E12 standard value: **$R = 270 \text{ }\Omega$** (chosen to be safely above the minimum). **Step 2: Recompute actual current at $R = 270 \text{ }\Omega$** (1 mark) $$I_{actual} = \frac{5.0 - 2.1}{270} = 10.74 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 3: Worst-case port current** (1 mark) $$I_{port,max} = 8 \times 10.74 \text{ mA} = 85.96 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 4: Compare to ATmega328P absolute maximums** (1 mark) | Parameter | ATmega328P Absolute Max | Our Design | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | DC current per I/O pin | 40 mA | 10.74 mA | Safe | | DC current per port (8 pins) | 100 mA (recommended 70 mA) | 85.96 mA | Borderline | **Step 5: Mitigation** (1 mark) To stay safely below the recommended 70 mA, the design should either: (i) limit simultaneous ON LEDs to **5** ($5 \times 10.74 = 53.7$ mA), or (ii) increase $R$ to **$470 \text{ }\Omega$** reducing per-LED current to ~6 mA. **Step 6: Drawing & DDR configuration** (2 marks) - `DDRB = 0xFF;` to configure all 8 pins of Port B as outputs. - Each LED cathode connects to the GPIO pin; anode connects to $V_{CC}$ via the resistor (sinking configuration). #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1: Determine the Timer1 prescaler and TOP value** (2 marks) We use **Fast PWM mode (WGM = 14)** with ICR1 as TOP: $$f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot (1 + TOP)}$$ Choosing a prescaler $N = 8$: $$TOP = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot f_{PWM}} - 1 = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{8 \times 500} - 1 = 3999$$ **Step 2: Compute OCR1A for 30% duty cycle** (1 mark) $$OCR1A = 0.30 \times (TOP + 1) - 1 = 0.30 \times 4000 - 1 = 1199$$ **Step 3: Register configuration** (2 marks) ```c TCCR1A = (1 << COM1A1) | (1 << WGM11); // Clear OC1A on match TCCR1B = (1 << WGM13) | (1 << WGM12) | (1 << CS11); // WGM=14, prescaler=8 ICR1 = 3999; OCR1A = 1199; DDRB |= (1 << PB1); // Enable OC1A output ``` **Step 4: Working explanation** (2 marks) - Timer1 counts from 0 to 3999 repeatedly. - When the counter equals 1199, OC1A is **driven LOW** (LED brighter because more time above threshold in a *non-inverting* variant; here we are in *inverting* mode via COM1A1:0 = 10 for total 30% effective brightness if interpreted as time-ON). - At TOP (3999), OC1A is **driven HIGH** again, repeating the cycle at 500 Hz. - The LED appears dimmer than full ON because the average voltage is $0.30 \times 5 = 1.5$ V effective for 30% duty cycle. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Bitwise Operations and Software Delay Calibration **(a)** Write a complete embedded C program for the **8051 microcontroller** to generate the pattern **1-0-0-1-0-0-1-0** on Port P2.0, P2.1, …, P2.7 respectively. Use bitwise operators. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design and analyze a **calibrated software delay routine** to produce exactly **100 ms** delay on a 8051 running at **12 MHz**. Show the calculation of inner and outer loop counts assuming 8 machine cycles per inner iteration. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1: Encode the pattern** (1 mark) The pattern `1-0-0-1-0-0-1-0` corresponds to the binary value: $$P2 = 0b10010010 = 0x92$$ **Step 2: Complete program** (4 marks) ```c #include void delay(void); void main(void) { P2 = 0x00; // Initialize all OFF while (1) { P2 = (P2 & 0x00) | 0x92; // Force pattern using OR with cleared bits delay(); P2 = 0x00; // All OFF delay(); } } void delay(void) { unsigned int i, j; for (i = 0; i < 200; i++) for (j = 0; j < 250; j++); } ``` **Step 3: Bitwise explanation** (2 marks) - `P2 & 0x00` clears all bits (bitwise AND with zero). - `| 0x92` sets bits 7, 4, and 1 using bitwise OR. - This demonstrates *read-modify-write* on the SFR. For atomicity in critical sections, one could use `sbit` declarations. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1: Machine cycle time** (1 mark) $$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$$ **Step 2: Total inner iterations needed** (1 mark) $$N_{total} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC} \cdot 8} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8} = 12,500$$ **Step 3: Nested loop decomposition** (2 marks) Choose $i = 125$, $j = 100$ such that $125 \times 100 = 12,500$ iterations. **Step 4: Final program** (2 marks) ```c void delay_100ms(void) { unsigned char i, j; for (i = 0; i < 125; i++) for (j = 0; j < 100; j++); } ``` **Step 5: Caveat** (1 mark) The actual delay is approximate because the loop overhead includes increment, compare, and branch instructions totalling **8 cycles per inner iteration**. For high-precision timing, use **hardware timers** (Timer0/1) instead of polled delays. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the clock enable**: On ARM Cortex-M parts, students often write to `GPIOA->MODER` without first enabling the clock in `RCC->AHB1ENR`. The pin will not respond. **Loss: up to 2 marks.** > 2. **Wrong MODER encoding**: MODER uses *two bits per pin*. Writing `(1 << n)` is wrong; the correct form is `(1 << (n*2))` with the pair being `01` for output. > 3. **Ignoring Vf**: Many students compute $R = \frac{V_{CC}}{I_f}$ without subtracting $V_f$, yielding an unrealistically low current. > 4. **Using blocking delays in ISR**: Calling `delay_ms(500)` inside an Interrupt Service Routine blocks all higher-priority interrupts — a major design flaw. > 5. **Sourcing vs sinking confusion**: An LED can be connected with anode to GPIO (sourcing) or cathode to GPIO (sinking). The logic level to turn it ON flips between the two configurations. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **LED is polarity-sensitive**: connect anode to the higher potential side through the resistor. - **Always use $R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_f}$** to compute the current-limiting resistor; never use $R = \frac{V_{CC}}{I_f}$ alone. - **GPIO configuration is two-step on ARM**: (1) enable peripheral clock in RCC, (2) set MODER to `01` for output. - **Bitwise operators** (`|`, `&`, `^`, `~`, `<<`) are the bread-and-butter of embedded C — memorize their truth tables. - **BSRR is atomic, ODR is not** — prefer BSRR in ISRs and concurrent contexts. - **Software delays are blocking and CPU-wasteful** — use hardware timers or SysTick for production code. - **Sourcing vs sinking**: verify the current direction your GPIO can safely source/sink per pin and per port. - **Pattern generation** on parallel ports is the basis of multiplexed displays and Charlieplexing. - **E-series resistor standards**: prefer E12 (10% tolerance) for hobby projects, E24 (5%) for precision. - **Total port current** must remain within the MCU's absolute maximum ratings (often 100 – 200 mA total per port). - **JTAG/SWD pins** lose their GPIO function when debug is active — reclaim them only after disabling debug. - **Open-drain outputs** require an external pull-up to drive an LED; verify the GPIO mode before connecting. - **PWM frequency** must be > 100 Hz to avoid visible flicker in LEDs (typical choice: 500 Hz – 1 kHz). - **Duty cycle** controls perceived brightness linearly only over a limited range due to human eye logarithmic response.

topics.title: Switch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBBRT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Switch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **microcontrollers and embedded systems**, a **Switch** is defined as an *electromechanical or solid-state binary input device* that mechanically or electronically interrupts the flow of current in a circuit, thereby providing a **HIGH (Logic 1)** or **LOW (Logic 0)** digital signal to a microcontroller's General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pin. The switch acts as the primary **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** component, allowing a user to inject discrete, deterministic control commands into an embedded system.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, switches are classified under **digital input interfacing devices**, and the term encompasses momentary push-buttons, toggle switches, slide switches, membrane keypads, DIP switches, and rotary encoders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Switches form the most fundamental class of **digital input transducers**. The ability to reliably read, debounce, and interpret a switch's state is a **CO4 (Apply)** level outcome expected of every B.Tech student.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a switch as a **door for electrons**. Imagine a water pipeline carrying water (current) from a reservoir (voltage source) to a town (microcontroller pin).

- **Open Switch = Closed Door:** No water (current) flows. The town sees an empty pipe.
- **Closed Switch = Open Door:** Water gushes through. The town receives the supply.

However, here is the catch — in a microcontroller, we don't just want *flow*; we want a **logical decision**. So we add a small device called a **pull-up** or **pull-down resistor** to make sure that when the door is closed, the controller unambiguously reads "1" or "0" (without this, the input pin would be in a **floating** state — like a feather in the wind, susceptible to random electrical noise).

## 1.3 Primary Classification of Switches

| S.No | Switch Type | Actuation | Bounce Behavior | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SPST (Single Pole Single Throw)** | ON / OFF | Mild | Power toggles |
| 2 | **SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw)** | Selects between 2 lines | Mild | Mode selection |
| 3 | **Momentary Push-Button** | Spring-return | **Severe** | Reset, User input |
| 4 | **DIP Switch** | Manual slider | None | Hardware configuration |
| 5 | **Toggle Switch** | Lever actuated | Mild | Power / Mode select |
| 6 | **Rotary Encoder** | Continuous rotation | Mechanical chatter | Volume, Menu navigation |
| 7 | **Membrane Keypad** | Matrix of buttons | **Severe** | Calculators, Appliances |
| 8 | **Reed Switch** | Magnetic field | None | Proximity sensing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (IEEE / IEC):**
> - **Contact Bounce Duration (metallic contacts):** Typically **1 ms to 10 ms**.
> - **Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V** or **5 V** (TTL/CMOS logic compatible).
> - **Contact Resistance (closed):** **< 50 mΩ** (ideal).
> - **Insulation Resistance (open):** **> 100 MΩ** (ideal).
> - **Mechanical Life Cycle:** **10,000 to 100,000 operations**.

## 1.4 Switch Symbols in Circuit Schematics

```
    SPST (Push-Button)         SPDT (Selector)         SPST (Toggle)
         ┌─┤ ├─┐                   ┌─┤ ├────┐              ╱
         │      │                   │  │     │             ╱
        ─┴──────┴─                 ─┴──┴──┬──┴─           ─────
                                       │   │
                                      ─┴───┴─
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrical Equivalent Model of a Mechanical Switch

A real-world mechanical switch is **never ideal**. It is modeled as a series combination of:

1. **Contact Resistance ($R_c$):** A small, non-linear resistance when closed.
2. **Parasitic Capacitance ($C_p$):** Capacitance between the contacts when open.
3. **Bounce Generator:** A stochastic time-domain noise source that emulates the multiple mechanical make/break events.

The **Thevenin equivalent** at the GPIO pin becomes a voltage divider between the pull-up/pull-down resistor and the switch contact resistance.

## 2.2 Pull-Up vs. Pull-Down Configurations — Structured Logic

### 2.2.1 Pull-Down Resistor Configuration

- The resistor $R_{PD}$ is connected between the **GPIO pin** and **GND (0 V)**.
- The switch is connected between the **GPIO pin** and **VCC** (+V).
- **Switch OPEN:** Pin is pulled to ground through $R_{PD}$ → Logic **0**.
- **Switch CLOSED:** Pin is shorted to VCC → Logic **1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Current Limiting Principle:** When the switch is closed, current flows from VCC through the switch to GND. The current must be limited by Ohm's Law to a safe value (typically 1–10 mA for indicator circuits, or just a few hundred micro-amps for direct GPIO drive).

### 2.2.2 Pull-Up Resistor Configuration

- The resistor $R_{PU}$ is connected between the **GPIO pin** and **VCC (+V)**.
- The switch is connected between the **GPIO pin** and **GND (0 V)**.
- **Switch OPEN:** Pin is pulled up to VCC through $R_{PU}$ → Logic **1**.
- **Switch CLOSED:** Pin is shorted to GND → Logic **0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Most modern microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC) have **internal pull-up resistors** (typically 20 kΩ to 50 kΩ) that can be software-enabled, eliminating the need for an external resistor in most simple applications.

## 2.3 The Critical Problem: Switch Bounce

When a mechanical switch is actuated, the metal contacts do **not** transition cleanly from OPEN to CLOSED. Due to:

- Mechanical inertia of the contacts
- Surface micro-irregularities
- Spring elasticity

…the contacts **oscillate** (bounce) for a few milliseconds, producing a noisy, multi-pulse waveform instead of a clean edge.

### 2.3.1 Debouncing Strategies

| Strategy | Method | Cost | Reliability | Latency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RC Low-Pass Filter** | Hardware (R + C) | Low | High | Analog dependent |
| **Schmitt Trigger** | Hardware (74HC14) | Low | High | < 1 µs |
| **Software Delay** | Wait 10–50 ms | Free | Medium | 10–50 ms |
| **Counter-Based** | Count stable samples | Free | High | Configurable |
| **State Machine** | FSM tracking edges | Free | Very High | None |
| **Flip-Flop Latch** | SR flip-flop hardware | Medium | Very High | < 1 µs |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Description | Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_{PD}}{R_{PD} + R_c}$ | Voltage at GPIO when switch closed (Pull-down) | Volts (V) | $R_c \ll R_{PD}$ required |
| 2 | $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_{PU}}{R_{PU} + R_{sw}}$ | Voltage at GPIO when switch open (Pull-up) | Volts (V) | $R_{sw}$ is switch resistance |
| 3 | $I_{switch} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_{PU}}$ | Current through closed switch (Pull-up) | Amperes (A) | Must be < 20 mA typical |
| 4 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | RC time constant | Seconds (s) | Debounce filter time constant |
| 5 | $t_{debounce} \approx 5\tau = 5RC$ | Effective debounce delay (settling to 99.3%) | Seconds (s) | Standard 5τ rule |
| 6 | $f_{bounce,max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot t_{bounce}}$ | Maximum bounce frequency | Hertz (Hz) | $t_{bounce}$ ≈ 1–10 ms |
| 7 | $V_{IH} \ge 0.7 \cdot V_{CC}$ | CMOS Logic HIGH input threshold | Volts (V) | For 5 V → 3.5 V min |
| 8 | $V_{IL} \le 0.3 \cdot V_{CC}$ | CMOS Logic LOW input threshold | Volts (V) | For 5 V → 1.5 V max |
| 9 | $P_{dissipated} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{R_{PU}}$ | Power loss in pull-up resistor | Watts (W) | Select $R_{PU} \ge 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| 10 | $N_{states} = 2^k$ | Number of unique inputs from k switches | States | For matrix keypad: $N = \text{rows} \times \text{cols}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
